merge joins on common columns while concat stacks along an axis direction
merge only works with numeric data while concat handles all data types
explanation: merge() performs database-style joins on common columns, while concat() stacks DataFrames along a specified axis.

text: For a custom scikit-learn transformer, which methods must be implemented for pipeline compatibility?
options: Only the predict method is required since transformers always make predictions
The score and evaluate methods are the only ones required for all pipelines
Only the transform method needs to be implemented for pipeline compatibility
The fit and transform methods must both be implemented at minimum for use
explanation: Custom transformers must implement fit() and transform() methods to be compatible with scikit-learn pipelines.

text: What is the advantage of multiprocessing over threading for CPU-bound ML tasks in Python?
options: Multiprocessing creates separate processes bypassing GIL for true parallelism
Threading bypasses the GIL while multiprocessing is limited by the GIL lock
Threading uses less memory because it creates separate isolated memory spaces
Multiprocessing is always slower but provides much better error handling now
explanation: Python's GIL prevents true parallel thread execution for CPU-bound tasks. Multiprocessing creates separate processes enabling true parallelism.

text: Why should you use np.allclose() instead of == when comparing floating-point arrays?
options: The allclose function is significantly faster than equality operator for arrays
The equality operator cannot be applied to any NumPy arrays at all ever
Floating-point arithmetic introduces rounding errors making exact comparison fail
The equality operator only works for integer arrays and raises errors on floats
explanation: Floating-point arithmetic introduces tiny rounding errors, making exact equality checks unreliable. allclose() checks within a tolerance.

text: Why is it critical to apply fit() only on training data for preprocessing in production ML?
options: Fitting on test data would make preprocessing steps run much more slowly
Fitting on all data causes data leakage by incorporating test set statistics
Fitting on training data alone ensures the model always achieves higher loss
Fitting on test data would automatically delete the test set from memory
explanation: Fitting on test data causes data leakage - the model indirectly learns test set characteristics, inflating performance estimates.

text: What problem does functools.lru_cache solve in feature engineering pipelines?
options: It logs all function calls to a database for auditing and tracking uses
It converts all function outputs to lower precision to save system memory
It automatically distributes computation across multiple GPU device units
It caches expensive function results to avoid redundant recomputation work
explanation: lru_cache memoizes function results, storing them so repeated calls with the same arguments return instantly without recomputation.

text: When handling very large datasets in pandas, what approach reduces memory most effectively?
options: Specifying optimal dtypes and reading data in chunks with iteration
Removing all column names to reduce the metadata overhead in RAM
Sorting the DataFrame by index before performing any analysis steps
Converting all columns to string type to ensure uniform representation
explanation: Specifying smaller dtypes (float32 vs float64, category for strings) and processing in chunks significantly reduces memory usage.

text: What is the purpose of __getitem__ and __len__ in a PyTorch custom Dataset class?
options: They define how to save and load model checkpoints to and from disk
They specify loss function and optimizer used during backpropagation
They enable indexed data access and size reporting for the DataLoader
They control the learning rate schedule during model training loop runs
explanation: __getitem__ enables indexed access and __len__ returns dataset size, both required by PyTorch's DataLoader for batching.

text: Why is np.einsum() considered more flexible than standard NumPy operations for tensors?
options: It runs exclusively on GPU hardware while standard operations use CPU only
It expresses complex multi-dimensional operations in compact Einstein notation
It automatically converts all inputs to higher precision floating point types
It only works with two-dimensional arrays making the interface much simpler
explanation: np.einsum() uses Einstein summation notation to express complex tensor operations in a single compact expression.

text: What design pattern does scikit-learn's Pipeline class implement and why is it beneficial?
options: The observer pattern notifying all components when training data changes
The composite pattern chaining steps to prevent data leakage in CV folds
The singleton pattern ensuring only one model instance exists in memory
The factory pattern creating new model instances based on input data type
explanation: Pipeline implements a composite pattern chaining preprocessing and estimation, preventing data leakage during cross-validation.

text: How does contextlib.contextmanager help manage resources in data processing?
options: It provides automatic type checking for all function arguments runtime
It creates permanent global variables accessible from any module at all
It enables resource management using generator-based context managers
It automatically converts synchronous code into asynchronous coroutines
explanation: contextlib.contextmanager allows writing context managers using generator functions with yield for automatic resource cleanup.

text: What is data cleaning in the context of data preprocessing?
options: Compressing data for storage
Adding noise to the data
Encrypting data for security
Fixing errors and removing inconsistencies
explanation: Data cleaning involves identifying and correcting errors, handling missing values, and removing inconsistencies in datasets.

text: Which technique fills missing values with the column average?
options: Normalization step
Mode imputation
Deletion method
Mean imputation
explanation: Mean imputation replaces missing values with the arithmetic mean of the available values in that column.

text: What does one-hot encoding convert?
options: Categories into binary vectors
Numbers into text labels
Images into pixel arrays
Audio into text transcripts
explanation: One-hot encoding converts categorical variables into binary vectors where each category gets its own binary column.

text: What is the purpose of removing duplicate records from a dataset?
options: To add more noise and improve model robustness
To reduce the number of features in the dataset
To avoid bias from repeated identical observations
To increase the total number of training samples
explanation: Duplicates can bias the model by overrepresenting certain observations and should be removed for accurate analysis.

text: Which method scales features to a range between zero and one?
options: Standardization
Log transformation
Min-max normalization
Binning technique
explanation: Min-max normalization scales each feature to a fixed range, typically 0 to 1, using the minimum and maximum values.

text: What is a common file format for storing tabular datasets?
options: CSV
PNG
MP3
EXE
explanation: CSV (Comma-Separated Values) is one of the most common file formats for storing and exchanging tabular data.

text: What does the term 'missing data' refer to in a dataset?
options: Data stored in a different file location
Values that are absent for some observations
Values that are larger than the average value
Data that has been encrypted for privacy
explanation: Missing data refers to absent values in a dataset, often represented as NaN, NULL, or blank entries in data files.

text: What is the first step typically performed in any data preprocessing pipeline?
options: Data collection
Hyperparameter tuning
Model training
Feature selection
explanation: Data collection is typically the first step, gathering raw data from various sources before any processing can begin.

text: Which type of data contains categories like color or country name?
options: Numerical data
Sequential data
Continuous data
Categorical data
explanation: Categorical data represents groups or categories, such as colors, country names, or product types.

text: What does the term 'outlier' refer to in a dataset?
options: A value significantly different from others
The average value of all observations
A missing entry in a data column
The most common value in the data
explanation: An outlier is a data point that differs significantly from other observations, potentially indicating errors or rare events.

text: What is the difference between normalization and standardization?
options: Normalization works only with text while standardization works only with numeric data
Normalization removes outliers while standardization only handles missing values in data
Normalization scales to 0-1 range while standardization centers with zero mean unit variance
Normalization increases feature count while standardization decreases feature count total
explanation: Normalization scales features to [0,1] using min-max, while standardization transforms to zero mean and unit variance using z-scores.

text: Why might you use stratified sampling instead of simple random sampling?
options: It guarantees that the sample will be larger than simple random samples
It preserves the proportion of each class in the sample matching population
Stratified sampling is always faster than simple random sampling methods
Stratified sampling eliminates the need for any data cleaning steps after
explanation: Stratified sampling ensures each class is proportionally represented, important for imbalanced datasets where random sampling may miss minority classes.

text: What is the purpose of applying a log transformation to skewed data?
options: It converts categorical variables into numerical features for models
It reduces skewness making the distribution closer to normal shape
It removes all outliers automatically from the dataset completely
It increases the total number of samples available for training
explanation: Log transformation reduces right skewness by compressing large values and expanding small values, making distributions more normal.

text: When should you use label encoding versus one-hot encoding for categories?
options: Label encoding is used for numerical features while one-hot is for text only
One-hot encoding should be used only when the dataset has fewer than ten rows
Label encoding for ordinal categories and one-hot encoding for nominal ones
Label encoding is always superior to one-hot encoding in every scenario
explanation: Label encoding preserves order for ordinal categories, while one-hot encoding prevents false ordinal relationships in nominal categories.

text: What problem does the SMOTE technique address in preprocessing?
options: It handles missing values by predicting them using regression models
It removes highly correlated features from the dataset efficiently
It standardizes all features to have the same scale and distribution
It generates synthetic minority class samples to balance class distribution
explanation: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) creates synthetic samples for the minority class to address class imbalance.

text: What is the purpose of binning continuous variables into discrete groups?
options: To increase the total number of features in the dataset significantly
To convert numerical data into completely random categorical labels
To remove all missing values from the continuous variable columns
To reduce noise and capture non-linear patterns in the data values
explanation: Binning groups continuous values into discrete intervals, reducing noise and helping capture non-linear relationships in the data.

text: How does the Interquartile Range method detect outliers in a dataset?
options: Values above the mean are always considered outliers by the IQR method
Only the single maximum and minimum values are considered outliers here
Values outside 1.5 times the IQR from Q1 and Q3 are flagged as outliers
Values within one standard deviation of median are flagged as outlier
explanation: The IQR method flags values below Q1 - 1.5*IQR or above Q3 + 1.5*IQR as outliers, providing a robust detection approach.

text: What is data augmentation and when is it commonly used?
options: Encrypting sensitive data fields before storing them in the database
Removing features to reduce dimensionality in high-dimensional datasets
Converting unstructured data into structured tabular format for use
Creating modified copies of existing data to increase training set size
explanation: Data augmentation creates modified copies of data (rotations, flips, noise) to increase training size, commonly used in computer vision.

text: Why is it important to handle missing values before training a model?
options: Missing values always indicate that the entire dataset is unreliable here
Missing values only affect visualization and have no impact on models
Most ML algorithms cannot process datasets that contain any missing values
Handling missing values is optional since models automatically ignore them
explanation: Most ML algorithms cannot handle missing values directly and will either error or produce biased results if they are not addressed.

text: What is feature scaling and why is it important for distance-based algorithms?
options: It converts all features to categorical type for better model interpretability
It duplicates important features to give them more weight during training
It ensures features contribute equally so larger-scale features do not dominate
It removes features with low variance to reduce the dimensionality of data
explanation: Feature scaling ensures features contribute equally to distance calculations, preventing features with larger scales from dominating algorithms like KNN.

text: A dataset has 40% missing values in a critical feature. Which imputation strategy is most appropriate?
options: Ignore the feature entirely and remove it from the training dataset now
Simply delete all rows with missing values to ensure data quality overall
Replace all missing values with zero since it is the simplest approach
Use a predictive model like KNN or regression to impute the missing values
explanation: With 40% missing values, deletion would lose too much data. Predictive imputation using KNN or regression preserves patterns better than simple statistics.

text: Why can applying preprocessing transformations before splitting data cause data leakage?
options: Preprocessing transformations always modify the target variable directly
Statistics from test data influence the transformation applied to training data
Transformations always remove the most important features from the dataset
Preprocessing makes the model run slower during the inference stage overall
explanation: If you fit preprocessing on the full dataset before splitting, test set statistics leak into training transformations, causing overly optimistic evaluation.

text: When dealing with high-cardinality categorical features, why might target encoding outperform one-hot?
options: One-hot encoding creates massive sparse matrices while target encoding is compact
Target encoding always produces more accurate results regardless of the data
Target encoding removes the need for any validation set during training time
One-hot encoding automatically introduces data leakage into training process
explanation: High-cardinality one-hot encoding creates many sparse columns increasing dimensionality. Target encoding replaces categories with their target mean, keeping dimensionality low.

text: In time-series data, why must preprocessing respect temporal order during train-test splitting?
options: Temporal order only matters for visualization and does not affect model results
Time-series data must always be converted to images before model training steps
Random splitting can leak future information into training causing unrealistic scores
Time-series data always has fewer missing values than cross-sectional datasets
explanation: Random splitting of time-series allows future data points into training, creating data leakage. Temporal splits ensure the model only learns from past data.

text: A feature has extreme outliers that cannot be removed because they represent valid rare events. What approach is best?
options: Use mean imputation to replace all outlier values with the column average
Apply min-max scaling which will properly handle extreme outlier values well
Apply robust scaling using median and IQR which is resistant to outlier values
Delete the entire feature since it contains any outlier values in the data
explanation: Robust scaling uses median and IQR instead of mean and standard deviation, making it resistant to outliers while preserving valid extreme values.

text: Why is the Yeo-Johnson transformation preferred over Box-Cox for some datasets?
options: Yeo-Johnson is always faster computationally than Box-Cox transformation
Yeo-Johnson only works with categorical features unlike Box-Cox method
Yeo-Johnson can handle both positive and negative values unlike Box-Cox
Yeo-Johnson produces exactly normal distributions while Box-Cox does not
explanation: Box-Cox requires strictly positive data, while Yeo-Johnson handles zero and negative values, making it more versatile for diverse datasets.

text: When preprocessing text data for NLP, why might subword tokenization outperform word-level tokenization?
options: Subword tokenization removes the need for any stopword removal preprocessing
It handles out-of-vocabulary words by breaking them into known subword units
It converts all text into numerical features without any vocabulary mapping
Subword tokenization always produces shorter sequences than word tokenization
explanation: Subword tokenization breaks unknown words into known subword pieces, handling OOV words gracefully while maintaining a manageable vocabulary size.

text: A dataset has features measured in different units with varying scales and distributions. Which preprocessing combination is most robust?
options: Apply no preprocessing since modern algorithms handle raw data effectively
Apply only min-max scaling to all features uniformly without further analysis
Use power transformation followed by standardization for each feature column
Use one-hot encoding on all features regardless of their data type values
explanation: Power transformation (like Yeo-Johnson) normalizes distributions first, then standardization ensures zero mean and unit variance, handling diverse scales robustly.

text: Why should you be cautious when using mean imputation for features with non-normal distributions?
options: It artificially reduces variance and can distort the true distribution shape
Mean imputation only works with categorical features not numerical ones
Mean imputation always increases the standard deviation of the feature values
It automatically removes all outliers before computing the replacement value
explanation: Mean imputation reduces variance by pulling values toward the center and can distort skewed distributions, potentially biasing subsequent analyses.

text: In a pipeline processing streaming data, why is incremental preprocessing preferred over batch preprocessing?
options: It automatically handles all missing values without any configuration needed
It processes data point by point without needing the entire dataset in memory
Incremental preprocessing eliminates the need for any feature engineering step
Incremental preprocessing always produces more accurate transformations overall
explanation: Incremental preprocessing processes data sequentially without requiring the full dataset in memory, essential for streaming scenarios with limited resources.

text: What does EDA stand for in data science?
options: Extended Data Algorithm
Extra Data Analysis
External Data Approach
Exploratory Data Analysis
explanation: EDA stands for Exploratory Data Analysis, the process of examining data to discover patterns and anomalies.

text: Which type of chart is best for showing the distribution of a single variable?
options: Pie chart
Line chart
Histogram
Gantt chart
explanation: A histogram displays the frequency distribution of a single numerical variable by grouping values into bins.

text: What does a scatter plot visualize?
options: The hierarchical structure of a dataset
The proportion of categories in a dataset
The trend of a variable over time periods
The relationship between two numerical variables
explanation: A scatter plot displays the relationship between two numerical variables, with each point representing an observation.

text: What is the median of a sorted dataset?
options: The middle value in order
The range of the data
The arithmetic average
The most frequent value
explanation: The median is the middle value in a sorted dataset, dividing the data into two equal halves.

text: Which plot type shows the five-number summary of a dataset?
options: Bar chart
Area chart
Box plot
Scatter plot
explanation: A box plot displays the five-number summary: minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum, along with outliers.

text: What does a correlation coefficient of +1 indicate?
options: No relationship at all
Perfect positive linear
Random relationship
Perfect negative linear
explanation: A correlation coefficient of +1 indicates a perfect positive linear relationship between two variables.

text: Which visualization is best for comparing quantities across categories?
options: Line chart
Bar chart
Heat map
Histogram
explanation: Bar charts are ideal for comparing quantities across different categories with rectangular bars of varying heights.

text: What is the mode of a dataset?
options: The most frequent value
The largest value
The average value
The middle value
explanation: The mode is the most frequently occurring value in a dataset.

text: What does a line chart typically display?
options: Trends over time
Data distribution
Category proportions
Feature correlations
explanation: Line charts display trends and changes in data over time, connecting sequential data points with lines.

text: What is the purpose of a heatmap in data analysis?
options: To compress images into smaller sizes
To display magnitude of values using colors
To show geographic locations on a map
To play audio data as visual patterns
explanation: A heatmap uses colors to represent the magnitude of values in a matrix, commonly used for correlation matrices.

text: What does a right-skewed distribution indicate about the data?
options: Most values are clustered on the left with a long tail extending right
Most values are clustered on the right with a long tail extending left
The distribution has exactly two peaks at equal distances from center
Values are perfectly symmetrically distributed around the central mean
explanation: A right-skewed (positively skewed) distribution has most values on the left with a long tail to the right, indicating few large values.

text: How does the Pearson correlation differ from the Spearman correlation?
options: Pearson requires sorted data while Spearman works with unsorted data only
Pearson only works with categorical data while Spearman works with numbers
Pearson measures linear relationships while Spearman measures monotonic ones
Pearson is always larger than Spearman for any given pair of variables
explanation: Pearson measures linear relationships between continuous variables, while Spearman measures monotonic relationships using ranked values.

text: What is a pair plot used for in exploratory data analysis?
options: Visualizing the decision boundary of a trained classification model
Displaying all pairwise feature relationships and distributions simultaneously
Comparing model performance metrics across different algorithm choices
Showing only the relationship between the target variable and one feature
explanation: A pair plot shows scatter plots for all pairs of features and histograms on the diagonal, giving a comprehensive view of feature relationships.

text: Why is it important to check for multicollinearity during EDA?
options: Checking for multicollinearity is optional and never affects final results
Multicollinearity only affects visualization and has no impact on any model
Multicollinearity always improves model accuracy and prediction performance
Highly correlated features can cause instability in regression model coefficients
explanation: Multicollinearity causes unstable regression coefficients and inflated standard errors, making it difficult to determine individual feature importance.

text: What information does a Variance Inflation Factor provide?
options: The optimal number of clusters for a K-means clustering algorithm
The degree to which a feature is explained by other features in model
The total number of missing values in each feature of the dataset
The probability that a feature follows a normal distribution pattern
explanation: VIF measures how much a feature's variance is inflated by correlation with other features, with high VIF indicating multicollinearity.

text: What is the purpose of a QQ plot in statistical analysis?
options: To compare model predictions against actual observed target values
To display the learning curve of a model during training process
To visualize the decision boundary of a classification model used
To assess whether data follows a specific theoretical distribution
explanation: A QQ (quantile-quantile) plot compares data quantiles against theoretical distribution quantiles to assess distributional assumptions.

text: How do violin plots improve upon standard box plots?
options: They only display the mean and are simpler to interpret than box plots
They show the actual probability density distribution shape of the data
They remove all outlier information to simplify the visualization look
They work only with categorical data unlike box plots which need numbers
explanation: Violin plots combine box plot information with kernel density estimation, showing the full distribution shape instead of just summary statistics.

text: What does the kurtosis of a distribution describe?
options: The asymmetry of the distribution around its mean value point
The tailedness and peakedness of the distribution compared to normal
The number of distinct modes or peaks present in the distribution
The total range between the minimum and maximum values observed
explanation: Kurtosis measures the heaviness of distribution tails and the sharpness of the peak relative to a normal distribution.

text: Why is it important to visualize data before applying machine learning algorithms?
options: Visualization is only for presentation and does not affect modeling choices
It is a mandatory step that all ML frameworks enforce before model training
Visualization automatically selects the best algorithm for the given dataset
It reveals patterns, outliers, and distributions that guide preprocessing decisions
explanation: Visualization reveals data characteristics like distributions, outliers, correlations, and patterns that inform preprocessing and algorithm selection decisions.

text: What does a correlation matrix heatmap help identify?
options: The exact prediction accuracy of a trained machine learning model
Pairs of features with strong positive or negative linear relationships
The best train-test split ratio for the given dataset size overall
The optimal hyperparameters for a gradient boosting model training
explanation: A correlation matrix heatmap visually identifies pairs of features with strong correlations, helping detect multicollinearity and redundant features.

text: A dataset shows a Pearson correlation of 0.02 between two features, but the scatter plot reveals a clear U-shaped pattern. What explains this?
options: The data has too many missing values to compute correlation correctly
The scatter plot is incorrectly rendered due to a visualization error
A correlation of 0.02 actually indicates a very strong relationship here
Pearson only captures linear relationships and misses non-linear patterns
explanation: Pearson correlation only measures linear relationships. A U-shaped pattern is non-linear, so Pearson reports near-zero despite a strong association.

text: When exploring a dataset with 500 features, which dimensionality reduction technique is most useful for initial EDA visualization?
options: Removing all features except the first two columns from the original data
Manually selecting 2 features based on column name alphabetical ordering
Using t-SNE or UMAP to project high-dimensional data into 2D for plotting
Creating 500 individual histograms and reviewing each one sequentially
explanation: t-SNE and UMAP project high-dimensional data into 2D while preserving local structure, enabling visual exploration of clusters and patterns.

text: During EDA you discover that a feature has a bimodal distribution. What might this suggest about the underlying data?
options: The data may contain two distinct subpopulations mixed into one feature
Bimodal distributions always indicate that the feature is perfectly normal
The feature should be removed because bimodal distributions are always errors
The feature has too many outliers and needs to be replaced with its mean
explanation: A bimodal distribution often suggests two distinct subpopulations in the data, potentially indicating meaningful subgroups worth investigating.

text: Why might the mean be a misleading measure of central tendency for income data?
options: Income data always follows a perfectly normal distribution in all nations
The mean cannot be computed for continuous variables like income amounts
Income data is always categorical so the mean has no mathematical meaning
Income data is typically right-skewed so the mean is pulled by high earners
explanation: Income distributions are typically right-skewed with a long tail of high earners, pulling the mean above the median, making median more representative.

text: How does Simpson's Paradox affect conclusions drawn during exploratory data analysis?
options: It only occurs in datasets with fewer than one hundred total observations
A trend in subgroups can reverse when groups are combined into aggregate
It guarantees that adding more features will always improve model accuracy
It makes all statistical tests invalid regardless of sample size used here
explanation: Simpson's Paradox occurs when a trend in subgroups reverses in aggregated data, often due to a confounding variable that must be accounted for.

text: A feature shows near-zero variance in the training set. Why should this be investigated before modeling?
options: They carry almost no discriminative information and may cause instability
Near-zero variance features provide the strongest predictive signal always
Near-zero variance means the feature is perfectly normally distributed
They always indicate data collection errors that must be manually fixed
explanation: Near-zero variance features carry little discriminative information and can cause numerical instability or overfit to noise in some algorithms.

text: You observe that two features have a Spearman correlation of 0.95. What preprocessing step might you consider?
options: Multiply the two features together to create a more powerful interaction term
Remove one of the redundant features or use PCA to reduce the collinearity
Convert both features to categorical variables to eliminate the correlation
Add both features to the model since high correlation always helps performance
explanation: Highly correlated features are redundant. Removing one or using PCA reduces multicollinearity and dimensionality without significant information loss.

text: During EDA of a classification dataset, you notice heavy class overlap in feature space. What does this suggest?
options: The classification task is inherently difficult and may need complex models
The dataset is perfect for a simple linear classifier without any tuning
The features should all be removed and replaced with randomly generated ones
Class overlap always means the labels are incorrect and need relabeling
explanation: Heavy class overlap means classes are not easily separable, suggesting the need for more complex models, better features, or acceptance of limited accuracy.

text: Why is it important to analyze the relationship between features and the target variable separately for different subgroups?
options: Analyzing subgroups always produces the same results as aggregate analysis does
Different subgroups may exhibit different patterns that aggregate analysis hides
Subgroup analysis is never useful and only wastes computational resources
Subgroup analysis is only applicable to time-series data and no other types
explanation: Different subgroups may have different relationships with the target, and aggregate analysis can mask these differences (related to Simpson's Paradox).

text: A dataset contains timestamps spanning five years. Which EDA technique best reveals seasonal patterns and trends?
options: Creating a single histogram of all values regardless of their timestamps
Sorting the data alphabetically by the timestamp column values only
Computing a single mean value across all five years of data points
Time-series decomposition into trend, seasonal, and residual components
explanation: Time-series decomposition separates data into trend, seasonal, and residual components, clearly revealing periodic patterns and long-term trends.

text: What is the goal of a supervised learning algorithm?
options: Generate new data from scratch
Learn a mapping from inputs to outputs
Find hidden patterns without labels
Group similar data points together
explanation: Supervised learning aims to learn a mapping function from input features to output labels using labeled training data.

text: Which of these is a classification algorithm?
options: Principal Component
Linear Regression
K-Means Clustering
Logistic Regression
explanation: Logistic Regression is a classification algorithm that predicts categorical outcomes despite its name containing regression.

text: What does a decision tree use to split data at each node?
options: Feature thresholds
Random selection
Output labels only
Time stamps alone
explanation: Decision trees split data at each node using feature thresholds that best separate the classes or reduce variance.

text: What type of problem predicts a continuous numerical value?
options: Clustering task
Association task
Classification task
Regression task
explanation: Regression predicts continuous numerical values, such as house prices or temperature, unlike classification which predicts categories.

text: Which algorithm finds the best-fit line through data points?
options: Random Forest model
K-Nearest Neighbors
Apriori Algorithm
Linear Regression
explanation: Linear Regression finds the best-fit line by minimizing the sum of squared differences between predicted and actual values.

text: What does KNN stand for in machine learning?
options: K-Null Notation
K-Nearest Neighbors
K-Node Navigation
K-Normal Networks
explanation: KNN stands for K-Nearest Neighbors, a simple algorithm that classifies data points based on the majority class of their K closest neighbors.

text: In binary classification, how many possible output classes exist?
options: Three
Two
One
Four
explanation: Binary classification has exactly two possible output classes, such as spam/not-spam or positive/negative.

text: What is a label in supervised learning?
options: An input feature column
The correct output answer
A training epoch count
A model parameter value
explanation: A label is the correct output or target value associated with each training example in supervised learning.

text: Which algorithm is often used for predicting house prices?
options: Linear Regression model
Naive Bayes classifier
K-Means algorithm
Apriori association rule
explanation: Linear Regression is commonly used for predicting continuous values like house prices based on features like size and location.

text: What does the term 'training' mean in supervised learning?
options: Visualizing data in chart format
Manually coding rules for predictions
Adjusting model parameters using data
Removing features from the dataset
explanation: Training involves adjusting model parameters by feeding labeled data and optimizing to minimize prediction errors.

text: How does regularization help prevent overfitting in linear regression models?
options: It adds a penalty term for large coefficients constraining model complexity
It increases the number of features to capture more complex data patterns
It multiplies all input features by a constant scaling factor for balance
It removes the least important training samples from the dataset entirely
explanation: Regularization adds a penalty for large coefficients (L1 for Lasso, L2 for Ridge), constraining model complexity and reducing overfitting.

text: What is the key assumption of Naive Bayes classification?
options: The decision boundary must be linear in the original feature space always
All features must be continuous numerical values with normal distribution
The dataset must have exactly equal numbers of samples in every class
Features are conditionally independent given the class label of the sample
explanation: Naive Bayes assumes features are conditionally independent given the class, simplifying probability calculations despite rarely being true in practice.

text: How does the C parameter in SVM affect the decision boundary?
options: C determines the number of support vectors regardless of the data layout
Higher C creates a tighter margin penalizing misclassification more strictly
C only affects the kernel type used and has no impact on the margin width
Higher C creates a wider margin allowing more misclassification of points
explanation: A higher C penalizes misclassification more strictly, creating a tighter margin that may overfit, while lower C allows more misclassification for generalization.

text: What advantage does a decision tree have over logistic regression for certain tasks?
options: Decision trees always achieve higher accuracy than logistic regression models
Decision trees can capture non-linear relationships without feature engineering
Decision trees require less training data than logistic regression to converge
Decision trees never overfit regardless of the depth or number of features
explanation: Decision trees naturally capture non-linear relationships and interactions without manual feature engineering, unlike logistic regression which models linear boundaries.

text: What is the difference between hard and soft margin classification in SVM?
options: Hard margin is faster to train while soft margin requires exponentially more time
Hard margin uses linear kernels while soft margin always uses non-linear kernels
Hard margin allows no violations while soft margin permits some misclassification
Hard margin permits misclassification while soft margin requires perfect separation
explanation: Hard margin SVM requires perfect separation with no misclassification, while soft margin allows some violations for better generalization on noisy data.

text: Why might logistic regression outperform complex models on small datasets?
options: Its simplicity and fewer parameters make it less prone to overfitting on small data
Logistic regression always converges in fewer epochs than any other algorithm does
It automatically augments small datasets to increase the number of training samples
Logistic regression ignores the training data and uses only prior knowledge
explanation: With few training samples, complex models overfit easily. Logistic regression's simplicity and fewer parameters lead to better generalization on small data.

text: What does the kernel trick enable SVM to do?
options: Map data to a higher-dimensional space to find non-linear decision boundaries
Reduce training time by eliminating the need for any optimization process
Convert regression problems into classification problems without data changes
Automatically select the best features from the dataset for classification
explanation: The kernel trick maps data into higher-dimensional space where linear separation is possible, enabling SVM to find non-linear decision boundaries.

text: How does K in KNN affect the bias-variance tradeoff of the classifier?
options: Both small and large K always produce identical bias and variance values here
K has no effect on the bias-variance tradeoff of the KNN classifier at all
Small K has low bias and high variance while large K has high bias low variance
Small K has high bias and low variance while large K has low bias high variance
explanation: Small K fits closely to local patterns (low bias, high variance), while large K smooths predictions (high bias, low variance).

text: What is the purpose of the sigmoid function in logistic regression?
options: It normalizes input features to have zero mean and unit variance values
It maps raw output values to probabilities between zero and one range
It determines the optimal learning rate for gradient descent optimization
It selects the most important features for the model automatically here
explanation: The sigmoid function maps any real number to a probability between 0 and 1, enabling logistic regression to output class probabilities.

text: Why is feature importance a useful output of tree-based models?
options: It guarantees that the model will achieve the highest possible accuracy
It automatically removes unimportant features from the training dataset
It converts categorical features into numerical features for processing
It reveals which features contribute most to the model prediction decisions
explanation: Feature importance from tree-based models shows which features contribute most to predictions, aiding interpretability and feature selection.

text: A linear SVM fails to classify a dataset with circular decision boundaries. Which approach best resolves this?
options: Switching to an RBF kernel to capture the non-linear circular boundary
Increasing the regularization parameter C to a very large positive value
Reducing the training set size to remove noise from the boundary area
Adding more linear features computed from the existing feature columns
explanation: An RBF (Radial Basis Function) kernel maps data to infinite-dimensional space where the circular boundary becomes linearly separable.

text: Why does Naive Bayes often perform surprisingly well despite its strong independence assumption?
options: The independence assumption is actually true for nearly all real-world datasets
Classification only needs correct ranking of class probabilities not exact values
Naive Bayes secretly uses feature correlations even though it assumes independence
The algorithm automatically corrects for violated assumptions during training
explanation: For classification, correct relative ordering of posterior probabilities matters more than exact values. Naive Bayes often ranks correctly despite probability estimates being miscalibrated.

text: In gradient boosting, how does each subsequent tree improve the overall model?
options: Each tree duplicates the first tree but with different random weight values
Each tree trains on entirely new data that was not used by any prior tree
Each tree is trained on a random subset of features ignoring previous results
Each tree fits the residual errors of the combined predictions of prior trees
explanation: Gradient boosting trains each subsequent tree on the residual errors (negative gradient of loss) from prior trees, progressively reducing the overall error.

text: When should you prefer Ridge regression over Lasso regression for regularization?
options: When you need exactly half the features to have zero coefficient values only
When you want to perform automatic feature selection by zeroing coefficients
When most features are relevant and you want to shrink all coefficients evenly
When the dataset has categorical features that need one-hot encoding first
explanation: Ridge (L2) shrinks all coefficients evenly without zeroing any, preferred when most features are believed to be relevant. Lasso (L1) performs feature selection.

text: A decision tree achieves perfect training accuracy but poor test accuracy. Which pruning strategy is most effective?
options: Grow the tree to maximum depth then add more features to improve test scores
Increase the minimum samples per leaf to the total training set size value
Remove all leaf nodes and keep only the root node for maximum simplicity
Apply cost-complexity pruning to find the subtree with best cross-validated score
explanation: Cost-complexity pruning (minimal cost-complexity pruning) systematically removes branches and uses cross-validation to find the optimal tree complexity.

text: Why is the choice of distance metric critical for KNN performance on high-dimensional data?
options: In high dimensions most distance metrics lose discriminative power between points
High-dimensional data always has the same distances regardless of metric used
KNN only works with Euclidean distance so the choice is never actually needed
Distance metrics have no effect on KNN in any number of dimensions at all
explanation: In high dimensions, distances concentrate (curse of dimensionality), making most distance metrics lose discriminative power. Manhattan distance often works better than Euclidean.

text: How does Platt scaling calibrate the output probabilities of an SVM classifier?
options: It fits a logistic regression on the SVM decision values to produce probabilities
It trains a separate neural network to convert SVM outputs into probabilities
It replaces the SVM kernel with a probability density estimation function
It applies min-max normalization directly to the raw SVM decision values
explanation: Platt scaling fits a sigmoid (logistic regression) on SVM decision function values using a held-out set, converting them to well-calibrated probabilities.

text: In multi-class classification, what advantage does one-vs-rest have over one-vs-one strategy?
options: Both strategies always produce identical results regardless of the dataset
One-vs-rest trains fewer classifiers making it computationally more efficient
One-vs-one trains fewer classifiers making it computationally more efficient
One-vs-rest always achieves higher accuracy than one-vs-one on all datasets
explanation: One-vs-rest trains K classifiers for K classes, while one-vs-one trains K*(K-1)/2, making one-vs-rest more efficient for large numbers of classes.

text: Why might a well-tuned logistic regression outperform a deep neural network for tabular data?
options: Logistic regression always converges to the global optimum unlike neural networks
Logistic regression can learn non-linear patterns better than neural networks
Deep neural networks cannot process numerical features in tabular format data
Deep networks need massive data and tabular datasets are often small with noise
explanation: On small-to-medium tabular datasets, deep networks often overfit due to high capacity. Well-regularized logistic regression generalizes better with limited data.

text: What is the effect of class imbalance on the decision boundary of a standard SVM?
options: Class imbalance has no effect on SVM decision boundaries in any scenario ever
The boundary shifts toward the minority class increasing its misclassification rate
The boundary always shifts toward the majority class regardless of data layout
SVM automatically handles class imbalance without any parameter adjustments
explanation: Standard SVM's decision boundary shifts toward the minority class because the majority class exerts more influence, increasing minority misclassification.

text: What is an ensemble method in machine learning?
options: Combining multiple models
A data cleaning technique
A single complex model
A visualization approach
explanation: Ensemble methods combine predictions from multiple models to produce better results than any individual model alone.

text: Which ensemble method trains models on random data subsets?
options: Stacking
Bagging
Pruning
Boosting
explanation: Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) trains multiple models on random subsets of the data sampled with replacement.

text: What is Random Forest built upon?
options: Multiple logistic regression models combined together
Multiple SVM models with different kernel functions
Multiple neural networks of varying layer depths
Multiple decision trees with random feature subsets
explanation: Random Forest is an ensemble of decision trees, each trained on random data subsets with random feature subsets.

text: How does a majority voting ensemble make predictions?
options: Using the most common prediction
Using the maximum prediction
Using the average prediction value
Using the minimum prediction
explanation: Majority voting selects the class predicted by the most individual models as the final ensemble prediction.

text: What is the main advantage of ensemble methods over single models?
options: They need less data input
They always train faster
They use less memory
They reduce overall error
explanation: Ensemble methods typically reduce error by combining diverse models, reducing variance and sometimes bias.

text: Which technique trains models sequentially to correct prior errors?
options: Bagging
Boosting
Blending
Stacking
explanation: Boosting trains models sequentially, with each new model focusing on correcting the errors made by previous models.

text: What does the term 'base learner' mean in ensemble learning?
options: The evaluation metric used
An individual model in ensemble
The training data subset used
The final combined model
explanation: A base learner is an individual model within an ensemble, also called a weak learner, whose predictions are combined.

text: Which of these is a popular gradient boosting library?
options: XGBoost
NumPy
Matplotlib
Pandas
explanation: XGBoost is a highly popular and efficient gradient boosting library known for winning many ML competitions.

text: What is the purpose of bootstrap sampling in bagging?
options: To select the best features
To normalize feature values
To create diverse training sets
To remove outliers from data
explanation: Bootstrap sampling creates multiple diverse training sets by sampling with replacement, ensuring model diversity in the ensemble.

text: How does Random Forest handle the prediction for classification tasks?
options: Uses the prediction of the last tree only
Uses the prediction of the first tree only
Takes majority vote from all the trees
Averages all tree predictions numerically
explanation: Random Forest uses majority voting across all trees for classification, selecting the class predicted most frequently.

text: How does feature randomness in Random Forest improve model performance?
options: It guarantees each tree will have identical accuracy on test data set
It eliminates the need for any hyperparameter tuning in the model
It automatically removes all irrelevant features from the dataset used
It reduces correlation between trees leading to better ensemble diversity
explanation: Random feature subsets reduce correlation between trees, increasing ensemble diversity. Diverse trees make different errors that cancel out when combined.

text: What is the key difference between bagging and boosting ensemble techniques?
options: Bagging trains models sequentially while boosting trains all models in parallel
Bagging trains models in parallel independently while boosting trains sequentially
Bagging always outperforms boosting on every possible dataset and problem type
Bagging only works with neural networks while boosting only works with trees
explanation: Bagging trains independent models in parallel on bootstrap samples, while boosting trains sequentially, each model correcting prior errors.

text: What does the learning rate parameter control in gradient boosting?
options: The contribution of each tree to the final ensemble prediction output
The percentage of features randomly selected at each tree split
The number of trees to include in the final ensemble model overall
The maximum depth allowed for each individual decision tree used
explanation: The learning rate (shrinkage) scales each tree's contribution, with smaller values requiring more trees but often achieving better generalization.

text: What is stacking in the context of ensemble methods?
options: Using a meta-learner to combine predictions from diverse base models
Removing the worst-performing model from the ensemble iteratively
Training the same model architecture on different random data subsets
Simply averaging the predictions from all models in the ensemble
explanation: Stacking uses a meta-learner trained on base model predictions to learn the optimal way to combine diverse models.

text: Why might an ensemble of weak learners outperform a single strong learner?
options: Combining diverse weak learners reduces variance without increasing bias much
Weak learners always have lower bias than any single strong learner model
Weak learners are always faster to train than any single strong learner is
An ensemble of weak learners never overfits regardless of ensemble size used
explanation: Combining diverse weak learners reduces variance through averaging, and boosting can also reduce bias, often outperforming a single complex model.

text: How does AdaBoost assign weights to training samples?
options: All samples receive equal weights throughout the entire training process
Misclassified samples receive higher weights so subsequent models focus on them
Weights are randomly assigned at each iteration without considering any errors
Correctly classified samples receive higher weights for reinforcement purposes
explanation: AdaBoost increases weights of misclassified samples so subsequent weak learners focus on the hard-to-classify examples.

text: What is out-of-bag error estimation in Random Forest?
options: Evaluating each tree on samples not included in its bootstrap training set
Using a separate test set that was manually created before model training
Measuring training error on the full dataset after all trees are built
Running cross-validation with exactly five folds on the entire dataset used
explanation: OOB error evaluates each tree on samples not in its bootstrap set, providing a built-in validation estimate without a separate test set.

text: What is the purpose of subsampling in Stochastic Gradient Boosting?
options: To convert the boosting algorithm into a bagging algorithm automatically
To ensure every sample is used exactly once across all trees in ensemble
To increase training time by processing every sample multiple times each
To reduce overfitting and training time by using random data subsets per tree
explanation: Subsampling in Stochastic Gradient Boosting uses random subsets per tree, reducing overfitting and computational cost while maintaining accuracy.

text: How does the max_depth parameter affect individual trees in an ensemble?
options: Deeper trees always improve ensemble accuracy without any risk of overfitting
Max depth has no effect on individual tree complexity or ensemble performance
Deeper trees have lower bias and higher variance capturing more complex splits
Deeper trees have higher bias and lower variance across ensemble predictions
explanation: Deeper trees fit more complex patterns (lower bias) but are more prone to overfitting (higher variance). Ensemble aggregation helps manage the variance.

text: Why is diversity important among base learners in an ensemble?
options: Diversity ensures all models make exactly the same predictions on every input
Diversity is irrelevant since all models should be identical for best results
Diverse models make different errors that can cancel out when combined together
Diverse models always have individually higher accuracy than identical models
explanation: Diverse models make uncorrelated errors, allowing the ensemble to correct individual model mistakes through aggregation.

text: In a gradient boosting ensemble, how does early stopping prevent overfitting?
options: It reduces the learning rate to exactly zero after a fixed number of trees
It removes the first few trees which are assumed to have the highest error
It monitors validation loss and stops adding trees when performance degrades
It limits the maximum number of features each tree can use at any split
explanation: Early stopping monitors validation loss during training and stops adding trees when performance on validation data begins to degrade.

text: Why does XGBoost use regularization terms in its objective function unlike traditional GBDT?
options: Regularization slows training to allow more careful optimization at each step
It penalizes tree complexity reducing overfitting and improving generalization
XGBoost regularization only affects the first tree and has no effect on others
Regularization in XGBoost replaces the need for any learning rate parameter
explanation: XGBoost adds L1 and L2 regularization on leaf weights and a penalty on tree complexity, explicitly controlling overfitting unlike traditional gradient boosting.

text: When would you prefer LightGBM's leaf-wise growth over XGBoost's level-wise growth?
options: When all features are categorical and no numerical features exist in data
When the dataset is very small and overfitting is a major concern for accuracy
When you need the simplest possible model with minimal hyperparameters to set
When training speed is critical and the dataset is large with many features
explanation: LightGBM's leaf-wise growth is faster on large datasets because it grows the leaf with the maximum loss reduction, while XGBoost's level-wise approach is more conservative.

text: How does the DART algorithm modify standard gradient boosting to reduce overfitting?
options: It randomly drops previously built trees during each boosting iteration step
It uses only the first and last trees and ignores all trees built in between
It doubles the learning rate at each iteration to converge more quickly here
It trains all trees simultaneously rather than sequentially to save total time
explanation: DART (Dropouts meet Multiple Additive Regression Trees) randomly drops trees during iteration, preventing over-specialization and reducing overfitting.

text: In a production system, why might model ensembles be problematic despite higher accuracy?
options: Ensembles are impossible to train on any modern GPU or cloud computing platform
They increase inference latency, memory usage, and deployment complexity significantly
Ensembles always produce less accurate predictions than single model systems
They require manual human review for every single prediction made by the system
explanation: Ensembles multiply computational cost at inference, require more memory, complicate deployment and monitoring, which may be prohibitive in latency-sensitive production systems.

text: What is the theoretical basis for why bagging reduces variance in predictions?
options: The theoretical basis is unknown and bagging works only through empirical results
Bagging increases variance because it uses more models than a single learner
Bagging reduces bias not variance since it trains on different data subsets each
Averaging independent estimates reduces variance by a factor of the ensemble size
explanation: By averaging N independent estimates, variance reduces by 1/N. Bootstrap samples approximate independent samples, so bagging approximates this variance reduction.

text: How does CatBoost handle categorical features differently from XGBoost and LightGBM?
options: CatBoost requires all features to be one-hot encoded before training can begin
CatBoost converts all categorical features into random numerical values first
CatBoost uses ordered target statistics to encode categoricals avoiding leakage
CatBoost cannot process any categorical features and only handles numerical ones
explanation: CatBoost uses ordered target statistics with a permutation-based approach to encode categorical features, avoiding target leakage that naive target encoding causes.

text: In a stacking ensemble, why should base model predictions be generated using cross-validation?
options: It prevents data leakage since base models should not predict on their training data
It ensures that all base models have identical performance before stacking them
Cross-validation makes base models train faster than using the full training set
Cross-validation automatically selects the best base models for the final stack
explanation: Using CV for base model predictions prevents data leakage - if base models predict on their training data, the meta-learner sees overfitted predictions leading to poor generalization.

text: What is the relationship between ensemble size and the law of diminishing returns?
options: Smaller ensembles always outperform larger ensembles due to reduced complexity
Adding more models always linearly improves accuracy without any upper bound limit
After a point additional models contribute marginal gains while increasing costs
Ensemble size has no effect on performance and only affects training speed value
explanation: Each additional model provides diminishing accuracy gains while linearly increasing computation. The optimal size balances accuracy improvement against resource costs.

text: Why is Bayesian optimization particularly effective for tuning ensemble hyperparameters?
options: It only works with ensemble methods and cannot tune single model parameters
It models the objective function to intelligently select promising configurations
Bayesian optimization always finds the global optimum in exactly one iteration
Bayesian optimization randomly samples hyperparameters like grid search does
explanation: Bayesian optimization builds a probabilistic model of the objective function, intelligently selecting hyperparameter configurations that are likely to perform well.

text: What is unsupervised learning?
options: Learning from labeled data
Learning with human feedback
Learning from test data only
Learning without any labels
explanation: Unsupervised learning finds patterns in data without labeled outputs, relying on the structure within the data itself.

text: What is the goal of clustering in unsupervised learning?
options: Predicting a target value
Generating synthetic data
Grouping similar data points
Reducing training time
explanation: Clustering groups similar data points together based on their features, without any predefined labels or categories.

text: Which algorithm groups data into K predefined number of clusters?
options: K-Nearest Neighbors
K-Fold Validation
K-Means Clustering
K-Mode Analysis
explanation: K-Means clustering partitions data into K predefined clusters by minimizing the distance between points and cluster centroids.

text: What is dimensionality reduction used for?
options: Increasing the sample count
Reducing the number of features
Converting data to text format
Adding more features to data
explanation: Dimensionality reduction reduces the number of features while preserving important information, making data easier to process.

text: Which technique is a common dimensionality reduction method?
options: Principal Component Analysis
Logistic Regression
Random Forest model
Gradient Boosting
explanation: PCA (Principal Component Analysis) is a widely used dimensionality reduction technique that projects data onto its principal components.

text: What is the centroid in K-Means clustering?
options: The smallest value in a cluster
The label assigned to a cluster
The data point farthest from cluster
The center point of a cluster
explanation: The centroid is the center point of a cluster, computed as the mean of all data points assigned to that cluster.

text: Which type of learning is used for customer segmentation?
options: Supervised learning
Reinforcement learning
Unsupervised learning
Semi-supervised learning
explanation: Customer segmentation uses unsupervised learning (clustering) to group customers into segments without predefined labels.

text: What does anomaly detection aim to find?
options: The most common patterns
The best classification model
Unusual or rare data points
The optimal feature weights
explanation: Anomaly detection identifies unusual or rare data points that deviate significantly from the majority of the data.

text: What is a dendrogram used to visualize?
options: Neural network architecture
Linear regression coefficients
Hierarchical clustering results
Confusion matrix of a model
explanation: A dendrogram is a tree-like diagram that visualizes the hierarchical relationships between clusters in hierarchical clustering.

text: Which algorithm does not require specifying the number of clusters beforehand?
options: Mini-Batch K-Means
K-Medoids
K-Means
DBSCAN
explanation: DBSCAN automatically determines the number of clusters based on data density, unlike K-Means which requires K to be specified.

text: How does DBSCAN determine cluster membership compared to K-Means?
options: DBSCAN requires the number of clusters while K-Means determines it automatically
DBSCAN groups points by density connectivity while K-Means uses centroid distance
DBSCAN uses distance to centroids while K-Means uses density-based grouping
Both algorithms use identical methods to determine cluster membership always
explanation: DBSCAN groups points based on density connectivity (eps and min_samples), while K-Means assigns points to the nearest centroid.

text: What is the elbow method used for in K-Means clustering?
options: To decide the maximum number of iterations for the K-Means convergence
To determine the optimal number of features to use for the clustering task
To evaluate whether K-Means or DBSCAN is the better algorithm for the data
To select the optimal K by finding the point where inertia reduction slows
explanation: The elbow method plots inertia (within-cluster sum of squares) versus K, and the optimal K is at the elbow where the rate of decrease sharply changes.

text: What does the silhouette score measure in clustering evaluation?
options: How well each point fits its cluster versus the nearest neighboring cluster
The accuracy of cluster labels compared to true class labels in dataset
The total computational time required to train the clustering algorithm
The number of outliers present in each cluster after model convergence
explanation: Silhouette score measures how similar a point is to its own cluster versus the nearest cluster, ranging from -1 to 1 with higher being better.

text: How does hierarchical clustering differ from K-Means in its approach?
options: Hierarchical builds a tree of nested clusters while K-Means uses flat partitions
Hierarchical requires specifying K while K-Means does not need any parameters
Hierarchical only works with text data while K-Means works with numbers only
Hierarchical always produces fewer clusters than K-Means on the same data set
explanation: Hierarchical clustering builds a hierarchy of nested clusters (dendrogram), while K-Means directly partitions data into K flat clusters.

text: What is the role of the epsilon parameter in DBSCAN?
options: It defines the minimum number of clusters the algorithm must discover
It sets the maximum distance for two points to be considered neighbors
It controls the learning rate for optimizing the cluster assignments
It specifies the dimensionality reduction ratio before clustering starts
explanation: Epsilon (eps) defines the maximum distance between two points for them to be considered part of the same neighborhood in DBSCAN.

text: Why might PCA lose important information during dimensionality reduction?
options: PCA always preserves all information regardless of components selected count
It projects onto directions of maximum variance and may discard informative ones
PCA can only reduce to exactly two dimensions and no other number of them
It requires labeled data and fails when applied to unsupervised datasets
explanation: PCA keeps components with the highest variance, but some lower-variance components may contain discriminative information relevant to the task.

text: What is the difference between agglomerative and divisive hierarchical clustering?
options: Agglomerative starts with individual points and merges while divisive starts whole and splits
Both approaches always produce identical clustering results on any dataset given to them
Agglomerative only works with numerical data while divisive handles categorical data only
Agglomerative starts with one cluster and splits while divisive starts with individual points
explanation: Agglomerative (bottom-up) starts with each point as a cluster and merges, while divisive (top-down) starts with one cluster and splits.

text: How does t-SNE differ from PCA for dimensionality reduction?
options: Both methods produce identical visualizations on any dataset given to them always
PCA is non-linear while t-SNE is a purely linear dimensionality reduction method
PCA preserves global linear structure while t-SNE preserves local non-linear structure
PCA can only reduce to two dimensions while t-SNE works with any target dimension
explanation: PCA preserves global linear structure, while t-SNE focuses on preserving local neighborhood structure, better for visualizing non-linear clusters.

text: What challenge does the curse of dimensionality pose for clustering algorithms?
options: High-dimensional data always forms perfectly separated clusters automatically here
It makes clustering faster because more dimensions provide more information now
The curse of dimensionality only affects supervised learning and never clustering
In high dimensions distance metrics become less meaningful reducing cluster quality
explanation: In high dimensions, distances between points become similar, making it hard for clustering algorithms to distinguish close from far points.

text: What is Gaussian Mixture Model clustering based on?
options: A density-based approach identical to DBSCAN using epsilon neighborhoods
Probabilistic assignment assuming data comes from a mixture of Gaussian distributions
Hard assignment of each point to exactly one cluster using closest centroid
A hierarchical approach that builds a dendrogram of cluster relationships
explanation: GMM assumes data is generated from a mixture of Gaussian distributions, using probability to soft-assign points to clusters.

text: Why might K-Means converge to a suboptimal solution and how is this typically addressed?
options: Suboptimal convergence only occurs when the dataset has fewer than ten samples
K-Means always finds the globally optimal solution regardless of initialization
It can converge to local optima so K-Means++ initialization is used for better starts
K-Means is guaranteed to diverge without specialized GPU hardware for computation
explanation: K-Means is sensitive to initial centroid placement and can converge to local optima. K-Means++ initialization selects better starting centroids, improving results.

text: How does spectral clustering handle non-convex cluster shapes that K-Means cannot?
options: It constructs a similarity graph and uses eigenvectors of its Laplacian for clustering
It converts all non-convex clusters into convex ones before applying K-Means again
It simply increases the number of clusters K until the shapes are captured correctly
It uses the same distance metric as K-Means but with more iterations for accuracy
explanation: Spectral clustering constructs a similarity graph, computes eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian to embed data, then applies K-Means in the spectral space.

text: What is the key advantage of UMAP over t-SNE for dimensionality reduction?
options: UMAP is a linear method while t-SNE is non-linear which makes UMAP faster here
UMAP requires labeled data while t-SNE works in a fully unsupervised manner only
UMAP better preserves global structure and scales to much larger datasets efficiently
UMAP always produces better visualizations than t-SNE on every possible dataset
explanation: UMAP preserves more global structure than t-SNE while being significantly faster, making it practical for large datasets.

text: In topic modeling, how does Latent Dirichlet Allocation discover topics from documents?
options: It models documents as mixtures of topics and topics as distributions over words
It uses supervised labels to assign documents to predefined topic categories only
It requires human experts to manually define all topics before running analysis
It clusters documents using K-Means on word frequency vectors directly always
explanation: LDA is a generative probabilistic model that represents documents as mixtures of topics and topics as distributions over words, discovering topics unsupervised.

text: Why is the choice of linkage criterion important in agglomerative hierarchical clustering?
options: All linkage criteria produce identical dendrograms regardless of the input dataset
Linkage criterion has no effect on the final clustering result in any case ever
Linkage criterion only affects the visualization of dendrogram and not the clusters
Different linkage criteria produce different cluster shapes and handle noise differently
explanation: Single linkage can create chain-like clusters, complete linkage produces compact clusters, Ward's minimizes variance. The choice significantly affects cluster shapes.

text: How does the Variational Autoencoder differ from a standard autoencoder for unsupervised learning?
options: Both models are identical in architecture and only differ in their training data used
VAE produces only deterministic encodings while standard autoencoders are probabilistic
VAE learns a probabilistic latent space enabling meaningful data generation sampling
Standard autoencoders can generate new data while VAEs can only compress existing data
explanation: VAEs learn a probabilistic latent space with continuous, structured representations, enabling meaningful generation of new data by sampling from the learned distribution.

text: When evaluating clustering without ground truth labels, why is the Davies-Bouldin Index useful?
options: It can only evaluate K-Means clustering and does not work with any other algorithms
It always produces a score between zero and one like the accuracy metric in classification
It measures the ratio of within-cluster to between-cluster distances with lower being better
It requires ground truth labels to compute making it a supervised evaluation metric
explanation: The Davies-Bouldin Index measures the average ratio of within-cluster scatter to between-cluster separation, with lower values indicating better-defined clusters.

text: What is the fundamental limitation of using reconstruction error alone to evaluate autoencoders?
options: Low reconstruction error may indicate memorization rather than useful representation learning
Reconstruction error can only be computed for image data and not for tabular data
Reconstruction error is too computationally expensive to compute on modern hardware
Reconstruction error always perfectly reflects the quality of the learned latent space
explanation: Low reconstruction error may mean the autoencoder memorized training data rather than learning useful generalizable representations, especially with high capacity.

text: How does contrastive learning create useful representations without labels?
options: It uses K-Means clustering as an intermediate step before training the final model
It requires ground truth cluster assignments to define the contrastive loss function
It trains models using manually annotated labels for every single data sample used
It learns to pull augmented views of same samples together and push different apart
explanation: Contrastive learning creates positive pairs from augmented views of the same sample and negative pairs from different samples, learning representations that capture semantic similarity.

text: Why is the Information Criterion approach preferred over the elbow method for determining optimal K?
options: Information criteria require no data and determine K from theoretical analysis alone here
The elbow method and information criteria always agree on the same optimal K value
The elbow method is always more accurate than any information criterion approach used
Information criteria like BIC provide a principled statistical framework for model selection
explanation: Information criteria (AIC, BIC) provide a rigorous statistical framework balancing model fit and complexity, while the elbow method relies on subjective visual interpretation.

text: What is a confusion matrix used for?
options: Visualizing data distributions
Evaluating classification results
Preprocessing raw data
Training neural networks
explanation: A confusion matrix summarizes classification results by showing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.

text: What does accuracy measure in classification?
options: The number of features used
The proportion of correct predictions
The size of the training data
The speed of model training
explanation: Accuracy is the proportion of correctly classified instances out of the total number of instances.

text: What is the purpose of splitting data into training and test sets?
options: To remove outliers from the original dataset
To increase the training time of the model
To reduce the total amount of data available
To evaluate how well the model generalizes
explanation: Splitting data evaluates generalization - training data teaches the model while test data measures performance on unseen data.

text: What does precision measure in a classification model?
options: The overall accuracy of the model on all classes
The speed of prediction for each data sample
The proportion of actual positives correctly identified
The proportion of positive predictions that are correct
explanation: Precision measures the proportion of positive predictions that are actually correct, indicating the model's exactness.

text: What does recall measure in a classification model?
options: The proportion of actual positives correctly identified
The number of features used by the trained model
The computational time required for each prediction
The proportion of positive predictions that are correct
explanation: Recall (sensitivity) measures the proportion of actual positive instances that the model correctly identifies.

text: What is cross-validation used for?
options: Generating synthetic data
Reducing the number of features
Assessing model performance robustly
Training the final production model
explanation: Cross-validation assesses model performance by training and testing on different data subsets, providing a robust performance estimate.

text: What is overfitting in the context of model evaluation?
options: The model performs well on training but poorly on test data
The model performs poorly on training data
The model has too few parameters
The model takes too long to train
explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model performs well on training data but poorly on test data, indicating it memorized noise.

text: What does the F1 score combine?
options: Bias and variance
Training and test error
Precision and recall
Accuracy and speed
explanation: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a balanced measure of both metrics.

text: What is a validation set used for?
options: Testing the final model performance
Training the model parameters
Cleaning and preprocessing the data
Tuning hyperparameters during development
