explanation: Confidence of a rule A→B is the conditional probability P(B|A), measuring how often B appears in transactions that contain A.

text: In PCA, the principal components are the eigenvectors of which matrix?
options: The original data matrix
The confusion matrix
The identity matrix
The covariance matrix of the data
explanation: Principal components are eigenvectors of the data's covariance matrix, with corresponding eigenvalues indicating the variance captured by each component.

text: In DBSCAN, a core point is defined as a point that has:
options: Exactly one neighbor
No neighbors
At least minPts points within its epsilon neighborhood
The maximum distance from all points
explanation: A core point has at least minPts neighboring points within its epsilon radius, forming the basis of dense cluster regions.

text: t-SNE differs from PCA in that it:
options: Maximizes variance
Preserves local structure through non-linear mapping
Is deterministic
Is a linear method
explanation: t-SNE is a non-linear technique that preserves local neighborhood structure, unlike PCA which is linear and preserves global variance.

text: The 'lift' metric in association rules measures:
options: How much more likely B occurs with A compared to independently
The confidence minus support
The support of the rule
The number of items in the rule
explanation: Lift measures the ratio of observed support to expected support if A and B were independent; lift > 1 indicates a positive association.

text: K-Means++ improves upon standard K-Means by:
options: Using only one centroid
Reducing the number of clusters needed
Using fewer iterations
Selecting initial centroids that are well-spread out
explanation: K-Means++ uses a smart initialization strategy that selects initial centroids proportionally spread out, leading to better and faster convergence.

text: Ward's method in hierarchical clustering minimizes:
options: Maximum distance between clusters
The number of clusters
The total within-cluster variance at each merge
Single linkage distance
explanation: Ward's method merges clusters that result in the minimum increase in total within-cluster variance, producing compact and similar-sized clusters.

text: The Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) differs from K-Means in that it:
options: Uses hard assignments only
Provides soft probabilistic cluster assignments
Does not use centroids
Cannot handle more than two clusters
explanation: GMM assigns probabilities of belonging to each cluster (soft assignment), unlike K-Means which makes hard assignments to the nearest centroid.

text: The perplexity parameter in t-SNE approximately controls:
options: The number of effective nearest neighbors considered
The learning rate
The number of output dimensions
The number of iterations
explanation: Perplexity in t-SNE loosely controls the number of effective nearest neighbors, balancing between local and global structure preservation.

text: Isolation Forest detects anomalies based on the principle that:
options: Anomalies are always at cluster centers
Anomalies have the highest density
Anomalies are the most frequent points
Anomalies are easier to isolate and require fewer splits in random trees
explanation: Isolation Forest exploits the fact that anomalies are few and different, requiring fewer random splits to isolate them from the rest of the data.

text: UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) improves upon t-SNE by:
options: Only working in 3D
Being a linear method
Better preserving global structure and being faster on large datasets
Requiring more parameters
explanation: UMAP generally preserves both local and global data structure better than t-SNE and scales more efficiently to large datasets.

text: Accuracy in classification is calculated as:
options: Precision × Recall
Correct Predictions / Total Predictions
True Positives / (True Positives + False Negatives)
True Positives / Total Predictions
explanation: Accuracy is the ratio of correct predictions (both true positives and true negatives) to the total number of predictions made.

text: A confusion matrix for binary classification has how many cells?
explanation: A binary confusion matrix has 4 cells: True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, and False Negatives.

text: Precision measures:
options: The proportion of actual positives correctly identified
The number of features used
The overall accuracy
The proportion of predicted positives that are actually positive
explanation: Precision is TP / (TP + FP), measuring how many of the predicted positive cases are actually positive.

text: Recall (sensitivity) measures:
options: The overall error rate
The proportion of predicted positives that are correct
The proportion of actual positives that were correctly identified
The specificity of the model
explanation: Recall is TP / (TP + FN), measuring how many actual positive cases the model successfully identified.

text: The F1-score is the harmonic mean of:
options: Accuracy and precision
Precision and recall
Sensitivity and specificity
Accuracy and recall
explanation: The F1-score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall: 2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall), balancing both metrics.

text: Cross-validation is used to:
options: Evaluate model performance more reliably using multiple splits
Remove outliers
Train the model faster
Increase the dataset size
explanation: Cross-validation evaluates model performance by splitting data into multiple folds, training and testing on different subsets for a more reliable estimate.

text: A True Positive (TP) in classification means the model:
options: Incorrectly predicted negative
Correctly predicted negative
Incorrectly predicted positive
Correctly predicted positive
explanation: A True Positive occurs when the model correctly predicts the positive class, matching the actual positive label.

text: Hyperparameters are values that are:
options: Learned during training
Calculated from test data
Set before training begins
Always equal to 1
explanation: Hyperparameters are configuration values set before training (like learning rate, K in KNN) that are not learned from the data.

text: ROC stands for:
options: Rate of Convergence
Receiver Operating Characteristic
Random Optimization Chart
Regression Output Curve
explanation: ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve plots the True Positive Rate against the False Positive Rate at various classification thresholds.

text: A False Positive (FP) is also known as a:
options: Type I error
True alarm
Type II error
Correct rejection
explanation: A False Positive (Type I error) occurs when the model incorrectly predicts the positive class when the actual class is negative.

text: In K-fold cross-validation with K=5, the model is trained and evaluated how many times?
explanation: In 5-fold cross-validation, the model is trained and evaluated 5 times, each time using a different fold as the test set and the remaining 4 for training.

text: AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve) of 0.5 indicates:
options: No predictions made
Perfect classification
Random guessing performance
Very good model
explanation: An AUC of 0.5 means the model performs no better than random guessing, while 1.0 indicates perfect classification.

text: Grid search for hyperparameter tuning works by:
options: Training only once
Exhaustively trying all combinations of specified parameter values
Using gradient descent on parameters
Randomly sampling parameters
explanation: Grid search evaluates all possible combinations of hyperparameter values from a specified grid, selecting the combination with the best cross-validation score.

text: Accuracy is a misleading metric when the dataset is:
options: Highly imbalanced
Large
Balanced
Numerical
explanation: With imbalanced data, a model can achieve high accuracy by simply predicting the majority class, making accuracy misleading for minority class performance.

text: A learning curve plots model performance against:
options: The number of features
The test set size only
The number of classes
The size of the training data
explanation: A learning curve shows how model performance changes as the training set size increases, helping diagnose underfitting or overfitting.

text: When recall is more important than precision, it means we want to minimize:
options: True Positives
False Positives
False Negatives
True Negatives
explanation: Maximizing recall means minimizing False Negatives (missed positives), critical in scenarios like disease detection where missing a case is costly.

text: Stratified K-fold cross-validation ensures that each fold:
options: Contains only one class
Maintains the same class distribution as the full dataset
Is shuffled randomly
Has equal number of features
explanation: Stratified K-fold ensures each fold has approximately the same proportion of each class as the complete dataset, important for imbalanced data.

text: The specificity of a model measures the proportion of:
options: Actual negatives correctly predicted
All correct predictions
Actual positives correctly predicted
Predicted positives that are correct
explanation: Specificity (True Negative Rate) is TN / (TN + FP), measuring the proportion of actual negatives that the model correctly identifies.

text: Random search for hyperparameters differs from grid search by:
options: Only testing one parameter at a time
Using no parameters
Testing all combinations
Randomly sampling a fixed number of parameter combinations
explanation: Random search samples a fixed number of random combinations from the parameter space, often finding good parameters faster than exhaustive grid search.

text: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) differs from MSE in that it:
options: Squares the errors
Ignores large errors
Uses absolute differences without squaring
Is always larger than MSE
explanation: MAE computes the average of absolute differences between predictions and actual values, treating all errors equally without the squaring that amplifies large errors in MSE.

text: The precision-recall tradeoff means that as the classification threshold increases:
options: Both precision and recall increase
Precision tends to increase while recall decreases
Neither is affected
Both decrease equally
explanation: Raising the threshold makes the model more selective (higher precision) but misses more true positives (lower recall), creating a tradeoff.

text: The macro-averaged F1-score for multi-class classification:
options: Computes F1 for each class and takes the unweighted mean
Uses only the majority class
Weights by class frequency
Is the same as accuracy
explanation: Macro F1 calculates the F1-score independently for each class and then takes the unweighted average, treating all classes equally regardless of size.

text: Bayesian optimization for hyperparameter tuning uses:
options: Random sampling only
No statistical methods
Exhaustive search
A probabilistic model to guide the search toward promising regions
explanation: Bayesian optimization builds a probabilistic surrogate model of the objective function and uses it to intelligently select the most promising hyperparameters to evaluate next.

text: Cohen's Kappa score accounts for:
options: Training time
Only model accuracy
Agreement between predictions and labels beyond chance
The number of features
explanation: Cohen's Kappa measures the agreement between predicted and actual classifications, adjusted for the agreement that would occur by chance alone.

text: In a learning curve, if training and validation scores are both low, the model is likely:
options: Out of memory
Underfitting (high bias)
Perfectly fit
Overfitting
explanation: Both low training and validation scores indicate underfitting (high bias), meaning the model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns.

text: The log-loss (cross-entropy loss) penalizes predictions that are:
options: Confident and wrong more heavily than uncertain predictions
Close to the true label
Always correct
Made on training data only
explanation: Log-loss heavily penalizes confident incorrect predictions because the logarithm of a near-zero probability produces a very large loss value.

text: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV) uses each sample as a test set exactly:
options: Never
K times
Twice
Once, with all remaining samples for training
explanation: LOOCV trains on N-1 samples and tests on the remaining 1, repeating N times so each sample serves as the test set exactly once.

text: The Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) is preferred for imbalanced datasets because it:
options: Ignores false negatives
Only considers true positives
Uses all four confusion matrix values and is balanced even with class imbalance
Is always positive
explanation: MCC uses TP, TN, FP, and FN to produce a balanced measure from -1 to 1, providing a reliable metric even with highly imbalanced datasets.

text: The calibration curve (reliability diagram) assesses whether a model's:
options: Feature importances are correct
Predicted probabilities match actual frequencies of outcomes
Hyperparameters are optimal
Training speed is adequate
explanation: A calibration curve compares predicted probabilities against actual observed frequencies, showing whether a model's probability estimates are well-calibrated.

text: Nested cross-validation is used when:
options: Both model selection (hyperparameter tuning) and performance evaluation must be unbiased
Only one model is being evaluated
No test set is available
The dataset is very small with only 5 samples
explanation: Nested CV uses an inner loop for hyperparameter tuning and an outer loop for performance estimation, providing an unbiased evaluation of the tuning process itself.

text: What is feature selection in machine learning?
options: Removing all categorical features
Creating new features from scratch
Choosing the most relevant features from a dataset
Training a model on all features
explanation: Feature selection is the process of choosing the most relevant features to improve model performance and reduce overfitting.

text: What is the purpose of feature engineering?
options: To remove all outliers from data
To increase the number of training samples
To train multiple models simultaneously
To transform raw data into meaningful features for better model performance
explanation: Feature engineering transforms raw data into features that better represent the underlying problem, improving model accuracy.

text: Which of the following is a filter method for feature selection?
options: Recursive Feature Elimination
Correlation-based selection
Lasso Regression
Forward selection
explanation: Correlation-based selection is a filter method that ranks features based on their statistical correlation with the target variable.

text: What does one-hot encoding do?
options: Removes duplicate features
Normalizes the target variable
Scales numerical features to a range of 0-1
Converts categorical variables into binary columns
explanation: One-hot encoding converts each category into a separate binary column, allowing categorical data to be used in ML models.

text: What is feature extraction?
options: Splitting data into training and test sets
Deleting irrelevant data columns
Reducing dimensionality by creating new features from existing ones
Sorting features alphabetically
explanation: Feature extraction creates new features from existing data, often reducing dimensionality while preserving important information.

text: Which technique is commonly used for feature extraction?
options: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
Gradient Descent
Cross-validation
K-Means Clustering
explanation: PCA is a widely used feature extraction technique that transforms features into a set of linearly uncorrelated principal components.

text: What is a polynomial feature?
options: A feature selected using random sampling
A feature created by raising existing features to a power or multiplying them together
A feature that changes over time
A feature with only two possible values
explanation: Polynomial features are created by raising existing features to powers or combining them multiplicatively to capture non-linear relationships.

text: Why might you remove highly correlated features?
options: They provide redundant information and can cause multicollinearity
They always improve accuracy
They make the dataset smaller
They are always categorical
explanation: Highly correlated features carry redundant information and can cause multicollinearity, which negatively affects certain models.

text: What is label encoding?
options: Encoding images as text
Removing labels from data
Assigning numerical labels to categories
Creating multiple binary columns
explanation: Label encoding assigns a unique integer to each category, converting categorical text data into numerical form.

text: What is feature scaling?
options: Normalizing or standardizing feature values to a common range
Adding more features to the dataset
Converting features to text format
Removing features with low variance
explanation: Feature scaling brings all features to a similar range, preventing features with larger values from dominating the model.

text: What is the key difference between wrapper and filter methods for feature selection?
options: Wrapper methods evaluate feature subsets using a model, while filter methods use statistical measures
Filter methods require more computation
Wrapper methods only work with linear models
Filter methods are always faster
explanation: Wrapper methods use a predictive model to evaluate feature subsets, while filter methods rely on statistical measures independent of any model.

text: What is Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)?
options: A filter method that ranks features by correlation
A wrapper method that recursively removes the least important features
An embedded method using L1 regularization
A technique for generating synthetic features
explanation: RFE is a wrapper method that repeatedly trains a model and removes the least important feature until the desired number of features is reached.

text: Which of the following creates interaction features?
options: Taking the log of a single feature
Multiplying two or more features together
Encoding ordinal categories
Standardizing features to zero mean
explanation: Interaction features are created by multiplying two or more features together to capture combined effects on the target variable.

text: What is the variance threshold method used for?
options: Normalizing feature variance to 1
Selecting features with variance above a certain threshold
Adding noise to low-variance features
Computing the variance of the target variable
explanation: Variance threshold removes features with variance below a specified value, eliminating near-constant features that carry little information.

text: What is the advantage of using Lasso (L1) regularization for feature selection?
options: It works only on categorical data
It always selects exactly half the features
It requires no hyperparameter tuning
It can shrink feature coefficients to exactly zero, effectively selecting features
explanation: L1 regularization can drive coefficients to exactly zero, providing an embedded feature selection mechanism within model training.

text: Which domain-specific feature would be most useful for a time-series sales prediction model?
options: Word frequency in product descriptions
Hash of the customer ID
Day of the week and holiday indicators
Pixel intensity of product images
explanation: Day of the week and holiday indicators capture temporal patterns that directly influence sales, making them valuable domain-specific features.

text: What is target encoding for categorical variables?
options: Using the target variable as a feature directly
Encoding the target variable as one-hot
Replacing categories with the mean of the target variable for that category
Removing the target variable from the dataset
explanation: Target encoding replaces each category with the mean target value for that category, capturing the relationship between the feature and target.

text: What problem can arise with one-hot encoding when a categorical feature has many unique values?
options: It removes missing values
It always reduces model accuracy
It converts numbers to text
It creates too many sparse binary columns, increasing dimensionality
explanation: One-hot encoding creates one column per category, so features with many categories lead to high-dimensional sparse data (the curse of dimensionality).

text: What is automated feature engineering?
options: Using algorithms to automatically generate and select useful features
Training a model without any feature preprocessing
Removing all engineered features from a model
Manually selecting features based on domain knowledge
explanation: Automated feature engineering uses tools like Featuretools to automatically create and select features from relational datasets.

text: What is the purpose of binning (discretization) in feature engineering?
options: Splitting the dataset into folds
Removing outliers from the dataset
Converting continuous values into categorical bins or intervals
Encoding binary features
explanation: Binning converts continuous numerical features into discrete intervals, which can help capture non-linear relationships and reduce noise.

text: In mutual information-based feature selection, what does a mutual information score of zero between a feature and target indicate?
options: The feature has zero variance
The feature and target are statistically independent
The feature is perfectly correlated with the target
The target variable is continuous
explanation: A mutual information score of zero means the feature and target share no information, indicating statistical independence.

text: What risk does target encoding introduce if not handled carefully?
options: It always increases dimensionality
It removes all categorical features
It makes features non-numeric
It can cause target leakage, leading to overfitting
explanation: Target encoding can leak target information into features, causing overfitting. Techniques like smoothing and cross-validation encoding mitigate this.

text: What is the 'curse of dimensionality' in the context of feature engineering?
options: It refers to missing values in high-dimensional data
As the number of features increases, data becomes sparser and models may perform worse
Dimensionality always improves accuracy
Models become faster with more features
explanation: The curse of dimensionality means that as features increase, the data space becomes exponentially sparse, making it harder for models to find patterns.

text: How does Featuretools implement Deep Feature Synthesis (DFS)?
options: By stacking transformation and aggregation primitives across relational tables
By training a neural network for each feature
By applying PCA recursively
By using deep learning to generate features
explanation: DFS in Featuretools creates features by stacking transformation and aggregation primitives across related tables in a relational dataset.

text: What advantage does Elastic Net provide over using Lasso or Ridge alone for feature selection?
options: It works only on unsupervised learning tasks
It requires no regularization parameter
It combines L1 and L2 penalties, handling correlated features better than Lasso alone
It always selects fewer features than Lasso
explanation: Elastic Net combines L1 and L2 regularization, selecting features like Lasso while handling groups of correlated features more effectively.

text: When using polynomial features of degree 2 on a dataset with 10 features, approximately how many features are generated (including the original)?
explanation: Degree 2 polynomial features for n features produces C(n+2, 2) = (n+1)(n+2)/2 = 66 features, including original, squared, and interaction terms.

text: What is feature hashing (the hashing trick)?
options: Encrypting features for security
Sorting features by hash values
Mapping high-dimensional categorical features to a fixed-size vector using a hash function
Removing duplicate features using hashing
explanation: Feature hashing maps categories to a fixed-size vector via a hash function, efficiently handling high-cardinality features without a full dictionary.

text: What is the Boruta algorithm used for?
options: Clustering features into groups
Generating polynomial features automatically
Encoding categorical variables
An all-relevant feature selection method based on random forest shadow features
explanation: Boruta is a feature selection algorithm that creates shadow features (randomized copies) and uses random forest to identify all relevant features.

text: Why is log transformation commonly applied to skewed numerical features?
options: It always increases feature variance
It converts continuous features to categorical
It removes all negative values
It compresses the range and makes the distribution more symmetric, benefiting linear models
explanation: Log transformation reduces right-skewness, compresses the range of large values, and makes distributions more normal, which helps linear models.

text: In time-series feature engineering, what are lag features?
options: Features that are always constant over time
Features that predict future values without any past data
Features with missing timestamps
Previous time step values of a variable used as features for the current prediction
explanation: Lag features use values from previous time steps as predictors, capturing temporal dependencies in time-series forecasting models.

text: What is a neuron in a neural network?
options: A type of database
A basic computational unit that receives inputs, applies weights, and produces an output
A hardware component
A loss function
explanation: A neuron is the fundamental unit of a neural network that computes a weighted sum of inputs, adds a bias, and applies an activation function.

text: What is an activation function?
options: A function that initializes model weights
A function that decides whether a neuron should be activated by introducing non-linearity
A function that calculates loss
A function that splits data into train and test sets
explanation: Activation functions introduce non-linearity into neural networks, enabling them to learn complex patterns beyond linear relationships.

text: What does ReLU stand for?
options: Regularized Linear Unit
Rectified Linear Unit
Recurrent Learning Utility
Reduced Learning Update
explanation: ReLU stands for Rectified Linear Unit, which outputs the input directly if positive and zero otherwise: f(x) = max(0, x).

text: What is the output range of the sigmoid activation function?
options: -1 to 1
0 to infinity
-infinity to infinity
0 to 1
explanation: The sigmoid function maps any input to a value between 0 and 1, making it useful for binary classification outputs.

text: What is backpropagation?
options: A method to split data into batches
A type of activation function
An algorithm that computes gradients of the loss with respect to weights for learning
A technique to initialize weights
explanation: Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight by applying the chain rule, enabling weight updates.

text: What does gradient descent do in neural network training?
options: Updates weights in the direction that minimizes the loss function
Adds more layers to the network
Randomly shuffles the training data
Increases the loss function value
explanation: Gradient descent iteratively updates model weights in the direction opposite to the gradient of the loss function to minimize error.

text: What is a perceptron?
options: A data preprocessing step
A multi-layer neural network
A type of loss function
The simplest form of a neural network with a single neuron
explanation: A perceptron is the simplest neural network model consisting of a single neuron that performs binary classification using a step function.

text: Which loss function is commonly used for binary classification?
options: Huber Loss
Hinge Loss
Mean Squared Error
Binary Cross-Entropy
explanation: Binary cross-entropy measures the difference between predicted probabilities and actual binary labels, making it ideal for binary classification.

text: What is the softmax function used for?
options: Initializing network weights
Reducing overfitting
Converting a vector of values into a probability distribution for multi-class classification
Binary classification only
explanation: Softmax converts raw output scores into probabilities that sum to 1, enabling multi-class classification with interpretable outputs.

text: What is dropout in neural networks?
options: Randomly deactivating neurons during training to prevent overfitting
Decreasing the learning rate over time
Removing layers permanently from the network
Dropping data samples from the training set
explanation: Dropout randomly sets a fraction of neuron outputs to zero during training, preventing the network from relying too heavily on specific neurons.

text: What problem does batch normalization address?
options: Data imbalance by resampling
Vanishing gradients by normalizing layer inputs to stabilize training
Overfitting by adding regularization
Feature selection by removing low-variance features
explanation: Batch normalization normalizes inputs to each layer, reducing internal covariate shift, stabilizing training, and allowing higher learning rates.

text: What is the key advantage of the Adam optimizer over standard SGD?
options: It always converges to the global minimum
It uses more memory than SGD
It requires no learning rate
It adapts the learning rate per parameter using first and second moment estimates
explanation: Adam combines momentum and RMSProp, adapting learning rates for each parameter using running averages of gradients and squared gradients.

text: What is the vanishing gradient problem?
options: Loss function reaching zero too quickly
Neurons producing constant output values
Weights becoming too large during training
Gradients becoming extremely small in early layers, making training very slow
explanation: In deep networks, gradients can shrink exponentially as they propagate backward through layers, making it hard for early layers to learn.

text: Why is ReLU preferred over sigmoid in hidden layers of deep networks?
options: ReLU eliminates the need for backpropagation
ReLU outputs probabilities between 0 and 1
ReLU always outputs positive values
ReLU mitigates the vanishing gradient problem and is computationally efficient
explanation: ReLU does not saturate for positive inputs (avoiding vanishing gradients) and is computationally cheap compared to sigmoid or tanh.

text: What is the difference between stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and mini-batch gradient descent?
options: Mini-batch always uses exactly 50% of the data
SGD uses the entire dataset per update
There is no difference between them
SGD uses one sample per update; mini-batch uses a small subset
explanation: SGD updates weights using a single training sample, while mini-batch gradient descent uses a small batch, balancing speed and stability.

text: What is the output range of the tanh activation function?
options: -infinity to 0
0 to 1
0 to infinity
-1 to 1
explanation: The tanh function outputs values between -1 and 1, making it zero-centered unlike sigmoid, which helps with gradient flow.

text: What is an epoch in neural network training?
options: A single weight update step
A hyperparameter for learning rate
One complete pass through the entire training dataset
The final layer of the network
explanation: An epoch represents one full iteration over the entire training dataset, after which the model has seen every training sample once.

text: What is the purpose of the learning rate in gradient descent?
options: It sets the batch size for training
It defines the dropout probability
It controls the step size for weight updates during optimization
It determines the number of layers in the network
explanation: The learning rate controls how large each weight update step is; too high causes divergence, too low causes slow convergence.

text: What does categorical cross-entropy loss measure?
options: The variance of input features
The number of misclassified samples
Distance between cluster centroids
The difference between predicted class probabilities and true one-hot encoded labels
explanation: Categorical cross-entropy computes the log loss between the predicted probability distribution and the true class distribution for multi-class tasks.

text: What is weight initialization and why is it important?
options: Setting all weights to zero for simplicity
Removing bias terms from the network
Assigning initial values to weights to ensure proper gradient flow and convergence
Freezing weights during training
explanation: Proper weight initialization (e.g., Xavier, He) ensures gradients neither vanish nor explode, enabling effective training from the start.

text: What is the 'dying ReLU' problem?
options: ReLU causing overfitting in shallow networks
ReLU outputs becoming too large
ReLU being incompatible with batch normalization
Neurons getting stuck outputting zero for all inputs, permanently ceasing to learn
explanation: If a neuron's weights update such that it always receives negative inputs, ReLU outputs zero and the gradient is zero, so it never recovers.

text: How does He initialization differ from Xavier initialization?
options: He initialization only works for output layers
He initialization is designed for ReLU activations, scaling by 2/n instead of 1/n
There is no difference; they are identical
He initialization uses smaller weights than Xavier
explanation: He initialization scales weights by sqrt(2/n_in), accounting for the fact that ReLU zeros out half the inputs, while Xavier uses sqrt(1/n_in).

text: What is gradient clipping used for?
options: Accelerating training by skipping small gradients
Removing gradients below a threshold
Converting gradients to integers
Preventing exploding gradients by capping gradient values to a maximum norm
explanation: Gradient clipping limits the gradient norm to a threshold, preventing the exploding gradient problem common in deep and recurrent networks.

text: What is the difference between L1 and L2 regularization in neural networks?
options: They are identical in their effect on the model
L1 adds absolute weight penalties promoting sparsity; L2 adds squared weight penalties promoting small weights
L1 is always better than L2
L2 promotes sparsity while L1 promotes small weights
explanation: L1 regularization adds the sum of absolute weights (encouraging sparsity), while L2 adds the sum of squared weights (encouraging small, distributed weights).

text: How does learning rate scheduling improve training?
options: By increasing the learning rate as loss decreases
By keeping the learning rate constant throughout training
By eliminating the need for an optimizer
By reducing the learning rate over time, allowing coarse early updates and fine later adjustments
explanation: Learning rate scheduling reduces the rate during training, enabling large steps initially for fast progress and smaller steps later for fine-tuning.

text: What is the Leaky ReLU activation function?
options: f(x) = max(0, x)
f(x) = max(alpha * x, x) where alpha is a small positive constant for negative inputs
f(x) = tanh(x)
explanation: Leaky ReLU allows a small gradient (alpha * x) for negative inputs, addressing the dying ReLU problem by ensuring neurons always have a non-zero gradient.

text: Why might a single perceptron fail to learn the XOR function?
options: Perceptrons cannot handle binary inputs
XOR is not linearly separable, and a single perceptron can only learn linear decision boundaries
XOR requires too much training data
XOR has too many output classes
explanation: The XOR function is not linearly separable, so a single perceptron with a linear decision boundary cannot correctly classify all XOR inputs.

text: What is the purpose of skip connections (residual connections) in deep networks?
options: To convert deep networks into shallow ones
To allow gradients to flow directly through shortcut paths, enabling training of very deep networks
To reduce the number of parameters
To skip training certain layers
explanation: Skip connections allow gradients to bypass layers via shortcut paths, mitigating vanishing gradients and enabling effective training of very deep networks like ResNet.

text: What is the computational complexity advantage of mini-batch gradient descent over batch gradient descent?
options: Mini-batch requires fewer total epochs
Mini-batch uses less memory but the same computation
Mini-batch updates weights more frequently, enabling faster convergence per wall-clock time
Batch gradient descent is always faster
explanation: Mini-batch processes subsets of data for more frequent weight updates, converging faster in wall-clock time even if requiring more total updates.

text: What does the RMSProp optimizer do differently from standard SGD?
options: It divides the learning rate by a running average of recent gradient magnitudes, adapting per parameter
It uses a fixed learning rate for all parameters
It ignores gradient information entirely
It adds momentum to all weight updates
explanation: RMSProp divides the learning rate by the root mean square of recent gradients, providing adaptive per-parameter learning rates and handling varying gradient scales.

text: What type of neural network is primarily used for image processing?
options: Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)
Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
Autoencoder
Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)
explanation: CNNs are designed to process grid-like data such as images, using convolutional layers to automatically learn spatial features.

text: What is a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) designed for?
options: Generating random numbers
Feature selection
Sequential data processing where order matters
Image classification
explanation: RNNs process sequential data by maintaining a hidden state that captures information from previous time steps in the sequence.

text: What is transfer learning?
options: Moving a model from one server to another
Using a pre-trained model on a new but related task
Training a model from scratch on new data
Transferring data between databases
explanation: Transfer learning reuses a model trained on a large dataset for a related task, reducing training time and data requirements.

text: What is a GAN (Generative Adversarial Network)?
options: A system where a generator and discriminator compete to produce realistic data
A network used only for text processing
A type of regression model
A network that classifies images into categories
explanation: A GAN consists of a generator that creates fake data and a discriminator that tries to distinguish real from fake, improving through competition.

text: What is an autoencoder?
options: A supervised classification model
A type of reinforcement learning agent
A neural network that learns to compress input into a lower-dimensional representation and reconstruct it
A model that automatically writes code
explanation: An autoencoder encodes input into a compressed latent representation and decodes it back, learning useful data representations.

text: What is the purpose of the attention mechanism in deep learning?
options: To speed up gradient descent
To normalize the input data
To reduce the number of parameters
To allow the model to focus on the most relevant parts of the input
explanation: The attention mechanism enables models to weigh different parts of the input differently, focusing on the most relevant information for each output.

text: What does LSTM stand for?
options: Long Short-Term Memory
Large Scale Transfer Method
Layered Structured Text Model
Linear Sequential Training Model
explanation: LSTM stands for Long Short-Term Memory, a type of RNN designed to learn long-range dependencies in sequential data.

text: What is fine-tuning in the context of deep learning?
options: Removing layers from a trained model
Adjusting hyperparameters randomly
Increasing the dataset size
Continuing training of a pre-trained model on a new dataset with a small learning rate
explanation: Fine-tuning involves taking a pre-trained model and training it further on task-specific data, usually with a small learning rate to preserve learned features.

text: What is the transformer architecture primarily known for?
options: Clustering data points
Processing sequences using self-attention instead of recurrence
Image generation
Reinforcement learning tasks
explanation: Transformers process sequences using self-attention mechanisms, enabling parallel processing and capturing long-range dependencies without recurrence.

text: What is a GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)?
options: A gradient update rule
A generative model for images
A simplified version of LSTM with fewer gates for processing sequential data
A type of CNN layer
explanation: GRU is a variant of LSTM with fewer parameters, using reset and update gates instead of separate forget, input, and output gates.

text: What is the key advantage of LSTMs over standard RNNs?
options: LSTMs use gating mechanisms to selectively remember and forget information, solving the vanishing gradient problem
LSTMs work only on image data
LSTMs are faster to train
LSTMs have fewer parameters
explanation: LSTMs use forget, input, and output gates to control information flow, allowing them to maintain long-term dependencies that standard RNNs struggle with.

text: In a GAN, what is the role of the discriminator?
options: To select training hyperparameters
To generate fake samples
To classify whether input data is real or generated by the generator
To encode input into latent space
explanation: The discriminator is a classifier trained to distinguish between real data samples and fake ones produced by the generator.

text: What is self-attention in transformers?
options: A mechanism where each element in a sequence attends to all other elements to compute its representation
Attention applied to the output layer only
A form of dropout regularization
A technique to reduce model size
explanation: Self-attention computes attention scores between all pairs of positions in a sequence, allowing each token to gather context from the entire sequence.

text: What is the encoder-decoder architecture commonly used for?
options: Sequence-to-sequence tasks like machine translation
Feature selection
Binary classification only
Data augmentation
explanation: The encoder-decoder architecture encodes input into a fixed representation and decodes it into an output sequence, commonly used for translation.

text: What is a variational autoencoder (VAE)?
options: An autoencoder with a fixed latent space
A type of GAN
An autoencoder without a decoder
An autoencoder that learns a probabilistic latent space, enabling generation of new data
explanation: A VAE constrains the latent space to follow a probability distribution, allowing it to generate new, meaningful data by sampling from that space.

text: What does multi-head attention achieve in transformers?
options: It eliminates the need for positional encoding
It allows the model to attend to information from different representation subspaces simultaneously
It reduces the number of attention computations
It replaces the feed-forward layers
explanation: Multi-head attention runs multiple attention mechanisms in parallel, each learning different aspects of the relationships between tokens.

text: What is the purpose of the bottleneck layer in an autoencoder?
options: To apply batch normalization
To increase the input dimensionality
To output final predictions
To force the network to learn a compressed representation of the input
explanation: The bottleneck layer has fewer neurons than input/output layers, forcing the autoencoder to learn the most essential compressed features of the data.

text: In transfer learning, what does 'freezing layers' mean?
options: Deleting layers from the model
Adding new layers to the model
Setting all weights to zero
Keeping the weights of certain layers fixed during fine-tuning
explanation: Freezing layers means their weights are not updated during training, preserving learned features while only training the newly added or unfrozen layers.

text: How does a GRU differ from an LSTM in terms of gates?
options: GRU has no gates
GRU has four gates while LSTM has two
GRU uses reset and update gates, while LSTM uses forget, input, and output gates
They have identical gate structures
explanation: GRU simplifies LSTM by combining the forget and input gates into an update gate and using a reset gate, resulting in fewer parameters.

text: What is mode collapse in GANs?
options: The model running out of memory
The generator producing limited varieties of output, failing to capture the full data distribution
The generator producing very diverse outputs
The discriminator becoming too weak
explanation: Mode collapse occurs when the generator learns to produce only a few types of outputs instead of the full diversity of the real data distribution.

text: How do positional encodings work in the original transformer architecture?
options: They use the index of each token directly
They assign random values to each position
They use sine and cosine functions of different frequencies to encode token positions
They use learned embeddings for each position
explanation: The original transformer uses sinusoidal functions of different frequencies for positional encoding, allowing the model to learn relative positions.

text: What is the Wasserstein GAN (WGAN) designed to improve?
options: Image resolution of generated samples
Inference speed of the generator
Training stability by using the Wasserstein distance instead of JS divergence as the loss function
The number of layers in the discriminator
explanation: WGAN uses the Earth Mover's (Wasserstein) distance, providing more meaningful gradients and improving training stability over the original GAN loss.

text: What is the computational complexity of self-attention with respect to sequence length n?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Self-attention computes pairwise attention scores for all positions, resulting in O(n^2) complexity with respect to sequence length.

text: In a transformer, what are the Query, Key, and Value matrices used for?
options: They compute attention by matching queries against keys to weight the values
They are used for positional encoding
They represent the encoder, decoder, and output layers
They replace the feed-forward network
explanation: Queries and Keys compute attention weights via dot products, and these weights are applied to Values to produce context-aware representations.

text: What is the key innovation of ResNet that enabled training of networks with 100+ layers?
options: Using a different activation function
Using larger batch sizes
Reducing the number of parameters per layer
Introducing residual (skip) connections that learn residual mappings F(x) + x
explanation: ResNet's skip connections let layers learn residual functions F(x) = H(x) - x, making it easier to train identity mappings and enabling very deep networks.

text: What is the teacher forcing technique used in sequence-to-sequence models?
options: Using the ground truth output as input to the decoder during training instead of the model's own predictions
Using a pre-trained teacher model for knowledge distillation
Training two models simultaneously
Forcing the model to use a specific learning rate
explanation: Teacher forcing feeds the actual target sequence as decoder input during training, speeding convergence but potentially causing exposure bias at inference.

text: What problem does the deconvolutional (transposed convolution) layer solve in CNNs?
options: Pooling across channels
Upsampling feature maps to increase spatial resolution for tasks like image generation
Reducing feature map dimensions
Normalizing batch statistics
explanation: Transposed convolution upsamples feature maps, increasing spatial dimensions. It is used in generators, autoencoders, and segmentation networks.

text: What is knowledge distillation?
options: Training a smaller student model to mimic a larger teacher model's outputs
Compressing a dataset into fewer samples
Extracting knowledge from a database
Splitting a large model across multiple GPUs
explanation: Knowledge distillation trains a compact student model to reproduce the soft probability outputs of a larger teacher model, achieving compression with minimal accuracy loss.

text: Why are dilated (atrous) convolutions useful in deep learning?
options: They normalize feature maps
They replace max pooling layers
They increase the receptive field without increasing the number of parameters or reducing resolution
They reduce the number of parameters in the model
explanation: Dilated convolutions insert gaps between kernel elements, expanding the receptive field exponentially without adding parameters or losing spatial resolution.

text: What is the difference between a conditional GAN (cGAN) and a standard GAN?
options: cGAN has no discriminator
cGAN uses a different loss function
cGAN conditions both generator and discriminator on additional information like class labels
cGAN can only generate text
explanation: A conditional GAN provides additional information (e.g., class labels) to both the generator and discriminator, enabling controlled generation of specific outputs.

text: What is tokenization in NLP?
options: Breaking text into smaller units like words or subwords
Converting text into numerical vectors
Removing punctuation from text
Translating text between languages
explanation: Tokenization splits text into individual tokens (words, subwords, or characters), which is the first step in most NLP pipelines.

text: What is stemming?
options: Counting word frequency
Converting text to lowercase
Adding prefixes to words
Reducing words to their root or base form by removing suffixes
explanation: Stemming reduces words to their base form by chopping off suffixes (e.g., 'running' to 'run'), though it may produce non-dictionary words.

text: What does TF-IDF stand for?
options: Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency
Text Feature-Intelligent Data Format
Total Frequency-Internal Data File
Token Filter-Index Data Frame
explanation: TF-IDF stands for Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency, a statistic that reflects how important a word is to a document in a corpus.

text: What is the bag of words (BoW) model?
options: A representation that counts word occurrences in a document, ignoring word order
A technique for generating text
A deep learning model for text
A method for translating documents
explanation: Bag of words represents text as a vector of word counts or frequencies, disregarding grammar and word order.

text: What is sentiment analysis?
options: Determining the emotional tone or opinion expressed in text
Counting the number of sentences
Extracting named entities from text
Detecting the language of a text
explanation: Sentiment analysis identifies whether text expresses positive, negative, or neutral sentiment, commonly used for reviews and social media analysis.

text: What is lemmatization?
options: Reducing words to their dictionary form using vocabulary and morphological analysis
Converting text to binary format
Splitting sentences into individual characters
Removing all vowels from words
explanation: Lemmatization uses vocabulary and morphological analysis to return the base dictionary form of a word (e.g., 'better' to 'good').

text: What is Named Entity Recognition (NER)?
options: Removing named entities from text
Translating proper nouns between languages
Identifying and classifying named entities like persons, organizations, and locations in text
Classifying documents into categories
explanation: NER identifies and categorizes named entities in text into predefined classes such as person, organization, location, and date.

text: What is Word2Vec?
options: A rule-based text parser
A spell-checking algorithm
A technique that learns dense vector representations of words from large text corpora
A database for storing text documents
explanation: Word2Vec learns dense vector embeddings where semantically similar words have similar vector representations, using either CBOW or Skip-gram.

text: What are stop words in NLP?
options: Common words like 'the', 'is', 'at' that are often removed during preprocessing
Words that contain errors
Words that indicate the end of a sentence
Words used only in headlines
explanation: Stop words are frequently occurring words that carry little meaningful information and are often removed to reduce noise in NLP tasks.

text: What does BERT stand for?
options: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers
Batch Efficient Regression Technique
Binary Encoded Recurrent Transformer
Basic Entity Recognition Tool
explanation: BERT stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, a pre-trained language model that considers context from both directions.

text: What is the key difference between stemming and lemmatization?
options: Stemming always produces valid words
Lemmatization removes prefixes while stemming removes suffixes
Lemmatization considers the context and part of speech to return valid dictionary words, while stemming just chops suffixes
Stemming is slower than lemmatization
explanation: Lemmatization uses morphological analysis and dictionary lookup to return valid base forms, while stemming applies simple rules to strip suffixes.

text: How does TF-IDF differ from simple term frequency?
options: TF-IDF only works with bigrams
TF-IDF counts only rare words
TF-IDF weights terms higher if they are frequent in a document but rare across the corpus
TF-IDF ignores term frequency entirely
explanation: TF-IDF multiplies term frequency by inverse document frequency, giving higher scores to words that are distinctive to a document within the corpus.

text: What are the two training approaches used by Word2Vec?
options: Forward and backward pass
Supervised and unsupervised
Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW) and Skip-gram
Encoder and decoder
explanation: CBOW predicts a target word from surrounding context words, while Skip-gram predicts surrounding words from a target word.

text: What makes BERT different from previous NLP models like Word2Vec?
options: BERT generates context-dependent word embeddings, unlike Word2Vec's static embeddings
BERT can only process single sentences
BERT uses a fixed vocabulary
BERT uses a smaller model architecture
explanation: BERT produces different embeddings for the same word based on its context, while Word2Vec generates a single fixed vector per word regardless of context.

text: What is GloVe in the context of word embeddings?
options: A deep learning architecture for text generation
A text classification metric
A tokenization algorithm
Global Vectors for Word Representation, which learns embeddings from word co-occurrence statistics
explanation: GloVe (Global Vectors) trains word vectors by factorizing the word co-occurrence matrix, combining global statistics with local context windows.

text: What is the masked language model (MLM) pre-training objective used by BERT?
options: Removing masked tokens from the vocabulary
Randomly masking tokens in the input and training the model to predict them
Predicting the next word in a sequence
Translating masked words between languages
explanation: BERT's MLM randomly masks 15% of input tokens and trains the model to predict them, forcing bidirectional context understanding.

text: What is the GPT architecture's primary approach to language modeling?
options: Convolutional text processing
Bag of words with attention
Bidirectional encoding
Autoregressive left-to-right text generation using a decoder-only transformer
explanation: GPT uses a unidirectional (left-to-right) decoder-only transformer that generates text autoregressively, predicting the next token given previous tokens.

text: What is an n-gram in NLP?
options: A model with n parameters
A feature vector of dimension n
A neural network with n layers
A contiguous sequence of n items (words or characters) from a given text
explanation: An n-gram is a contiguous sequence of n items from text. Bigrams (n=2) and trigrams (n=3) capture local word patterns and co-occurrences.

text: What is word embedding dimensionality typically set to?
options: 50-300 dimensions
Equal to the vocabulary size
2-5 dimensions
1000-5000 dimensions
explanation: Word embeddings typically use 50-300 dimensions, balancing expressiveness and computational efficiency. Common choices are 100, 200, or 300.

text: What preprocessing step is essential before applying bag of words or TF-IDF?
options: Text cleaning including lowercasing, removing punctuation, and stop word removal
Image resizing
Data augmentation
Feature scaling
explanation: Text preprocessing normalizes the input by lowercasing, removing punctuation, and filtering stop words to improve the quality of BoW and TF-IDF representations.

text: What is the key difference between BERT's encoder-only architecture and GPT's decoder-only architecture?
options: BERT is larger than GPT
BERT can only process English text
BERT uses bidirectional context for understanding tasks; GPT uses unidirectional context for generation tasks
GPT uses convolutional layers while BERT uses attention
explanation: BERT sees both left and right context making it ideal for understanding tasks, while GPT only sees left context, making it suited for generation.

text: What is Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) used for in modern NLP models?
options: Subword tokenization that iteratively merges the most frequent character pairs to handle rare words
Encrypting text data
Compressing model weights
Encoding text as binary
explanation: BPE starts with characters and iteratively merges frequent pairs into subword tokens, enabling models to handle rare and out-of-vocabulary words effectively.

text: What is the 'attention is all you need' paper's key contribution?
options: Developing the bag of words model
Proposing the transformer architecture that replaces recurrence entirely with self-attention
Introducing RNNs for NLP
Creating the first word embeddings
explanation: The 2017 paper introduced the transformer, showing that self-attention alone (without recurrence or convolution) achieves state-of-the-art results in NLP.

text: What is the BLEU score used for in NLP?
options: Evaluating the quality of machine-translated text by comparing n-gram overlap with reference translations
Measuring word embedding quality
Measuring sentiment polarity
Ranking search results
explanation: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) compares n-gram precision between generated and reference translations, widely used to evaluate translation quality.

text: What is the difference between hard and soft attention mechanisms?
options: They are identical in implementation
Hard attention uses more memory
Hard attention selects a single input position (non-differentiable); soft attention computes a weighted average over all positions (differentiable)
Soft attention is less accurate
explanation: Soft attention uses differentiable weighted averages allowing backpropagation, while hard attention makes discrete selection decisions requiring reinforcement learning.

text: What problem does WordPiece tokenization solve compared to word-level tokenization?
options: It works only with Chinese text
It makes the vocabulary larger
It handles out-of-vocabulary words by breaking them into known subword pieces
It removes the need for embeddings
explanation: WordPiece splits unknown words into subword units from a fixed vocabulary, eliminating out-of-vocabulary issues while keeping the vocabulary manageable.

text: What is the perplexity metric in language modeling?
options: The number of unique words in a corpus
The ratio of training to test vocabulary
The exponentiated average negative log-likelihood of a sequence, measuring how well a model predicts text
The number of parameters in a language model
explanation: Perplexity measures how well a probability model predicts a sample. Lower perplexity indicates the model is less 'surprised' by the test data.

text: How does the Skip-gram model with negative sampling improve Word2Vec training efficiency?
options: By only updating weights for a small number of randomly sampled negative words instead of the full vocabulary
By using all words as negative samples
By removing the hidden layer
By using batch normalization
explanation: Negative sampling updates weights for only the target word and a few randomly chosen negative words, dramatically reducing computation compared to full softmax.

text: What is the Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) pre-training task in BERT?
options: Classifying sentences by topic
Generating the next sentence in a conversation
Predicting the sentiment of the next sentence
Training the model to predict whether two sentences appear consecutively in the original text
explanation: NSP trains BERT to predict whether sentence B actually follows sentence A, helping the model understand inter-sentence relationships for downstream tasks.

text: What is the ELMo model's key innovation over Word2Vec?
options: Using a smaller vocabulary
Training on labeled data only
Using convolutional layers for text
Generating context-dependent embeddings using bidirectional LSTMs trained on a language modeling objective
explanation: ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models) produces dynamic word representations that vary based on context, using deep bidirectional LSTM language models.

text: What is image classification?
options: Detecting the edges in an image
Splitting an image into segments
Assigning a label or category to an entire image
Increasing image resolution
explanation: Image classification assigns a single label to an entire image, identifying the main subject or category it belongs to.

text: What is the purpose of a convolutional layer in a CNN?
options: To normalize pixel values
To extract spatial features from the input by applying learned filters
To classify the final output
To reduce the image size
explanation: Convolutional layers apply learnable filters to the input, detecting spatial features like edges, textures, and patterns at various levels of abstraction.

text: What does pooling do in a CNN?
options: It reduces the spatial dimensions of feature maps while retaining important information
It applies activation functions
It increases the number of filters
It adds more features to the image
explanation: Pooling (e.g., max pooling) reduces feature map dimensions, decreasing computation and providing translation invariance.

text: What is object detection?
options: Compressing images for storage
Converting images to grayscale
Classifying an entire image into one category
Identifying and locating multiple objects within an image using bounding boxes
explanation: Object detection identifies what objects are in an image and where they are, typically using bounding boxes around detected objects.

text: What is data augmentation in computer vision?
options: Collecting more data from the internet
Removing duplicate images
Reducing image resolution
Applying transformations like rotation, flipping, and cropping to existing images to increase training data
explanation: Data augmentation artificially increases the training set by applying transformations to existing images, improving model generalization.

text: What is image segmentation?
options: Classifying each pixel of an image into a category
Cropping images to a fixed size
Resizing images for input to a model
Detecting edges in an image
explanation: Image segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel in an image, creating a detailed understanding of the scene at the pixel level.

text: What is YOLO in object detection?
options: A type of image filter
A data augmentation technique
A pooling method
You Only Look Once, a real-time object detection algorithm
explanation: YOLO (You Only Look Once) is a fast object detection model that processes the entire image in a single forward pass, enabling real-time detection.

text: Why is image preprocessing important before feeding images to a CNN?
options: It removes all color information
It standardizes input size, scales pixel values, and improves model training
It increases the number of classes
It is optional and has no effect
explanation: Preprocessing ensures consistent input dimensions, normalizes pixel values (e.g., 0-1 range), and can apply transformations that help the model learn better.

text: What does max pooling do?
options: Adds padding to the feature map
Selects the maximum value from each pooling region
Applies a learned filter to the input
Averages all values in a region
explanation: Max pooling takes the maximum value from each pooling window, retaining the most prominent features while reducing spatial dimensions.

text: What is the typical input format for images in a CNN?
options: A 1D vector of pixel values
A single scalar value
A 2D matrix of text
A 3D tensor with height, width, and color channels
explanation: Images are represented as 3D tensors with dimensions for height, width, and channels (e.g., 3 channels for RGB color images).

text: What is the difference between semantic segmentation and instance segmentation?
options: Instance segmentation only works on medical images
Semantic segmentation is more accurate
Semantic segmentation labels every pixel by class but doesn't distinguish between instances of the same class; instance segmentation does
They are the same technique
explanation: Semantic segmentation assigns class labels to pixels, while instance segmentation additionally distinguishes between separate objects of the same class.

text: How does R-CNN (Region-based CNN) approach object detection?
options: By processing the entire image at once
By using only fully connected layers
By applying edge detection filters
By generating region proposals, then classifying each proposed region with a CNN
explanation: R-CNN generates candidate region proposals using selective search, then classifies each region with a CNN to detect and classify objects.

text: What is the advantage of YOLO over R-CNN for object detection?
options: YOLO processes the image in a single pass, making it significantly faster for real-time applications
YOLO is always more accurate
YOLO uses fewer convolutional layers
YOLO works only on grayscale images
explanation: YOLO performs detection in a single forward pass through the network, achieving real-time speeds while R-CNN requires multiple passes for region proposals.

text: What is the role of stride in a convolutional layer?
options: It controls how many pixels the filter moves at each step, affecting the output feature map size
It sets the activation function
It defines the batch size
It determines the number of filters
explanation: Stride determines the step size of the filter as it slides across the input. A larger stride reduces the output spatial dimensions.

text: What does padding do in a convolutional layer?
options: Increases the number of filters
Adds extra pixels around the input border to control the output size
Applies a pooling operation
Removes edge pixels from the image
explanation: Padding adds pixels (usually zeros) around the input edges, allowing the output to maintain the same spatial dimensions as the input (same padding).

text: Which data augmentation technique horizontally mirrors an image?
options: Color jittering
Horizontal flipping
Rotation
Random erasing
explanation: Horizontal flipping creates a mirror image along the vertical axis, a simple and effective augmentation for many vision tasks.

text: What is the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric used for in object detection?
options: Evaluating the overlap between predicted and ground truth bounding boxes
Counting the number of detected objects
Measuring classification accuracy
Measuring inference speed
explanation: IoU measures the ratio of the overlap area to the union area of predicted and ground truth bounding boxes, used to evaluate detection quality.

text: What is global average pooling and when is it used?
options: Averaging all pixel values in the input image
Normalizing feature map averages
Applying max pooling globally
Computing the average of each feature map to produce a single value, often replacing fully connected layers before classification
explanation: Global average pooling averages each feature map to a single value, reducing parameters and overfitting compared to flattening and using fully connected layers.

text: Why is normalization of pixel values (e.g., dividing by 255) important for CNNs?
options: It removes noise from images
It converts color images to grayscale
It changes the image resolution
It scales pixel values to a 0-1 range, improving gradient flow and training stability
explanation: Normalizing pixel values to [0,1] ensures input values are on a similar scale, which improves gradient flow and speeds up convergence during training.

text: What is the purpose of a feature pyramid network (FPN) in object detection?
options: To apply data augmentation
To classify images into categories
To build multi-scale feature maps that help detect objects of varying sizes
To reduce the color channels of an image
explanation: FPN creates a pyramid of feature maps at different scales, combining low-resolution semantically strong features with high-resolution spatially precise features.

text: What is non-maximum suppression (NMS) used for in object detection?
options: Removing redundant overlapping bounding boxes by keeping only the highest-confidence detection
Normalizing bounding box coordinates
Maximizing the feature map resolution
Increasing the number of bounding boxes
explanation: NMS eliminates duplicate detections by keeping only the highest-confidence bounding box among those with significant overlap (high IoU).

text: How does the anchor box mechanism work in single-shot detectors like YOLO?
options: It pre-defines boxes of various sizes and aspect ratios at each grid cell, and the model predicts offsets from these anchors
It uses a single bounding box for all objects
It generates anchors using random sampling
It removes all bounding boxes during training
explanation: Anchor boxes are predefined bounding boxes of different sizes and aspect ratios. The model learns to predict adjustments to these anchors for precise detection.

text: What is the U-Net architecture commonly used for?
options: Biomedical image segmentation with an encoder-decoder structure and skip connections
Object detection in videos
Image generation using GANs
Text classification
explanation: U-Net features a symmetric encoder-decoder with skip connections that combine fine spatial details with semantic context, excelling at medical image segmentation.

text: What is the receptive field of a neuron in a CNN?
options: The number of parameters in the neuron
The output shape of the neuron
The learning rate used for that neuron
The region of the original input that influences the neuron's output
explanation: The receptive field is the area of the input image that contributes to a specific neuron's activation. Deeper layers have larger receptive fields.

text: What is the difference between Faster R-CNN and the original R-CNN?
options: Faster R-CNN uses a different loss function
Faster R-CNN uses more training data
Faster R-CNN eliminates convolutional layers
Faster R-CNN introduces a Region Proposal Network (RPN) that shares convolutional features, replacing slow selective search
explanation: Faster R-CNN replaces selective search with a Region Proposal Network that shares features with the detection network, dramatically improving speed.

text: What is the purpose of depthwise separable convolutions used in MobileNet?
options: To reduce computation by separating spatial and channel-wise convolutions
To replace pooling layers
To increase the receptive field
To increase model accuracy at the cost of speed
explanation: Depthwise separable convolutions split standard convolutions into depthwise (spatial) and pointwise (channel) operations, greatly reducing computation and parameters.

text: What is mean Average Precision (mAP) in object detection?
options: The average number of bounding boxes per image
The average pixel intensity of detected regions
The mean of average precision values across all object classes, measuring overall detection performance
The mean IoU across all predictions
explanation: mAP computes the average precision for each class at different IoU thresholds and averages them, providing a comprehensive detection performance metric.

text: How does CutMix data augmentation differ from standard image cropping?
options: CutMix converts images to grayscale
CutMix only crops the center of images
CutMix replaces a random patch of one image with a patch from another image, mixing both labels proportionally
CutMix removes random patches without replacement
explanation: CutMix cuts a region from one training image and pastes it onto another, mixing the labels proportionally to the area, improving generalization and localization.

text: What is the Dice coefficient used for in image segmentation?
options: Calculating the number of convolutional filters needed
Determining optimal image resolution
Measuring object detection speed
Evaluating the overlap between predicted and ground truth segmentation masks
explanation: The Dice coefficient measures the similarity between predicted and ground truth masks as 2*|intersection|/|sum of areas|, widely used in medical segmentation.

text: What is the Vision Transformer (ViT) approach to image classification?
options: Using standard CNNs with attention layers
Splitting an image into patches, treating them as tokens, and processing them with a standard transformer encoder
Using RNNs to process image rows sequentially
Applying GANs for feature extraction
explanation: ViT divides images into fixed-size patches, linearly embeds them, adds positional encodings, and processes them through a transformer encoder for classification.

text: What is Hadoop?
options: An open-source framework for distributed storage and processing of large datasets
A cloud computing service
A programming language
A type of database
explanation: Hadoop is an open-source framework that enables distributed storage (HDFS) and processing (MapReduce) of large datasets across clusters of computers.

text: What does MapReduce do?
options: A programming model that processes large datasets in parallel with map and reduce operations
Maps network connections between servers
Reduces the size of stored files
Creates maps of data locations
explanation: MapReduce processes data in two phases: Map transforms data into key-value pairs, and Reduce aggregates the results across all mapped outputs.

text: What is Apache Spark?
options: A web server
A fast, in-memory distributed computing engine for big data processing
A database management system
A visualization tool
explanation: Apache Spark is an in-memory distributed computing framework that processes big data much faster than MapReduce for iterative and interactive workloads.

text: What is ETL in data processing?
options: Evaluate, Test, Learn
Encrypt, Transfer, Log
Extract, Transform, Load — a process for moving data between systems
Extract, Train, Label
explanation: ETL stands for Extract, Transform, Load — extracting data from sources, transforming it into the desired format, and loading it into a target system.

text: What is a data lake?
options: A networking protocol
A small, structured database
A type of data visualization
A centralized repository that stores raw data in its native format at any scale
explanation: A data lake stores raw data in its original format (structured, semi-structured, or unstructured) at scale, allowing flexible future analysis.

text: What is the difference between batch processing and stream processing?
options: Stream processing is always slower
Batch processes data in large chunks at intervals; stream processes data continuously in real-time
They are the same thing
Batch processing only works on text data
explanation: Batch processing handles large volumes of data at scheduled intervals, while stream processing handles data continuously as it arrives in real-time.

text: What is distributed computing?
options: Running a program on a single powerful machine
Splitting a computation across multiple networked machines to work in parallel
Distributing software to users
A type of cloud storage
explanation: Distributed computing divides a task across multiple machines that communicate over a network, enabling processing of datasets too large for one machine.

text: What is HDFS?
options: Hadoop Distributed File System, a distributed storage system for large files
Hybrid Data Format Standard
High Definition File System
Hard Disk Formatting Service
explanation: HDFS is the storage component of Hadoop that distributes and replicates large files across multiple nodes for fault-tolerant storage.

text: Which of the following is a NoSQL database?
options: MongoDB
PostgreSQL
Oracle
MySQL
explanation: MongoDB is a popular NoSQL document database that stores data in flexible JSON-like documents, well-suited for big data applications.

text: What is a data pipeline?
options: An automated series of steps that moves and transforms data from source to destination
A physical cable connecting data centers
A type of database index
A network protocol
explanation: A data pipeline automates the flow of data through a sequence of processing steps from sources to destinations like data warehouses or ML models.

text: What advantage does Spark have over Hadoop MapReduce for iterative ML algorithms?
options: Spark keeps data in memory between iterations, avoiding costly disk I/O
Spark does not support distributed computing
Spark requires fewer nodes
Spark uses disk-based processing
explanation: Spark's in-memory processing is significantly faster for iterative algorithms like ML training that require multiple passes over the data.

text: What is Apache Kafka primarily used for?
options: Data visualization
Batch data processing
Static file storage
Distributed real-time event streaming and message processing
explanation: Kafka is a distributed streaming platform for building real-time data pipelines, handling high-throughput event streams with low latency.

text: What is the difference between a data lake and a data warehouse?
options: A data warehouse stores more data than a data lake
A data lake only stores text data
A data lake stores raw data in native format; a data warehouse stores structured, processed data for analysis
They are the same thing
explanation: Data lakes store raw, unprocessed data in any format (schema-on-read), while data warehouses store cleaned, structured data optimized for queries (schema-on-write).

text: What are the three V's of big data?
options: Vectors, Variance, Visibility
Value, Verification, Visualization
Version, Validity, Variables
Volume, Velocity, Variety
explanation: The three V's — Volume (scale of data), Velocity (speed of data flow), and Variety (types of data) — define the key characteristics of big data.

text: What is a Spark RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)?
options: A file format for big data
A random data distribution method
An immutable distributed collection of objects that can be processed in parallel
A type of database table
