A weighting scheme that prioritizes heavier models in an ensemble
A technique that encodes categories using the log of the ratio of positive to negative class distributions
An encoding that uses the inverse document frequency as weights
explanation: WoE encoding replaces categorical values with the natural log of the ratio of positive to negative class proportions, commonly used in credit scoring.

text: What is the difference between feature selection and feature extraction?
options: Extraction removes features while selection adds new ones
Selection always reduces dimensions more than extraction does
Selection chooses existing features while extraction creates new transformed features from raw data
Extraction can only be applied to image data while selection works on any type
explanation: Feature selection picks a subset of original features, while feature extraction creates new features by transforming or combining original ones.

text: What is a neuron in a neural network?
options: A physical brain cell used for computation
A computational unit that applies weights and an activation function to inputs
A data storage unit in the network memory
A connection cable between two network layers
explanation: An artificial neuron receives inputs, multiplies them by weights, sums them, adds a bias, and applies an activation function to produce output.

text: What is an activation function in a neural network?
options: A function that initializes the network weights
A function that measures the training loss
A function that selects which neurons to deactivate
A function that introduces non-linearity into the network output
explanation: An activation function introduces non-linearity, allowing neural networks to learn complex patterns beyond linear relationships.

text: What is the ReLU activation function?
options: f(x) = tanh(x)
f(x) = max(0, x)
explanation: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) outputs the input directly if positive, otherwise outputs zero: f(x) = max(0, x).

text: What is a hidden layer in a neural network?
options: A layer that is invisible during model inference
A layer that stores the training data
A layer between the input and output layers
A layer that only processes encrypted data
explanation: A hidden layer is any layer between the input and output layers of a neural network that performs intermediate computations.

text: What is backpropagation used for in neural networks?
options: Propagating data backwards through the network for inference
Sending predictions back to the user interface
Reversing the order of training data samples
Computing gradients of the loss function to update weights
explanation: Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight by applying the chain rule, enabling weight updates.

text: What is an epoch in neural network training?
options: A single forward pass through one data sample
The time taken to train one layer of the network
A specific milestone in model accuracy improvement
One complete pass through the entire training dataset
explanation: An epoch is one complete pass through the entire training dataset during the training process.

text: What is the purpose of a loss function in deep learning?
options: To measure data loss during network transmission
To quantify how well the model predictions match the actual values
To count the number of neurons in the network
To determine the optimal batch size for training
explanation: The loss function measures the difference between the model's predictions and the actual target values, guiding the optimization process.

text: What is gradient descent used for in deep learning?
options: Increasing the learning rate over time
Selecting the best activation function automatically
Generating training data for the network
Optimizing model weights by minimizing the loss function
explanation: Gradient descent iteratively adjusts model weights in the direction that minimizes the loss function.

text: What is a batch in the context of neural network training?
options: The entire training dataset processed at once
A collection of trained models stored for comparison
A group of hyperparameters tested simultaneously
A subset of training samples processed together in one iteration
explanation: A batch is a subset of training samples that are processed together in one forward and backward pass during training.

text: Which framework is commonly used for building deep learning models?
options: Django
Flask
TensorFlow
Express
explanation: TensorFlow is one of the most popular deep learning frameworks for building, training, and deploying neural network models.

text: What is the vanishing gradient problem?
options: When gradients become too large, causing numerical overflow
When the model runs out of memory during gradient computation
When gradients become extremely small, preventing effective weight updates in early layers
When gradients point in random directions during training
explanation: The vanishing gradient problem occurs when gradients become very small in early layers during backpropagation, slowing or stopping learning.

text: What is dropout regularization in neural networks?
options: Permanently removing neurons that have small weight values
Dropping out the last layer of the network during inference
Randomly deactivating neurons during training to prevent overfitting
Reducing the learning rate when training accuracy drops
explanation: Dropout randomly deactivates a fraction of neurons during training, forcing the network to learn redundant representations and reducing overfitting.

text: What is batch normalization used for?
options: Sorting training batches by their difficulty level
Normalizing layer inputs to stabilize and accelerate training
Adjusting the batch size dynamically during training
Converting batch data from float to integer format
explanation: Batch normalization normalizes the inputs to each layer by adjusting mean and variance, stabilizing training and allowing higher learning rates.

text: What is the difference between SGD and Adam optimizer?
options: Adam uses a fixed learning rate while SGD adapts rates per parameter
SGD can only be used for classification while Adam is for regression
SGD uses a fixed learning rate while Adam adapts learning rates per parameter
Adam requires more memory but SGD requires more computation time
explanation: SGD uses a fixed learning rate for all parameters, while Adam adapts learning rates individually using estimates of first and second moments of gradients.

text: What is a learning rate scheduler?
options: A tool for scheduling model training on specific hardware
A calendar for planning model development milestones
A mechanism that adjusts the learning rate during training
An algorithm for scheduling batch processing jobs
explanation: A learning rate scheduler adjusts the learning rate during training, such as reducing it when the loss plateaus, to improve convergence.

text: What is early stopping in neural network training?
options: Terminating training before the first epoch completes
Stopping the model from making predictions after a time limit
Removing early layers of the network to reduce complexity
Stopping training when validation performance stops improving to prevent overfitting
explanation: Early stopping monitors validation performance and stops training when it stops improving, preventing the model from overfitting to training data.

text: What is the purpose of weight initialization in neural networks?
options: To set all weights to zero for a clean starting point
To set initial weights that enable effective gradient flow during training
To permanently fix certain weights that should not change
To copy weights from a completely different neural network
explanation: Proper weight initialization sets starting values that prevent vanishing or exploding gradients, enabling effective training from the beginning.

text: What is the difference between stochastic and mini-batch gradient descent?
options: Mini-batch uses one sample while stochastic uses the full dataset
Stochastic is deterministic while mini-batch introduces randomness
Stochastic uses one sample per update while mini-batch uses a small group of samples
Mini-batch always converges faster than stochastic gradient descent
explanation: Stochastic gradient descent updates weights using one sample at a time, while mini-batch uses a small group, balancing speed and stability.

text: What is transfer learning in deep learning?
options: Transferring data between two different training servers
Using a pre-trained model's learned features for a new task
Moving a model from a GPU to a CPU for deployment
Copying the architecture of one network to create another
explanation: Transfer learning uses features learned by a model trained on a large dataset as a starting point for a model on a different but related task.

text: What is the exploding gradient problem?
options: When gradient values become very small during backpropagation
When the model generates too many gradient computations per batch
When gradient values grow exponentially large, causing unstable weight updates
When gradients are computed for features that should be ignored
explanation: Exploding gradients occur when gradient values grow exponentially during backpropagation, leading to extremely large weight updates and training instability.

text: What is the Xavier (Glorot) initialization method?
options: Initializing all weights to a constant value of one
Initializing weights from a distribution scaled by the number of input and output neurons
Setting weights to the eigenvalues of the input data matrix
Copying weights from the previous training run as initialization
explanation: Xavier initialization draws weights from a distribution with variance 2/(n_in + n_out), maintaining consistent gradient variance across layers.

text: What is the difference between layer normalization and batch normalization?
options: Batch normalization normalizes across features while layer normalization normalizes across samples
Layer normalization only works during inference while batch normalization only works during training
Batch normalization is always superior to layer normalization in all scenarios
Layer normalization normalizes across features for each sample while batch normalization normalizes across samples for each feature
explanation: Layer normalization normalizes across all features within a single sample, while batch normalization normalizes each feature across the batch dimension.

text: What is the purpose of residual connections (skip connections)?
options: Skipping the forward pass for certain layers to save computation
Connecting the output layer directly to the input for validation
Allowing gradients to flow directly through shortcut paths, enabling training of very deep networks
Removing redundant layers that do not contribute to learning
explanation: Residual connections add the input of a layer to its output, allowing gradients to bypass layers and enabling effective training of very deep networks.

text: What is the lottery ticket hypothesis?
options: That neural networks with random weights always outperform trained models
That the best model architecture is always found by random search
That dense networks contain sparse subnetworks that can match the full network's performance when trained in isolation
That larger batch sizes always lead to better model generalization
explanation: The lottery ticket hypothesis states that randomly initialized dense networks contain sparse subnetworks (winning tickets) that can achieve comparable accuracy.

text: What is knowledge distillation in deep learning?
options: Extracting human knowledge and encoding it as training data
Removing unnecessary knowledge from a trained model to reduce size
Distilling the most important features from raw input data
Training a smaller student model to mimic a larger teacher model's outputs
explanation: Knowledge distillation trains a smaller (student) model to replicate the behavior of a larger (teacher) model, often using soft probability outputs.

text: What is gradient clipping used for?
options: Removing gradients for parameters that have already converged
Clipping input data values to a specified range before training
Limiting gradient values to a maximum threshold to prevent exploding gradients
Reducing the number of gradient computation steps per batch
explanation: Gradient clipping limits gradient values to a maximum threshold during backpropagation, preventing the exploding gradient problem.

text: What is the difference between model parallelism and data parallelism?
options: Data parallelism splits the model across devices while model parallelism splits data
Data parallelism is only for CPUs while model parallelism is only for GPUs
Model parallelism splits the model across devices while data parallelism replicates the model on each device with different data
Model parallelism is always faster than data parallelism
explanation: Model parallelism distributes different parts of a model across devices, while data parallelism replicates the model on each device and splits the data.

text: What is neural architecture search (NAS)?
options: Using automated methods to discover optimal neural network architectures
Manually designing neural network architectures based on domain expertise
Searching through pre-trained models to find the best one
Navigating the file system to locate neural network configuration files
explanation: NAS uses automated methods like reinforcement learning or evolutionary algorithms to discover optimal neural network architectures for a given task.

text: What is mixed precision training?
options: Training with both labeled and unlabeled data simultaneously
Mixing different optimizers during the training process
Using both 16-bit and 32-bit floating-point numbers to speed up training while maintaining accuracy
Training the same model with different batch sizes alternately
explanation: Mixed precision training uses lower-precision (FP16) arithmetic for speed while maintaining critical computations in higher precision (FP32) for accuracy.

text: What is the purpose of the warmup phase in learning rate scheduling?
options: Pre-loading training data into memory before starting training
Running the model on validation data before the main training begins
Gradually increasing the learning rate from a small value to prevent early instability
Initializing the GPU memory caches for faster computation
explanation: Learning rate warmup gradually increases the learning rate from near zero, preventing large early gradient updates that could destabilize training.

text: What is a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) primarily used for?
options: Processing sequential text data
Processing grid-like data such as images
Generating random numbers for simulations
Managing database queries and transactions
explanation: CNNs are designed to process grid-like data such as images by using convolutional filters to detect spatial patterns.

text: What is a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) designed for?
options: Processing fixed-size image inputs
Clustering data points into groups
Processing sequential data with temporal dependencies
Reducing the dimensionality of feature spaces
explanation: RNNs are designed to process sequential data by maintaining a hidden state that captures information from previous time steps.

text: What is a convolutional filter (kernel)?
options: A function that filters out noisy data samples
A small matrix that slides over the input to detect features
A sorting algorithm for organizing pixel values
A type of activation function used in CNNs
explanation: A convolutional filter is a small matrix of weights that slides across the input data to detect specific features like edges or textures.

text: What is pooling in a CNN?
options: Combining multiple CNNs into one model
Adding more convolutional layers to the network
Sharing weights between different layers
Reducing the spatial dimensions of feature maps
explanation: Pooling reduces the spatial dimensions of feature maps by taking the maximum or average value within a window, reducing computation.

text: What is a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)?
options: A framework with a generator and discriminator competing against each other
A network that classifies images into adversarial categories
A network that detects adversarial attacks on other models
A method for generating training data from labeled examples
explanation: A GAN consists of a generator that creates fake data and a discriminator that distinguishes real from fake, training through competition.

text: What is an autoencoder?
options: A tool for automatically encoding videos into compressed formats
An encryption algorithm for securing neural network weights
A neural network that learns to compress and reconstruct its input
A software tool that automatically writes code for encoding tasks
explanation: An autoencoder is a neural network that learns to encode input into a lower-dimensional representation and then decode it back to the original form.

text: What is LSTM in the context of neural networks?
options: Long Short-Term Memory
Large Scale Training Method
Linear Sequential Training Module
Layered Symmetric Transfer Model
explanation: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) is a type of RNN architecture designed to learn long-term dependencies in sequential data.

text: What is max pooling?
options: Selecting the maximum learning rate for training
Using the maximum number of layers in a network
Training for the maximum number of epochs possible
Taking the maximum value from each window in a feature map
explanation: Max pooling selects the maximum value from each window (patch) in a feature map, retaining the most prominent features.

text: What does the stride parameter control in a convolutional layer?
options: The number of filters applied to the input
The depth of the output feature map
The activation function used after convolution
The step size of the filter as it moves across the input
explanation: Stride determines how many pixels the filter moves at each step when sliding across the input, affecting the output size.

text: What is the Transformer architecture primarily known for?
options: Converting electrical power between voltage levels
Transforming images from one style to another
Converting data between different file formats
Processing sequences using self-attention instead of recurrence
explanation: The Transformer architecture uses self-attention mechanisms to process sequences in parallel, replacing the sequential nature of RNNs.

text: How does the attention mechanism work in neural networks?
options: It randomly selects which layers to update during training
It monitors system attention to prevent server overload
It selects which training samples to focus on in each epoch
It computes weighted importance scores for different parts of the input
explanation: The attention mechanism computes weighted importance scores for different input elements, allowing the model to focus on the most relevant parts.

text: What are the three components of the LSTM cell?
options: Input layer, hidden layer, and output layer
Encoder, decoder, and attention module
Convolution layer, pooling layer, and dense layer
Forget gate, input gate, and output gate
explanation: LSTM cells use three gates: the forget gate (what to discard), input gate (what to store), and output gate (what to output).

text: What is the purpose of padding in convolutional layers?
options: To increase the training speed of the model
To add regularization to prevent overfitting
To increase the depth of the feature maps
To preserve spatial dimensions of the input after convolution
explanation: Padding adds zeros around the input borders to preserve spatial dimensions after convolution, preventing the output from shrinking.

text: What is the GRU architecture and how does it differ from LSTM?
options: GRU uses four gates while LSTM uses three gates
GRU is designed for image data while LSTM is for sequential data
GRU combines the forget and input gates into a single update gate, making it simpler than LSTM
GRU requires more parameters than LSTM for the same hidden size
explanation: GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) simplifies LSTM by combining the forget and input gates into an update gate and merging the cell and hidden states.

text: What is a variational autoencoder (VAE)?
options: An autoencoder that varies its architecture during training
An autoencoder that uses variable-length input sequences
An autoencoder that adjusts its learning rate automatically
An autoencoder that learns a probabilistic latent space for generating new samples
explanation: A VAE learns a probabilistic latent space by encoding inputs as distributions, enabling generation of new realistic samples by sampling from this space.

text: What is the encoder-decoder architecture used for?
options: Encrypting and decrypting data for secure transmission
Encoding categorical variables and decoding predictions
Compressing model weights for efficient storage
Mapping input sequences to output sequences of potentially different lengths
explanation: The encoder-decoder architecture maps variable-length input sequences to variable-length output sequences, used in tasks like translation.

text: What is depthwise separable convolution?
options: A factorized convolution that applies separate spatial and channel-wise filters to reduce computation
A convolution applied only to the deepest layers of the network
A convolution that varies its depth based on input complexity
A standard convolution applied to depth maps from stereo cameras
explanation: Depthwise separable convolution factorizes a standard convolution into a depthwise spatial convolution and a pointwise 1x1 convolution, greatly reducing computation.

text: What is the mode collapse problem in GANs?
options: When the GAN model file becomes corrupted during training
When the discriminator perfectly classifies all generated samples
When the training loss increases instead of decreasing
When the generator produces only a limited variety of outputs
explanation: Mode collapse occurs when the generator learns to produce only a small variety of outputs instead of capturing the full diversity of the data distribution.

text: What is multi-head attention in the Transformer architecture?
options: Applying attention to multiple datasets simultaneously
Using multiple loss functions to train the attention mechanism
Running multiple attention operations in parallel and concatenating their outputs
Stacking multiple Transformer layers on top of each other
explanation: Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel with different learned projections, allowing the model to attend to information from different subspaces.

text: What is the purpose of 1x1 convolutions in deep networks?
options: They have no practical purpose and are never used
They always double the spatial resolution of feature maps
They change the number of channels without altering spatial dimensions
They replace all other convolution sizes in modern architectures
explanation: 1x1 convolutions change the number of channels (depth) of feature maps and can add non-linearity, used in architectures like Inception and ResNet.

text: What is the self-attention mechanism's computational complexity with respect to sequence length?
options: O(n) where n is the sequence length
O(n log n) where n is the sequence length
O(1) constant regardless of sequence length
O(n^2) where n is the sequence length
explanation: Self-attention has O(n^2) complexity because every position attends to every other position, making it expensive for very long sequences.

text: What is the reparameterization trick in variational autoencoders?
options: Expressing the random sampling as a deterministic function of learnable parameters and noise
Replacing model parameters with their reciprocals during training
Reparameterizing the loss function to use a different mathematical form
Changing the network architecture dynamically during training
explanation: The reparameterization trick enables backpropagation through the sampling step by expressing samples as z = μ + σ * ε, where ε is random noise.

text: What is the Wasserstein distance used for in WGANs?
options: Measuring the physical distance between GPU compute nodes
Computing the distance between training and test data distributions
Providing a smoother loss metric that correlates with generated sample quality
Measuring the difference between encoder and decoder outputs
explanation: The Wasserstein distance provides a smoother loss signal than the original GAN loss, correlating better with sample quality and improving training stability.

text: What is dilated (atrous) convolution used for?
options: Reducing the number of parameters in convolutional layers
Diluting the strength of convolution filters to prevent overfitting
Converting 2D convolutions into 3D volumetric operations
Increasing the receptive field without increasing parameters or reducing resolution
explanation: Dilated convolution inserts gaps between filter elements, expanding the receptive field exponentially without increasing parameters or losing resolution.

text: What is the difference between transposed convolution and upsampling followed by convolution?
options: Upsampling followed by convolution is always more computationally expensive
Transposed convolution can only double the spatial dimensions
Simple upsampling produces higher quality results in all cases
Transposed convolution learns upsampling parameters while simple upsampling uses fixed interpolation
explanation: Transposed convolution has learnable parameters for upsampling but can produce checkerboard artifacts, while upsampling+convolution uses fixed interpolation followed by learned filtering.

text: What are positional encodings in Transformers?
options: Encodings that store the physical position of GPUs in a data center
Coordinates that locate the position of attention heads in the architecture
Signals added to input embeddings to provide sequence position information
Encodings that determine the position of layers in the network stack
explanation: Positional encodings inject sequence order information into the input, since the self-attention mechanism is inherently order-invariant.

text: What is a diffusion model in generative AI?
options: A model that simulates the diffusion of heat in physical systems
A model that spreads information through a social network graph
A model that slowly transfers knowledge between different tasks
A model that gradually adds noise to data then learns to reverse the process for generation
explanation: Diffusion models learn to generate data by training to reverse a gradual noising process, progressively denoising random noise into realistic samples.

text: What is the purpose of group normalization?
options: Normalizing data by grouping similar features together permanently
Grouping training samples by their labels before normalization
Dividing channels into groups and normalizing within each group, independent of batch size
Normalizing the output of grouped convolution operations only
explanation: Group normalization divides channels into groups and normalizes within each group, making it independent of batch size and effective for small batches.

text: What is the concept of neural network pruning?
options: Adding new neurons to improve model capacity
Removing redundant weights or neurons to create a smaller, faster model
Training the network on a pruned subset of the training data
Cutting the learning rate in half at regular intervals
explanation: Neural network pruning removes weights or neurons with small magnitudes or low importance, creating a smaller model with minimal accuracy loss.

text: What is contrastive learning in self-supervised deep learning?
options: Learning representations by pulling similar samples together and pushing dissimilar ones apart
Training a model to classify contrasting image pairs
Comparing the performance of two competing model architectures
Training with both positive and negative learning rates
explanation: Contrastive learning trains representations by bringing augmented views of the same sample closer while pushing different samples apart in embedding space.

text: What is tokenization in NLP?
options: Encrypting text for secure communication
Converting audio to text format
Breaking text into smaller units like words or subwords
Assigning security tokens to user accounts
explanation: Tokenization is the process of breaking text into smaller units called tokens, such as words, subwords, or characters.

text: What is stemming in text processing?
options: Adding prefixes to words to change their meaning
Translating words from one language to another
Counting the number of syllables in each word
Finding the root form of a word by removing suffixes
explanation: Stemming reduces words to their root form by removing suffixes, such as 'running' to 'run'.

text: What are stop words in NLP?
options: Words that signal the end of a sentence
Words that are misspelled in the text
Technical jargon that is difficult to process
Common words like 'the', 'is', 'at' that are often removed during preprocessing
explanation: Stop words are commonly used words like 'the', 'is', and 'at' that are often removed because they carry little meaningful information.

text: What is sentiment analysis?
options: Analyzing the grammatical structure of sentences
Counting the number of words in a document
Translating text between two languages
Determining the emotional tone or opinion expressed in text
explanation: Sentiment analysis determines whether text expresses positive, negative, or neutral emotions or opinions.

text: What is Named Entity Recognition (NER)?
options: Identifying and classifying entities like names, dates, and locations in text
Recognizing named files in a directory system
Creating new names for unnamed variables in code
Registering domain names for web entities
explanation: NER identifies and classifies named entities in text into categories like person names, organizations, locations, and dates.

text: What is lemmatization?
options: Splitting compound words into separate components
Adding grammatical markers to word endings
Translating technical terms into plain language
Converting words to their dictionary base form using vocabulary analysis
explanation: Lemmatization reduces words to their base dictionary form (lemma) using vocabulary and morphological analysis, such as 'better' to 'good'.

text: What is a corpus in NLP?
options: A large collection of text documents used for training or analysis
A single sentence used for testing
A specialized NLP programming language
A type of neural network for text processing
explanation: A corpus is a large, structured collection of text documents used for training language models or conducting linguistic analysis.

text: What is text classification?
options: Sorting text files by their file size
Classifying programming languages by their syntax
Assigning predefined categories to text documents
Organizing text by font style and formatting
explanation: Text classification assigns predefined categories or labels to text documents, such as spam detection or topic categorization.

text: What is Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging?
options: Tagging images with descriptive text labels
Creating tags for version control in software
Assigning priority tags to email messages
Labeling each word in a sentence with its grammatical role
explanation: POS tagging labels each word in a sentence with its grammatical category such as noun, verb, adjective, or adverb.

text: What is a language model?
options: A database of all words in a language
A tool for translating between programming languages
A model that predicts the probability of word sequences
A diagram showing the structure of a language
explanation: A language model estimates the probability of sequences of words, used for text generation, completion, and many NLP tasks.

text: What is the difference between stemming and lemmatization?
options: Stemming uses dictionary lookup while lemmatization uses rule-based suffix stripping
Stemming uses crude rule-based suffix removal while lemmatization uses vocabulary and morphological analysis
Lemmatization is always faster than stemming for large text corpora
Stemming produces real dictionary words while lemmatization may not
explanation: Stemming uses simple rules to strip suffixes (which may produce non-words), while lemmatization uses linguistic knowledge to return valid dictionary forms.

text: What are word embeddings?
options: Physical embeddings of text into printed documents
One-hot encoded vectors representing each unique word
Dense vector representations of words that capture semantic relationships
Binary representations of ASCII character codes
explanation: Word embeddings are dense, low-dimensional vector representations where semantically similar words are mapped to nearby points in vector space.

text: What is the Word2Vec model?
options: A model that converts words to their phonetic representation
A tool that converts speech recordings to text transcriptions
A dictionary that maps words to their translation in other languages
A neural network model that learns word embeddings from text context
explanation: Word2Vec learns word embeddings by training a neural network to predict context words (Skip-gram) or a target word from context (CBOW).

text: What is the purpose of an attention mechanism in sequence-to-sequence models?
options: To allow the decoder to focus on relevant parts of the input sequence
To limit the model's attention span to reduce computation
To monitor user attention during model inference
To select which training samples deserve more attention
explanation: Attention allows the decoder to dynamically focus on different parts of the input sequence for each output step, improving translation quality.

text: What is the BERT model designed for?
options: Generating realistic images from text descriptions
Translating text between exactly two languages
Detecting objects in images using text prompts
Bidirectional pre-training of language representations for various NLP tasks
explanation: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) pre-trains bidirectional language representations that can be fine-tuned for various NLP tasks.

text: What is the difference between n-gram models and neural language models?
options: Neural models can only handle bigrams while n-gram models handle any n
N-gram models always produce better results than neural language models
Neural language models require less training data than n-gram models
N-gram models use fixed context windows with count-based probabilities while neural models learn distributed representations
explanation: N-gram models estimate probabilities from word count statistics within fixed windows, while neural models learn continuous representations capturing longer-range patterns.

text: What is text summarization?
options: Increasing the length of text by adding more details
Converting text from one language to another language
Correcting spelling and grammar errors in text
Condensing a longer text into a shorter version while preserving key information
explanation: Text summarization creates a shorter version of text that retains the most important information, either extractively or abstractively.

text: What is the cosine similarity used for in NLP?
options: Computing the grammatical correctness of a sentence
Calculating the word count of a document
Determining the reading level of a text passage
Measuring the similarity between two text vectors based on their angle
explanation: Cosine similarity measures the similarity between two vectors by computing the cosine of the angle between them, commonly used to compare text embeddings.

text: What is machine translation?
options: Translating machine code into human-readable programming code
Converting spoken language into written text format
Automatically converting text from one natural language to another
Translating technical documentation into simple language
explanation: Machine translation automatically translates text from one natural language to another, such as English to French.

text: What is the BLEU score used to evaluate?
options: The accuracy of sentiment analysis models
The speed of text processing algorithms
The vocabulary size of a language model
The quality of machine-generated text compared to reference translations
explanation: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) measures the quality of machine-translated text by comparing n-gram overlap with reference translations.

text: What is the masked language model objective used in BERT?
options: Randomly masking input tokens and training the model to predict them from context
Masking the output layer to prevent overfitting during training
Hiding the model architecture from unauthorized users
Removing sensitive words from the training corpus
explanation: BERT's masked language model randomly masks 15% of input tokens and trains the model to predict them using bidirectional context.

text: What is byte-pair encoding (BPE) tokenization?
options: A subword tokenization method that iteratively merges the most frequent character pairs
Encoding text as raw bytes without any tokenization
Encoding each word as a pair of binary numbers
A method that tokenizes text into exactly two tokens per word
explanation: BPE starts with characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pairs to build a vocabulary of subword units.

text: What is the difference between extractive and abstractive summarization?
options: Extractive selects existing sentences while abstractive generates new sentences that may not appear in the source
Abstractive selects existing sentences while extractive generates new ones
Extractive works only on English text while abstractive is multilingual
Abstractive always produces shorter summaries than extractive methods
explanation: Extractive summarization selects and combines existing sentences from the source, while abstractive summarization generates new text that captures the key ideas.

text: What is the perplexity metric used for in language models?
options: Measuring the complexity of the model architecture
Calculating the confusion between similar word embeddings
Measuring the vocabulary diversity of generated text
Evaluating how well a language model predicts a sequence, with lower values indicating better performance
explanation: Perplexity measures how well a language model predicts a test set; lower perplexity indicates the model assigns higher probability to the actual sequences.

text: What is the GloVe word embedding method based on?
options: Training a recurrent neural network on word sequences
Using convolutional filters to extract word features
Clustering words based on their phonetic similarity
Factorizing the global word co-occurrence statistics matrix
explanation: GloVe (Global Vectors) learns word embeddings by factorizing the log of the global word-word co-occurrence matrix from a corpus.

text: What is the problem of out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words and how do subword models address it?
options: OOV words are too long to fit in memory; subword models truncate them
OOV words have no learned representation; subword models decompose them into known subword units
OOV words are always misspelled; subword models correct the spelling
OOV words are in foreign languages; subword models translate them
explanation: OOV words have no learned representation in word-level models; subword tokenization breaks unknown words into known subword units, handling any word.

text: What is the difference between autoregressive and autoencoding language models?
options: Autoencoding models generate text left-to-right while autoregressive models use bidirectional context
Autoregressive models can only process single sentences while autoencoding handles documents
Autoencoding models are always larger than autoregressive models
Autoregressive models predict tokens left-to-right while autoencoding models use bidirectional context with masked tokens
explanation: Autoregressive models like GPT predict the next token given previous tokens, while autoencoding models like BERT predict masked tokens using full bidirectional context.

text: What is the ROUGE metric used to evaluate?
options: The fluency and readability of generated text
The accuracy of named entity recognition systems
The speed of text generation in words per second
The overlap between generated summaries and reference summaries using recall-based measures
explanation: ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) measures the overlap of n-grams between generated and reference summaries, focusing on recall.

text: What is cross-lingual transfer learning in NLP?
options: Training separate models for each language independently
Converting text from one writing system to another
Using knowledge learned from one language to improve performance on tasks in another language
Comparing the grammar rules of two different languages
explanation: Cross-lingual transfer learning leverages multilingual models or shared representations to transfer knowledge from resource-rich to resource-poor languages.

text: What is the difference between contextual and static word embeddings?
options: Contextual embeddings are fixed while static embeddings change per sentence
Static embeddings require more training data than contextual embeddings
Contextual embeddings can only be used for classification tasks
Static embeddings generate a fixed vector per word regardless of context while contextual embeddings vary based on surrounding words
explanation: Static embeddings like Word2Vec assign one fixed vector per word, while contextual embeddings like BERT generate different vectors depending on the surrounding context.

text: What is computer vision?
options: A field focused on designing computer monitors
A technique for improving human eyesight with technology
A field that enables computers to interpret and understand visual data
A method for optimizing screen resolution settings
explanation: Computer vision is a field of AI that enables computers to interpret, analyze, and understand visual information from images and videos.

text: What is image classification?
options: Organizing image files into folders on a computer
Sorting images by their file size on disk
Converting images from one format to another
Assigning a label or category to an entire image
explanation: Image classification assigns a category label to an entire image, such as identifying whether an image contains a cat or dog.

text: What is object detection in computer vision?
options: Detecting broken objects in manufacturing photos
Identifying and locating objects within an image with bounding boxes
Finding specific files on a computer using image search
Detecting the size of objects using a ruler tool
explanation: Object detection identifies what objects are present in an image and where they are located, typically using bounding boxes.

text: What is a pixel?
options: A program that creates visual effects
The smallest unit of a digital image
A type of image compression algorithm
A measurement of screen brightness
explanation: A pixel is the smallest addressable element of a digital image, representing a single point of color.

text: What does RGB stand for in image processing?
options: Rapid Graphics Buffer
Red Green Blue
Raster Grid Binary
Resolution Grade Baseline
explanation: RGB stands for Red, Green, Blue, the three color channels used to represent colors in digital images.

text: What is image segmentation?
options: Cutting an image into equal-sized rectangular pieces
Dividing an image file into smaller file sizes
Splitting an image into its individual color channels
Partitioning an image into meaningful regions or segments
explanation: Image segmentation partitions an image into meaningful regions, assigning each pixel to a category or object.

text: What is data augmentation in computer vision?
options: Collecting additional images from the internet
Increasing the resolution of all training images
Applying transformations like rotation and flipping to increase training data variety
Adding metadata tags to each image file
explanation: Data augmentation applies transformations such as rotation, flipping, and cropping to existing images to increase training data diversity.

text: What is a grayscale image?
options: An image with only red and blue color channels
An image where each pixel has a single intensity value from black to white
An image that has been compressed to reduce file size
An image with very low contrast between colors
explanation: A grayscale image uses a single channel where each pixel has an intensity value ranging from black (0) to white (255).

text: Which library is commonly used for image processing in Python?
options: NumPy
Pandas
Flask
OpenCV
explanation: OpenCV (Open Source Computer Vision Library) is the most widely used library for image processing and computer vision tasks in Python.

text: What is facial recognition technology used for?
options: Measuring the speed of a processor
Creating 3D models of buildings
Compressing image files for storage
Identifying or verifying people from their facial features
explanation: Facial recognition technology identifies or verifies individuals by analyzing facial features from images or video frames.

text: What is the difference between semantic and instance segmentation?
options: Instance segmentation labels pixels by class while semantic segmentation counts objects
Semantic segmentation labels each pixel by class but does not distinguish between instances of the same class, while instance segmentation does
Semantic segmentation works only on grayscale images while instance works on color
Instance segmentation requires less computational resources than semantic segmentation
explanation: Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to each pixel, while instance segmentation additionally distinguishes between different instances of the same class.

text: What is transfer learning commonly used for in computer vision?
options: Transferring images between different storage devices
Converting grayscale images to color images automatically
Transferring pixel values from one image to another
Using a model pre-trained on ImageNet as a starting point for a new visual task
explanation: Transfer learning in computer vision uses models pre-trained on large datasets like ImageNet and fine-tunes them for specific tasks with less data.

text: What is the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric used for?
options: Measuring the overlap between two color histograms
Computing the intersection of two database query results
Evaluating the accuracy of bounding box predictions in object detection
Measuring the similarity between two text documents
explanation: IoU measures the overlap between the predicted bounding box and the ground truth box, calculated as the area of intersection divided by the area of union.

text: What are anchor boxes in object detection?
options: Fixed points used to align images during preprocessing
Predefined bounding boxes of various sizes and aspect ratios used as reference templates
The final output boxes after non-maximum suppression
Boxes drawn by human annotators around objects in training images
explanation: Anchor boxes are predefined bounding boxes of various sizes and aspect ratios that serve as reference templates for predicting object locations.

text: What is non-maximum suppression (NMS) used for?
options: Suppressing the maximum learning rate during training
Reducing the maximum pixel intensity in bright images
Preventing the maximum number of filters from being applied
Removing overlapping duplicate bounding box predictions for the same object
explanation: NMS removes redundant overlapping bounding box predictions by keeping only the highest-confidence box for each detected object.

text: What is the purpose of feature pyramid networks (FPN)?
options: Creating pyramid-shaped neural network architectures
Stacking images in a pyramid arrangement for batch processing
Reducing the feature dimensions in a pyramidal fashion
Building multi-scale feature maps for detecting objects at different sizes
explanation: FPN creates multi-scale feature maps by combining low-resolution semantically strong features with high-resolution features, improving detection of objects at different sizes.

text: What is image augmentation technique of random erasing?
options: Permanently deleting random images from the training set
Erasing the background of images to focus on foreground objects
Randomly masking rectangular regions of images during training
Removing random color channels from training images
explanation: Random erasing randomly masks rectangular regions of training images with random values, acting as a regularization technique to reduce overfitting.

text: What is optical character recognition (OCR)?
options: A method for optimizing the rendering of characters on screen
Creating optical illusions using computer-generated characters
Recognizing characters in a programming language compiler
Converting images of text into machine-readable text format
explanation: OCR converts images of typed, handwritten, or printed text into machine-readable text format.

text: What is the YOLO architecture known for in object detection?
options: Detecting objects in a single forward pass through the network making it very fast
Processing images one region at a time for maximum accuracy
Using only fully connected layers for object detection
Requiring separate models for each object category
explanation: YOLO (You Only Look Once) processes the entire image in a single forward pass, making it one of the fastest real-time object detection architectures.

text: What is the purpose of batch normalization in CNN architectures?
options: Ensuring all training batches have the same number of images
Sorting images in each batch by their resolution size
Converting all images to the same color space before processing
Normalizing intermediate feature maps to stabilize and speed up training
explanation: Batch normalization normalizes intermediate feature map activations, stabilizing training, enabling higher learning rates, and reducing sensitivity to initialization.

text: What is the difference between one-stage and two-stage object detectors?
options: Two-stage detectors are always faster than one-stage detectors
One-stage detectors can only detect one object per image
One-stage detectors directly predict boxes and classes in one pass while two-stage detectors first propose regions then classify them
Two-stage detectors use only convolutional layers while one-stage uses fully connected
explanation: One-stage detectors like YOLO predict directly, while two-stage detectors like Faster R-CNN first generate region proposals then classify them.

text: What is the panoptic segmentation task?
options: Segmenting panoramic 360-degree images into regions
Segmenting images captured from multiple camera angles simultaneously
Unifying semantic and instance segmentation to label every pixel with both a class and instance ID
Creating panoramic image stitches from multiple overlapping photos
explanation: Panoptic segmentation unifies semantic segmentation (for stuff like sky, road) and instance segmentation (for things like cars, people) into a single task.

text: What is the Region Proposal Network (RPN) in Faster R-CNN?
options: A separate network that classifies each proposed region
A network that reduces the resolution of input images
A network that generates candidate object locations for the detection pipeline
A network that performs non-maximum suppression on detected objects
explanation: The RPN slides over feature maps to generate candidate object regions with objectness scores, shared with the detection network for efficiency.

text: What is the concept of receptive field in CNNs?
options: The amount of GPU memory available for storing feature maps
The region of the input image that influences a particular feature map neuron
The total number of parameters in the convolutional network
The field of view of the camera that captured the training images
explanation: The receptive field is the region of the input image that affects the activation of a particular neuron in a feature map, growing with network depth.

text: What is the focal loss function designed to address?
options: The class imbalance between foreground and background in object detection
The problem of vanishing gradients in very deep networks
The difficulty of focusing cameras on moving objects
The computational cost of processing high-resolution images
explanation: Focal loss down-weights the contribution of easy examples and focuses training on hard, misclassified examples, addressing foreground-background class imbalance.

text: What is the Vision Transformer (ViT) approach?
options: Using traditional convolutional layers with transformer-style training
Applying transformer attention only to the final classification layer
Converting images to text descriptions before applying a language Transformer
Splitting images into patches and processing them as sequences with a Transformer architecture
explanation: ViT divides images into fixed-size patches, treats them as sequence tokens, and processes them with a standard Transformer encoder for classification.

text: What is style transfer in computer vision?
options: Transferring images between different storage devices
Converting image files from one format style to another
Transferring trained model weights between different tasks
Applying the artistic style of one image to the content of another using neural networks
explanation: Neural style transfer uses CNNs to separate and recombine the content of one image with the artistic style of another.

text: What is the mean Average Precision (mAP) metric in object detection?
options: The average pixel intensity across all detected objects
The average processing time per image frame during detection
The mean accuracy of the classification head across all epochs
The mean of average precision values computed for each object class at various IoU thresholds
explanation: mAP averages the AP (area under precision-recall curve) across all object classes and IoU thresholds, providing a comprehensive detection quality metric.

text: What is the deformable convolution operation?
options: A convolution with learnable offsets that allow the sampling grid to adapt to object shapes
A convolution that changes its filter weights based on the input
A convolution applied to elastically deformed images for augmentation
A convolution that deforms the output feature map to match a target shape
explanation: Deformable convolution adds learnable spatial offsets to the regular sampling grid, allowing the convolution to adapt to geometric variations in objects.

text: What is contrastive language-image pre-training (CLIP)?
options: A method for clipping extreme pixel values in image preprocessing
A technique for trimming video clips to relevant segments
A tool for comparing the language used in different image captions
A model that learns visual representations from natural language descriptions through contrastive learning
explanation: CLIP learns to associate images with text descriptions through contrastive learning, enabling zero-shot visual classification using natural language.

text: What are the three V's traditionally associated with big data?
options: Value, Variety, Visibility
Version, Validation, Verification
Virtual, Visual, Verbal
Volume, Velocity, Variety
explanation: The three V's of big data are Volume (large amounts), Velocity (high speed of data flow), and Variety (different data types).

text: What is Apache Hadoop?
options: A web application framework for building APIs
A programming language designed for data visualization
An open-source framework for distributed storage and processing of large datasets
A cloud-based email service platform
explanation: Apache Hadoop is an open-source framework that enables distributed storage and processing of large datasets across clusters of computers.

text: What is Apache Spark?
options: A unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing
A database management system for small datasets
A tool for creating interactive web dashboards
A programming language for embedded systems
explanation: Apache Spark is a unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing that provides in-memory computing for fast big data analysis.

text: What is the HDFS in Hadoop?
options: High Definition File Standard
Hardware Data Flow Scheduler
Hierarchical Database Format System
Hadoop Distributed File System
explanation: HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) is a distributed file system designed to store large files across multiple machines reliably.

text: What is MapReduce?
options: A geographic mapping tool for data visualization
A technique for reducing the size of map data files
A programming model for processing large datasets in parallel across a cluster
A method for creating reduced-size maps from satellite images
explanation: MapReduce is a programming model that processes large datasets in parallel by mapping data to key-value pairs and reducing them to aggregate results.

text: What is a data lake?
options: A centralized repository that stores raw data in its native format
A small database for temporary data storage
A visualization tool for displaying data as water-themed charts
A backup system that stores data in encrypted archives
explanation: A data lake is a centralized storage repository that holds raw data in its native format until it is needed for analysis.

text: What is batch processing in big data?
options: Processing data in small batches of exactly 100 records
Dividing a single file into batches for parallel download
Processing large volumes of data at scheduled intervals rather than in real time
Processing only the most recent batch of data and discarding older data
explanation: Batch processing handles large volumes of accumulated data at scheduled intervals, as opposed to real-time stream processing.

text: What is stream processing?
options: Processing data continuously as it arrives in real time
Processing video streaming content for entertainment
Converting audio streams into text transcriptions
Streaming processed results to a display monitor
explanation: Stream processing handles data continuously as it arrives, enabling real-time analytics and immediate insights.

text: What is a data warehouse?
options: A physical building where servers are stored
A temporary cache for frequently accessed web pages
A backup facility located in a separate geographic region
A central repository of structured, cleaned data optimized for querying and analysis
explanation: A data warehouse is a central repository of structured, integrated data from multiple sources, optimized for analytical queries and reporting.

text: What is NoSQL?
options: A programming language that replaces SQL entirely
A category of databases that do not exclusively use relational table structures
A tool for optimizing SQL query performance
A SQL extension for handling very large numbers
explanation: NoSQL databases do not rely exclusively on relational tables, supporting flexible schemas like document, key-value, graph, and column-family stores.

text: What is the difference between a data lake and a data warehouse?
options: A data warehouse stores raw data while a data lake stores only processed data
A data lake stores raw unprocessed data while a data warehouse stores structured, processed data
A data lake can only store text files while a data warehouse handles all formats
A data warehouse is always cloud-based while a data lake is always on-premises
explanation: Data lakes store raw data in its native format for flexible future use, while data warehouses store structured, cleaned data optimized for specific queries.

text: What is Apache Kafka used for?
options: Training machine learning models on distributed clusters
A visualization tool for big data analytics dashboards
A programming language for big data applications
A distributed event streaming platform for real-time data pipelines
explanation: Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform used for building real-time data pipelines and streaming applications.

text: What is the CAP theorem in distributed systems?
options: A theorem about the maximum capacity of database storage
A theorem stating a distributed system can guarantee at most two of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
A theorem about the computational accuracy of parallel algorithms
A theorem that defines the maximum number of concurrent users
explanation: The CAP theorem states that a distributed system can guarantee at most two of three properties: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance.

text: What is data partitioning (sharding) in distributed databases?
options: Creating backup copies of all data on each server node
Splitting data across multiple servers so each handles a subset
Encrypting different parts of the data with different keys
Compressing data partitions to reduce storage requirements
explanation: Data partitioning distributes data across multiple servers, with each server handling a subset, enabling horizontal scaling and parallel processing.

text: What is the advantage of in-memory computing in Spark over Hadoop MapReduce?
options: It requires less memory than disk-based processing
It can only process smaller datasets more efficiently
It keeps intermediate results in RAM, avoiding slow disk reads and writes
It eliminates the need for data serialization entirely
explanation: Spark's in-memory computing keeps intermediate results in RAM rather than writing to disk, making iterative algorithms and interactive queries much faster.

text: What is an ETL pipeline?
options: A network cable used for connecting data center servers
A process that Extracts, Transforms, and Loads data from sources to destinations
A testing framework for evaluating model performance
A logging system that tracks errors in data applications
explanation: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines extract data from sources, transform it into a suitable format, and load it into a destination system.

text: What is data replication in distributed systems?
options: Duplicating the code of data processing applications
Repeating data analysis experiments for validation
Creating synthetic copies of data for augmentation
Storing copies of data on multiple nodes for fault tolerance and availability
explanation: Data replication stores copies of data on multiple nodes, ensuring availability if one node fails and enabling read scaling.

text: What is Apache Hive used for?
options: Real-time stream processing of sensor data
Querying and managing large datasets using SQL-like syntax on Hadoop
Training deep learning models on distributed clusters
Monitoring server health in a data center
explanation: Apache Hive provides a SQL-like interface (HiveQL) for querying and managing large datasets stored in Hadoop's distributed storage.

text: What is the concept of horizontal scaling?
options: Increasing the processing power of a single machine
Widening the display resolution of monitoring dashboards
Expanding the number of columns in a database table
Adding more machines to a system to handle increased load
explanation: Horizontal scaling adds more machines to distribute the workload, as opposed to vertical scaling which increases the capacity of a single machine.

text: What is a Spark DataFrame?
options: A physical frame that holds server hardware
A data visualization frame for displaying charts
A single row of data in a Spark cluster
A distributed collection of data organized into named columns, similar to a table
explanation: A Spark DataFrame is a distributed collection of data organized into named columns, providing optimized processing across a cluster.

text: What is the Lambda architecture in big data?
options: An architecture that uses only serverless Lambda functions for processing
A hybrid architecture with batch and speed layers to provide both accuracy and low latency
An architecture based on the lambda calculus for functional data processing
A single-layer architecture that processes all data in one pass
explanation: Lambda architecture combines a batch layer for accurate processing of complete data with a speed layer for real-time low-latency processing.

text: What is the difference between Spark RDD and Spark DataFrame?
options: RDDs are strongly typed distributed collections while DataFrames have schema information enabling query optimization
DataFrames are lower-level than RDDs with less optimization support
RDDs support SQL queries while DataFrames require only programmatic access
DataFrames store data on disk while RDDs always keep data in memory
explanation: RDDs are low-level distributed collections, while DataFrames add schema information enabling Catalyst optimizer to perform query optimization.

text: What is the Kappa architecture and how does it differ from Lambda?
options: Kappa uses three processing layers while Lambda uses two
Kappa processes data in batches only while Lambda handles both batch and stream
Kappa simplifies Lambda by using only a stream processing layer for both real-time and reprocessing
Kappa requires more infrastructure than Lambda for the same workload
explanation: Kappa architecture simplifies Lambda by using only a stream processing layer, treating all data as streams and replaying the stream for reprocessing.

text: What is the purpose of Apache Flink in big data processing?
options: A stream processing framework with true event-time semantics and exactly-once guarantees
A file linking system for connecting distributed storage nodes
A data visualization library for creating interactive dashboards
A cluster management tool for scheduling batch processing jobs
explanation: Apache Flink is a distributed stream processing framework that provides true event-time processing, exactly-once state consistency, and low-latency computation.

text: What is data lineage in big data systems?
options: The family tree of data scientists who worked on a project
The line-by-line processing order of data in a pipeline
The complete record of data's origins, movements, and transformations throughout its lifecycle
The version history of a database schema definition
explanation: Data lineage tracks the complete journey of data from its origin through all transformations and movements, enabling auditing, debugging, and compliance.

text: What are columnar storage formats like Parquet advantageous for?
options: Processing individual rows in transactional workloads
Analytical queries that access specific columns, enabling compression and efficient I/O
Real-time write-heavy applications with frequent inserts
Storing unstructured data like images and videos
explanation: Columnar formats like Parquet store data by column, enabling efficient compression and fast analytical queries that access only needed columns.

text: What is the concept of data mesh?
options: A physical mesh network connecting data center servers
A data replication strategy using mesh topology
A visualization technique that displays data as a 3D mesh
A decentralized data architecture where domain teams own and share their data as products
explanation: Data mesh is a decentralized approach where domain-oriented teams own their data as products, with federated governance and self-serve infrastructure.

text: What is exactly-once processing semantics in stream processing?
options: Processing each message at most one time, possibly losing some messages
Processing each record at least once with possible duplicates
Processing records in exactly the order they were produced
Guaranteeing each record affects the final state exactly once even through failures
explanation: Exactly-once semantics guarantees that each record's effect on the system state occurs precisely once, even in the presence of failures and retries.

text: What is the purpose of a data lakehouse architecture?
options: A house-shaped data center for storing data lake infrastructure
Combining data lake flexibility with data warehouse reliability and performance
A small-scale version of a data lake for departmental use
A visualization interface that looks like a lakeside house
explanation: A data lakehouse combines the low-cost storage flexibility of data lakes with the management features and performance of data warehouses.

text: What is change data capture (CDC) in data engineering?
options: A system for capturing screenshots of database schema changes
A tool for detecting unauthorized changes to data files
A monitoring system that captures change requests from users
A pattern that identifies and tracks data changes in source systems for downstream processing
explanation: CDC identifies and captures changes (inserts, updates, deletes) in source databases and propagates them to downstream systems in near real-time.

text: What does MLOps stand for?
options: Machine Learning Operations
Machine Learning Online Processing System
Maximum Likelihood Optimization Protocol
Multi-Layer Operational Processing
explanation: MLOps stands for Machine Learning Operations, combining ML development with IT operations practices for deploying and maintaining models.

text: What is model deployment?
options: Training a model on a new dataset
Making a trained model available for use in a production environment
Deleting a model that is no longer needed
Downloading a pre-trained model from the internet
explanation: Model deployment is the process of integrating a trained machine learning model into a production environment where it can serve predictions.

text: What is a machine learning pipeline?
options: A physical network cable connecting ML servers
An automated sequence of steps from data ingestion to model deployment
A pipeline that transfers data between two databases
A visualization of the model's internal layer connections
explanation: An ML pipeline automates the sequence of steps including data collection, preprocessing, training, evaluation, and deployment.

text: What is version control for ML models?
options: Deleting old versions of models to save storage
Converting models between different framework versions
Controlling which users can access the model
Tracking different versions of models, data, and code over time
explanation: Model version control tracks changes to models, datasets, and code over time, enabling reproducibility and rollback capabilities.

text: What is model monitoring?
options: Continuously tracking model performance and data quality in production
Watching a model train in real time on a display
Using a monitor to view model predictions visually
Recording the time taken to train each model
explanation: Model monitoring continuously tracks deployed model performance, data quality, and drift to ensure the model remains reliable in production.

text: What is a REST API commonly used for in ML deployment?
options: Resting the server between processing jobs
Restoring deleted models from backup storage
Restricting access to the model training code
Exposing model predictions as web service endpoints
explanation: REST APIs expose trained models as web service endpoints, allowing applications to send data and receive predictions over HTTP.

text: What is Docker used for in MLOps?
options: Training models on distributed GPU clusters
Storing large datasets in compressed archives
Packaging applications and their dependencies into portable containers
Designing the user interface for ML applications
explanation: Docker packages ML applications with their dependencies into containers, ensuring consistent environments across development and production.

text: What is experiment tracking in ML?
options: Tracking the physical location of ML hardware
Monitoring the experiment schedule of research labs
Tracking which team members contributed to each experiment
Recording hyperparameters, metrics, and artifacts for each training run
explanation: Experiment tracking records hyperparameters, metrics, code versions, and artifacts for each training run, enabling comparison and reproducibility.

text: What is a model registry?
options: A government registration database for AI systems
A registration form for signing up to use ML services
A log file that registers all model prediction requests
A central repository for managing and versioning trained ML models
explanation: A model registry is a central repository that stores, versions, and manages the lifecycle of trained ML models from development to production.

text: What is CI/CD in the context of MLOps?
options: Computer Intelligence / Computer Design
Cloud Infrastructure / Cloud Deployment
Code Inspection and Code Documentation
Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery for ML systems
explanation: CI/CD in MLOps automates the integration, testing, and deployment of ML code and models to ensure reliable and frequent updates.

text: What is model drift and why is it important to detect?
options: Physical movement of the server hosting the model requiring recalibration
The tendency of models to converge to the same predictions
Movement of model files between different storage systems
Degradation of model performance over time due to changes in data patterns
explanation: Model drift occurs when model performance degrades over time because the real-world data distribution shifts from the training data distribution.

text: What is the difference between data drift and concept drift?
options: Data drift is a change in input data distribution while concept drift is a change in the relationship between inputs and outputs
Concept drift changes input features while data drift changes the target variable
Data drift is always gradual while concept drift is always sudden
Concept drift affects training data while data drift affects test data only
explanation: Data drift is a change in the statistical distribution of input features, while concept drift is a change in the underlying relationship between features and the target.

text: What is A/B testing in the context of model deployment?
options: Testing the model on datasets labeled A and B alternatively
Testing two versions of a model by routing traffic to both and comparing performance
Comparing the accuracy of models trained with different optimizers
Running the model on two different hardware configurations
explanation: A/B testing deploys two model versions simultaneously, routing different portions of traffic to each and comparing real-world performance metrics.

text: What is feature store in MLOps?
options: A retail store that sells feature engineering tools
A database that stores the features of a software product
A visualization tool for plotting feature distributions
A centralized repository for storing, managing, and serving ML features
explanation: A feature store is a centralized repository that manages feature computation, storage, and serving, ensuring consistency between training and inference.

text: What is model serialization?
options: Training models one after another in sequential order
Assigning serial numbers to track model versions
Processing model predictions in a serial (non-parallel) manner
Converting a trained model into a format that can be saved and loaded
explanation: Model serialization converts a trained model into a storable format (like pickle, ONNX, or SavedModel) for saving, sharing, and deployment.

text: What is Kubernetes used for in MLOps?
options: Training neural networks using distributed computing
Creating visual dashboards for model performance metrics
Storing and versioning large training datasets
Orchestrating containerized applications for scaling and managing ML workloads
explanation: Kubernetes orchestrates containerized ML applications, handling scaling, load balancing, and deployment management across clusters.

text: What is the purpose of model validation before deployment?
options: Checking that the model file is not corrupted
Verifying model performance meets required standards on holdout data before production use
Validating that the training data was properly labeled
Ensuring the deployment server has enough disk space
explanation: Model validation ensures the model meets performance, fairness, and reliability standards on holdout data before being deployed to production.

text: What is MLflow used for?
options: Building machine learning models from scratch
Creating flow charts and diagrams for ML architectures
Managing the complete ML lifecycle including experiment tracking, model registry, and deployment
Controlling the flow of data through network switches
explanation: MLflow is an open-source platform for managing the ML lifecycle, including experiment tracking, model packaging, versioning, and deployment.

text: What is canary deployment for ML models?
options: Gradually rolling out a new model to a small subset of users before full deployment
Deploying models to a network named after canary islands
Testing models in an isolated environment named the canary zone
Using bird detection models as a baseline for quality testing
explanation: Canary deployment releases a new model version to a small percentage of traffic first, monitoring for issues before rolling out to all users.

text: What is data versioning and why is it important?
options: Storing the version number in each data record for tracking
Tracking changes to datasets over time to ensure reproducibility of ML experiments
Converting data between different software versions automatically
Numbering data batches sequentially during collection
explanation: Data versioning tracks changes to datasets over time, ensuring ML experiments can be reproduced exactly by using the same data version.

text: What is the training-serving skew problem?
options: The visual distortion of charts displaying training metrics
A discrepancy between the data or code used during training and what is used during serving predictions
The tendency for training accuracy to be higher than serving accuracy
A hardware mismatch between training GPUs and serving CPUs
explanation: Training-serving skew occurs when there are differences in data preprocessing, feature computation, or code between training and serving environments.

text: What is shadow deployment (shadow mode) for ML models?
options: Deploying the model in a dark-themed user interface
Running a new model in parallel with production but only logging predictions without serving them to users
Deploying models during off-peak hours to reduce server load
Hiding the model architecture from external users for security
explanation: Shadow deployment runs a new model alongside the production model, logging its predictions for comparison without actually serving them to users.

text: What is the purpose of ONNX in model deployment?
options: An open format for representing ML models enabling interoperability between frameworks
A cloud service for hosting ML models exclusively
A monitoring tool for tracking model performance in production
A data serialization format for storing training datasets
explanation: ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) provides an open format for ML models, enabling models trained in one framework to be deployed using another.

text: What is the concept of ML technical debt?
