options: The financial cost of computing resources for model training
The time delay between model training and deployment
The accumulation of maintenance burden from ML system complexity including data dependencies and configuration
The cost of hiring ML engineers for the development team
explanation: ML technical debt includes hidden costs from data dependencies, configuration complexity, feedback loops, and other factors that make ML systems expensive to maintain.

text: What is model quantization used for in deployment?
options: Reducing model size and inference time by using lower-precision number representations
Increasing the precision of model predictions for scientific applications
Converting continuous predictions into discrete categorical outputs
Quantifying the business impact of model predictions
explanation: Model quantization converts model weights from higher precision (FP32) to lower precision (INT8), reducing model size and accelerating inference.

text: What is the purpose of chaos engineering in ML systems?
options: Creating chaotic data distributions for stress testing models
Intentionally injecting failures to test system resilience and recovery
Randomly shuffling model parameters to improve generalization
Generating random architectures to find optimal configurations
explanation: Chaos engineering intentionally introduces failures like server crashes or data corruption to test and improve the resilience of ML systems in production.

text: What are feature flags used for in ML model deployment?
options: Enabling or disabling model features and versions dynamically without redeployment
Flagging important features in the dataset for review
Marking features that should be excluded from the model
Flagging errors in the feature engineering pipeline
explanation: Feature flags allow teams to toggle model versions, features, or behaviors dynamically in production without requiring a full redeployment.

text: What is the concept of model governance?
options: Policies, processes, and controls for managing ML models throughout their lifecycle
Government regulations about AI model development
The voting system used by model ensembles for predictions
Managing the permissions for who can train new models
explanation: Model governance encompasses the policies, processes, and controls for managing ML models throughout their lifecycle, including compliance, fairness, and accountability.

text: What is edge deployment for ML models?
options: Deploying models at the edge of a database table
Running ML models directly on edge devices like phones or IoT sensors rather than in the cloud
Placing model servers at the geographical edge of a network
Testing models with edge cases during the validation phase
explanation: Edge deployment runs optimized ML models directly on devices like smartphones and IoT sensors, enabling low-latency inference without cloud connectivity.

text: What is the blue-green deployment strategy for ML models?
options: Using two colored dashboards for monitoring model performance
Deploying models trained on two different datasets simultaneously
Color-coding model versions for easier visual identification
Maintaining two identical production environments and switching traffic between them for zero-downtime deployments
explanation: Blue-green deployment maintains two identical environments, allowing instant switching between old (blue) and new (green) model versions with zero downtime.

text: What is AI bias?
options: The tendency of AI to prefer faster algorithms
The preference of developers for specific programming languages
The direction in which neural network weights are initialized
Systematic unfairness in AI outcomes favoring certain groups over others
explanation: AI bias refers to systematic and unfair discrimination in AI outcomes that favor or disadvantage certain groups based on characteristics like race or gender.

text: What is data privacy in the context of AI?
options: Keeping datasets private from other team members
Making all training data publicly available
Encrypting only the model weights for security
Protecting individuals' personal information used in AI systems
explanation: Data privacy ensures that personal information used to train or operate AI systems is protected from unauthorized access and misuse.

text: What does fairness mean in AI systems?
options: Making sure all models are equally complex
Giving every user exactly the same prediction output
Ensuring AI decisions do not systematically disadvantage particular groups
Using an equal amount of data from every source
explanation: Fairness in AI means ensuring that AI systems do not produce outcomes that systematically disadvantage particular demographic groups.

text: What is transparency in AI?
options: Using transparent hardware for building AI servers
Publishing all source code as open source software
Making AI systems understandable and their decisions explainable
Displaying all training data to end users
explanation: Transparency in AI refers to making AI systems understandable and their decision-making processes explainable to users and stakeholders.

text: What is informed consent in relation to AI data collection?
options: Informing the AI model about the data it will process
Giving consent for the AI to make decisions on behalf of users
Obtaining permission from individuals before collecting and using their data
Informing developers about the types of data available
explanation: Informed consent means individuals are fully aware of and agree to how their personal data will be collected, used, and processed by AI systems.

text: What is algorithmic accountability?
options: Holding developers and organizations responsible for the impacts of their AI systems
Counting the number of algorithms in a system
Measuring the computational accuracy of algorithms
Accounting for the financial cost of running algorithms
explanation: Algorithmic accountability means developers and organizations are responsible for the societal impacts and outcomes produced by their AI systems.

text: What is the concern about deepfakes?
options: They can create realistic but fabricated content used for misinformation
They produce images with very high resolution
They make deep learning models run slower
They cause neural networks to produce inaccurate predictions
explanation: Deepfakes use AI to create realistic but fabricated audio, video, or images of people, raising concerns about misinformation and fraud.

text: What is the ethical concern about AI surveillance?
options: Surveillance cameras use too much electricity
Mass monitoring can violate privacy rights and enable authoritarian control
AI surveillance systems are too expensive to deploy widely
Surveillance data requires too much storage space
explanation: AI-powered surveillance raises ethical concerns about mass privacy violations, potential for abuse, and enabling disproportionate government or corporate control.

text: What does GDPR stand for in the context of AI regulations?
options: General Data Protection Regulation
Global Data Processing Registry
Generative Data Privacy Rule
Government Data Protection Requirement
explanation: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) is a European Union regulation that governs how personal data is collected, processed, and stored.

text: Why is diversity important in AI development teams?
options: Diverse perspectives help identify and reduce biases in AI systems
Diverse teams write faster code than homogeneous teams
Diversity is required by all software development standards
Diverse teams always build more complex models
explanation: Diverse development teams bring varied perspectives that help identify potential biases, blind spots, and fairness issues in AI systems.

text: What is the right to explanation in AI decision-making?
options: The right of individuals to receive meaningful explanations for automated decisions that affect them
The right of developers to explain their code without interference
The requirement for AI to explain its architecture to other AI systems
The right of companies to withhold explanations about proprietary algorithms
explanation: The right to explanation gives individuals the right to receive meaningful information about the logic behind automated decisions that significantly affect them.

text: What is the problem of representation bias in training data?
options: Using too many data representation formats in one dataset
When training data does not adequately represent all groups the model will serve
When the data representation uses too much memory
When features are represented in incompatible numerical formats
explanation: Representation bias occurs when training data underrepresents or excludes certain groups, causing the model to perform poorly for those groups.

text: What is differential privacy in machine learning?
options: Using different privacy settings for different users of the system
The difference in privacy levels between cloud and on-premises deployments
A method for differentiating between private and public data in a dataset
A mathematical framework that provides privacy guarantees by adding calibrated noise to data or outputs
explanation: Differential privacy adds carefully calibrated noise to data or query results, providing mathematical guarantees that individual records cannot be identified.

text: What is the trolley problem analogy in autonomous vehicle AI?
options: The ethical dilemma of how autonomous vehicles should prioritize lives in unavoidable accident scenarios
Deciding which shopping cart algorithm to use for e-commerce
Choosing the best path-finding algorithm for vehicle navigation
Determining the optimal number of vehicles in a fleet
explanation: The trolley problem in autonomous vehicles poses ethical dilemmas about how the AI should choose between unavoidable harmful outcomes in crash scenarios.

text: What is federated learning and how does it address privacy?
options: Training a model across multiple organizations using a shared central dataset
Training models on decentralized data without transferring raw data to a central server
Federating multiple model predictions into a single consensus output
Learning from a federation of pre-trained open-source models
explanation: Federated learning trains models on decentralized data at each device or institution, sharing only model updates rather than raw data to preserve privacy.

text: What is the concept of AI alignment?
options: Physically aligning server racks in a data center
Aligning the training data with the test data distribution
Making sure all layers in a neural network are properly aligned
Ensuring AI systems act in accordance with human values and intentions
explanation: AI alignment is the challenge of ensuring AI systems act in ways that are consistent with human values, goals, and intentions.

text: What is the impact of automation on employment?
options: Automation always creates more jobs than it eliminates
Automation has no significant effect on employment patterns
Automation only affects manufacturing and has no impact on other sectors
AI and automation may displace certain jobs while creating new ones, requiring workforce adaptation
explanation: AI and automation may displace workers in routine tasks while creating new roles, requiring reskilling and workforce adaptation strategies.

text: What is the environmental impact of training large AI models?
options: AI model training has negligible environmental impact
AI training only uses renewable energy sources
Large model training consumes significant energy and generates substantial carbon emissions
The environmental impact is limited to the disposal of old hardware
explanation: Training large AI models requires significant computational resources and energy, contributing to carbon emissions and environmental concerns.

text: What is model interpretability and why does it matter ethically?
options: The ability to convert model outputs into different languages
The ability to understand how a model reaches its decisions, enabling trust and accountability
The speed at which humans can learn to use the model interface
The capacity of the model to interpret various data formats
explanation: Model interpretability enables understanding of how decisions are made, which is essential for trust, accountability, and identifying potential biases.

text: What is the concept of responsible AI?
options: AI systems that respond quickly to user queries
An approach to developing AI that considers ethics, fairness, transparency, and societal impact
AI that takes responsibility for its own maintenance and updates
AI systems that are responsible for backing up their own data
explanation: Responsible AI is an approach to developing and deploying AI systems that considers ethical principles, fairness, transparency, privacy, and societal impact.

text: What is the distinction between equality and equity in AI fairness?
options: Equality means treating all groups the same while equity means providing tailored treatment to achieve fair outcomes
Equity requires identical model accuracy for all groups while equality allows variation
Equality is a legal requirement while equity is only a moral consideration
Equity means using equal amounts of data while equality means equal model complexity
explanation: Equality treats all groups identically, while equity recognizes that different groups may need different treatment to achieve genuinely fair outcomes.

text: What is the impossibility theorem of fairness in machine learning?
options: It is impossible to build a completely unbiased model
Multiple common fairness criteria cannot be simultaneously satisfied except in trivial cases
It is impossible to measure fairness in any quantitative way
Fair models always perform worse than unfair models
explanation: The impossibility theorem shows that several intuitive fairness criteria (like equalized odds and calibration) cannot all be satisfied simultaneously except when base rates are equal.

text: What is the difference between disparate treatment and disparate impact?
options: Disparate impact is always intentional while disparate treatment is unintentional
Disparate treatment affects data quality while disparate impact affects model accuracy
Disparate treatment uses protected attributes directly while disparate impact produces unequal outcomes without explicitly using them
Disparate impact is a European concept while disparate treatment is American
explanation: Disparate treatment directly uses protected attributes in decisions, while disparate impact produces unequal outcomes through proxy variables or systemic patterns.

text: What is the concept of model cards in responsible AI?
options: Physical cards distributed to users explaining AI products
Standardized documentation accompanying models detailing performance, limitations, and ethical considerations
Cards used in a card-sorting methodology for model architecture design
Identification cards assigned to each model version in a registry
explanation: Model cards are standardized documentation that accompany ML models, detailing their intended use, performance across groups, limitations, and ethical considerations.

text: What is adversarial robustness and why is it an ethical concern?
options: The ability to compete against rival companies in the AI market
The robustness of training procedures against hardware failures
The resilience of AI systems against small deliberate input perturbations that could cause harmful misclassifications
The competitive advantage gained by using adversarial training techniques
explanation: Adversarial robustness concerns the vulnerability of AI systems to small, deliberate input modifications that cause wrong predictions, which is critical in safety-sensitive applications.

text: What is the concept of datasheets for datasets?
options: Spreadsheets containing the actual data values
Hardware specification sheets for data storage devices
Standardized documentation describing dataset composition, collection process, and recommended uses
Summary statistics automatically generated by data analysis tools
explanation: Datasheets for datasets provide standardized documentation about a dataset's creation, composition, intended uses, and potential biases, promoting transparency.

text: What is the concept of value-sensitive design in AI?
options: Designing AI systems to maximize their monetary value
Setting the numerical values of model parameters through design
Designing user interfaces that display model values and metrics
A methodology that integrates human values throughout the AI system design process
explanation: Value-sensitive design is a methodology that accounts for human values like privacy, fairness, and autonomy throughout the entire AI system design process.

text: What is the concept of algorithmic auditing?
options: Auditing the financial transactions of AI companies
Checking the computational efficiency of algorithm implementations
Auditing the code quality of machine learning libraries
Systematically evaluating AI systems for bias, fairness, and compliance through independent review
explanation: Algorithmic auditing systematically evaluates AI systems for bias, fairness, accuracy, and regulatory compliance through independent or internal review processes.

text: What is the dual-use dilemma in AI research?
options: The challenge of using AI for both training and inference simultaneously
The difficulty of deploying AI models on both cloud and edge devices
The concern that AI research intended for beneficial purposes can also be misused for harmful applications
The challenge of using the same AI model for two different tasks
explanation: The dual-use dilemma refers to the concern that AI technologies developed for beneficial purposes can be repurposed for harmful applications like surveillance or weapons.

text: What is the existential risk argument regarding advanced AI?
options: The risk that AI companies will go bankrupt due to high development costs
The risk that AI will make existing programming languages obsolete
The danger that AI models will consume all available cloud computing resources
The concern that sufficiently advanced AI could pose threats to human civilization if not properly aligned
explanation: The existential risk argument warns that sufficiently advanced AI systems could pose catastrophic risks to humanity if their goals are not properly aligned with human values.

text: What is a chatbot an example of?
options: Database management
Artificial Intelligence
Hardware optimization
File compression
explanation: A chatbot uses AI techniques such as natural language processing to simulate human conversation.

text: Which programming language is most commonly associated with AI development?
options: COBOL
Pascal
Python
Fortran
explanation: Python is the most popular language for AI due to its rich ecosystem of libraries like TensorFlow and scikit-learn.

text: What does the term 'training' mean in machine learning?
options: Installing software on a server
Feeding data to a model so it can learn patterns
Writing new code for an algorithm
Compressing data for storage
explanation: Training refers to the process where a model learns patterns from data by adjusting its internal parameters.

text: Which type of AI can only perform a specific task it was designed for?
options: General AI
Super AI
Narrow AI
Conscious AI
explanation: Narrow AI, also called weak AI, is designed and trained to perform a single specific task like image recognition or spam filtering.

text: What is a dataset in the context of AI?
options: A type of neural network architecture
A collection of data used for training or evaluation
A programming library for visualization
A hardware device for computation
explanation: A dataset is a structured collection of data that is used to train, validate, or test machine learning models.

text: Which of the following best describes reinforcement learning?
options: Learning from labeled input-output pairs
Grouping data points without labels
Learning by receiving rewards or penalties for actions
Memorizing all training examples exactly
explanation: Reinforcement learning involves an agent learning to make decisions by receiving feedback in the form of rewards or penalties.

text: What does the Turing Test evaluate?
options: The speed of a computer processor
A machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behavior indistinguishable from a human
The accuracy of a classification model
The energy efficiency of an algorithm
explanation: The Turing Test, proposed by Alan Turing, evaluates whether a machine can exhibit behavior indistinguishable from a human in conversation.

text: Which industry commonly uses AI for fraud detection?
options: Agriculture
Textile manufacturing
Banking and finance
Construction
explanation: Banks and financial institutions widely use AI to detect unusual transaction patterns and prevent fraudulent activity.

text: What is the role of a label in supervised learning?
options: It names the programming language used
It provides the correct output for a given input
It defines the hardware requirements
It specifies the data storage format
explanation: In supervised learning, labels are the known correct outputs associated with input data, used to guide the model during training.

text: What is an AI agent?
options: A physical robot that performs surgery
A system that perceives its environment and takes actions to achieve goals
A type of database management system
A network security firewall
explanation: An AI agent is a system that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it using actuators to achieve specified goals.

text: What distinguishes a model-based reflex agent from a simple reflex agent?
options: It uses only condition-action rules
It maintains an internal state to track unobservable aspects of the environment
It always requires human supervision
It cannot operate in dynamic environments
explanation: A model-based reflex agent maintains an internal model of the world to handle partially observable environments, unlike simple reflex agents that rely solely on current percepts.

text: In which scenario would unsupervised learning be preferred over supervised learning?
options: When labeled data is abundant and well-curated
When the goal is to discover hidden groupings in unlabeled data
When prediction accuracy is the only metric
When the output categories are already defined
explanation: Unsupervised learning is preferred when labels are unavailable and the goal is to discover hidden structures or groupings within the data.

text: What is the exploration-exploitation tradeoff in reinforcement learning?
options: Choosing between training speed and model accuracy
Balancing trying new actions versus using known rewarding actions
Selecting between supervised and unsupervised approaches
Deciding between local and cloud deployment
explanation: The exploration-exploitation tradeoff involves balancing the need to try new actions to discover better rewards versus exploiting known actions that already yield good rewards.

text: How does transfer learning reduce development effort?
options: By eliminating the need for any data entirely
By reusing knowledge from a pre-trained model on a new related task
By automatically generating source code
By compressing the model to a smaller file size
explanation: Transfer learning leverages features learned from one task to improve performance on a different but related task, reducing the amount of training data and time needed.

text: What is the primary limitation of a rule-based expert system compared to machine learning?
options: It requires a GPU for execution
It cannot handle tasks where rules are difficult to enumerate explicitly
It always produces probabilistic outputs
It needs more training data than neural networks
explanation: Rule-based expert systems struggle when the domain knowledge is difficult to capture as explicit rules, whereas ML models can learn complex patterns directly from data.

text: What role does the reward function play in reinforcement learning?
options: It preprocesses the raw input features
It provides feedback that guides the agent toward desired behavior
It splits the dataset into training and testing subsets
It controls the learning rate schedule
explanation: The reward function defines the goal by providing scalar feedback signals that tell the agent how good or bad its actions are, shaping its learned policy.

text: Why is the frame problem significant in AI planning?
options: It causes memory overflow in neural networks
It describes the difficulty of representing what does not change after an action
It prevents models from converging during training
It limits the speed of search algorithms
explanation: The frame problem highlights the challenge of efficiently representing which aspects of the world remain unchanged when an action is performed, complicating AI planning systems.

text: What is the key idea behind ensemble methods in machine learning?
options: Using a single powerful model for all predictions
Combining multiple models to produce a more robust prediction
Reducing the dataset size before training
Training one model on multiple GPUs simultaneously
explanation: Ensemble methods combine predictions from multiple models to achieve better generalization and robustness than any individual model alone.

text: How does a knowledge graph differ from a traditional relational database for AI applications?
options: It stores data only in flat CSV files
It represents entities and relationships as a graph enabling flexible semantic queries
It requires more storage than any other format
It cannot handle unstructured data at all
explanation: Knowledge graphs represent information as interconnected entities and relationships, enabling richer semantic reasoning and flexible queries compared to rigid table schemas.

text: What is the significance of the No Free Lunch theorem in machine learning?
options: It proves that deep learning is always superior
It states that no single algorithm performs best on every possible problem
It guarantees convergence for all gradient methods
It shows that more data always leads to better accuracy
explanation: The No Free Lunch theorem demonstrates that no single learning algorithm universally outperforms all others across every possible problem domain.

text: What is the symbol grounding problem in AI?
options: The difficulty of training neural networks with symbolic loss functions
The challenge of connecting abstract symbols to real-world meaning and perception
The problem of encoding Unicode symbols in datasets
The issue of grounding electrical circuits in AI hardware
explanation: The symbol grounding problem asks how abstract symbols manipulated by AI systems acquire meaning, connecting them to real-world sensory experiences and referents.

text: How does the Chinese Room argument challenge strong AI?
options: It proves neural networks cannot learn language
It argues that syntactic manipulation of symbols does not constitute genuine understanding
It demonstrates that AI cannot pass the Turing Test
It shows that Chinese language processing is computationally harder
explanation: Searle's Chinese Room argument contends that a system can manipulate symbols according to rules without truly understanding their meaning, challenging the claim that computation equals cognition.

text: What is the difference between epistemic and aleatory uncertainty in AI models?
options: Epistemic relates to data noise while aleatory relates to model complexity
Epistemic arises from limited knowledge and is reducible; aleatory arises from inherent randomness and is irreducible
Epistemic only applies to classification while aleatory applies to regression
Both terms describe the same type of uncertainty
explanation: Epistemic uncertainty stems from insufficient data or knowledge and can be reduced with more data, while aleatory uncertainty is inherent randomness in the process and cannot be eliminated.

text: Why is reward shaping considered both useful and dangerous in reinforcement learning?
options: It always speeds up training without side effects
It can accelerate learning but may introduce unintended biases that lead to suboptimal policies
It only works for discrete action spaces
It eliminates the need for exploration entirely
explanation: Reward shaping can guide the agent toward desired behavior faster, but poorly designed shaping rewards may create shortcuts that lead the agent to exploit the shaped reward rather than achieving the true objective.

text: What is Moravec's paradox and its implication for AI development?
options: Complex reasoning is harder for AI than simple arithmetic
Sensorimotor skills that are easy for humans are surprisingly hard for AI while abstract reasoning is comparatively easier
AI systems always outperform humans on perceptual tasks
Parallel processing is always slower than sequential processing in AI
explanation: Moravec's paradox observes that high-level reasoning requires relatively little computation while low-level sensorimotor skills demand enormous computational resources, contrary to human intuition.

text: How does counterfactual reasoning enhance explainability in AI systems?
options: By showing the largest weights in a neural network
By describing what minimal changes to the input would alter the model's decision
By listing all training examples used during fitting
By computing feature importance via permutation
explanation: Counterfactual explanations describe the smallest changes to input features that would result in a different prediction, providing intuitive and actionable explanations for model decisions.

text: What is the key challenge of multi-agent reinforcement learning compared to single-agent settings?
options: It requires less computational power overall
The environment becomes non-stationary because other agents are simultaneously learning and adapting
It eliminates the need for a reward function
It always converges faster to an optimal solution
explanation: In multi-agent RL, each agent faces a non-stationary environment because other agents are also learning and changing their policies, making convergence guarantees much harder to establish.

text: What does the concept of instrumental convergence suggest about advanced AI systems?
options: That all AI systems will converge to the same architecture
That sufficiently advanced AI systems may develop similar sub-goals like self-preservation regardless of final objectives
That AI training always converges to a global minimum
That multiple AI models produce identical outputs given the same data
explanation: Instrumental convergence suggests that intelligent agents with diverse final goals may converge on similar instrumental sub-goals such as self-preservation, resource acquisition, and goal-content integrity.

text: Why is the problem of induction a fundamental concern for machine learning generalization?
options: It makes backpropagation computationally infeasible
Past observations do not logically guarantee future patterns will hold, yet ML relies on this assumption
It prevents neural networks from having more than two layers
It means training data must always be larger than test data
explanation: The problem of induction highlights that generalizing from observed data to unseen cases is not logically guaranteed, which is a foundational philosophical concern underlying all machine learning generalization.

text: What is the alignment tax in the context of AI safety?
options: The financial cost of purchasing AI safety hardware
The additional cost in performance, time, or resources required to make an AI system safer and more aligned
The tax imposed by governments on AI companies
The computational overhead of running antivirus on AI servers
explanation: The alignment tax refers to the extra cost in development effort, compute, or capability that is incurred when building AI systems that are safe and aligned with human intentions compared to unaligned alternatives.

text: What is a scalar in linear algebra?
options: A matrix with multiple rows and columns
A single numerical value without direction
A vector with three dimensions
An operation that transforms matrices
explanation: A scalar is a single number, as opposed to a vector or matrix, and represents a zero-dimensional quantity.

text: What is the dot product of two vectors?
options: A new vector perpendicular to both inputs
A scalar obtained by summing element-wise products
A matrix formed by outer multiplication
A vector obtained by cross multiplication
explanation: The dot product multiplies corresponding elements of two vectors and sums the results, yielding a scalar value.

text: What does the mean of a dataset represent?
options: The most frequently occurring value
The middle value when data is sorted
The average value computed by summing all values and dividing by count
The range between maximum and minimum values
explanation: The mean is calculated by summing all values in the dataset and dividing by the number of values, representing the central tendency.

text: What is a probability value of 0 for an event?
options: The event is certain to happen
The event is impossible
The event has a fifty percent chance
The event outcome is unknown
explanation: A probability of 0 means the event cannot occur, while a probability of 1 means it is certain to occur.

text: What is the shape of an identity matrix?
options: Rectangular with more rows than columns
Always a single row vector
Square with ones on the diagonal and zeros elsewhere
Triangular with random values
explanation: An identity matrix is a square matrix with ones along the main diagonal and zeros in all other positions, serving as the multiplicative identity for matrices.

text: What does the term 'variance' measure in statistics?
options: The central value of a distribution
The total count of data points
How spread out data points are from the mean
The correlation between two variables
explanation: Variance measures the average squared deviation of data points from their mean, quantifying the spread or dispersion of a distribution.

text: What is the transpose of a matrix?
options: The matrix multiplied by itself
The matrix with rows and columns swapped
The inverse of the original matrix
The matrix with all values negated
explanation: The transpose of a matrix is obtained by swapping its rows and columns, so element at position (i,j) moves to position (j,i).

text: What is a derivative in calculus?
options: The sum of all function values
The rate of change of a function at a given point
The area under a curve
The maximum value of a function
explanation: A derivative measures the instantaneous rate of change of a function with respect to its variable, which is fundamental to optimization in ML.

text: What is the range of a standard probability distribution?
options: Negative infinity to positive infinity
Minus one to one
Zero to one
Zero to infinity
explanation: Probability values are always between 0 and 1 inclusive, where 0 represents impossibility and 1 represents certainty.

text: What is a 2x3 matrix?
options: A matrix with 2 columns and 3 rows
A matrix with 2 rows and 3 columns
A square matrix of size 6
A diagonal matrix with 5 elements
explanation: A 2x3 matrix has 2 rows and 3 columns, containing a total of 6 elements.

text: Why is the log-likelihood used instead of likelihood in maximum likelihood estimation?
options: It produces a different optimal solution
It converts products into sums making optimization easier and numerically stable
It eliminates the need for calculus entirely
It always results in a convex objective function
explanation: Taking the logarithm of the likelihood converts products of probabilities into sums, which simplifies differentiation and avoids numerical underflow with very small probability values.

text: What does the eigenvalue of a matrix represent geometrically?
options: The angle of rotation applied by the transformation
The scaling factor applied to its corresponding eigenvector by the transformation
The determinant of the transformation matrix
The rank of the matrix after decomposition
explanation: An eigenvalue represents how much the corresponding eigenvector is scaled when the matrix transformation is applied, without changing the eigenvector's direction.

text: What is the purpose of L2 regularization from a mathematical perspective?
options: It removes features with zero variance from the model
It constrains the weight vector to lie within a hypersphere penalizing large weights
It converts a non-linear problem into a linear one
It ensures all weights become exactly zero
explanation: L2 regularization adds the squared magnitude of the weight vector to the loss, effectively constraining weights to a hypersphere and penalizing large values to prevent overfitting.

text: How does Bayes' theorem update a prior belief?
options: By averaging the prior with the likelihood
By multiplying the prior by the likelihood and normalizing with the evidence
By replacing the prior entirely with observed frequencies
By subtracting the evidence from the prior probability
explanation: Bayes' theorem computes the posterior probability by multiplying the prior by the likelihood and dividing by the marginal evidence, enabling systematic belief updating.

text: What does a positive semi-definite matrix guarantee about a quadratic form?
options: The quadratic form can take negative values
The quadratic form always equals zero
The quadratic form is always non-negative for any input
The quadratic form is always strictly positive
explanation: A positive semi-definite matrix ensures that the associated quadratic form produces non-negative values for all input vectors, which is important for convexity in optimization.

text: What is the chain rule used for in the context of neural networks?
options: Initializing weights to random values
Computing gradients of the loss with respect to parameters across multiple layers
Normalizing input features before training
Selecting the optimal learning rate schedule
explanation: The chain rule enables computation of gradients through composed functions, which is essential for backpropagation to compute how each parameter affects the loss across network layers.

text: What does the covariance matrix capture about a multivariate dataset?
options: Only the mean of each variable individually
The pairwise linear relationships and variances among all variables
The causal dependencies between variables
The non-linear correlations exclusively
explanation: The covariance matrix contains the variances of individual variables on the diagonal and pairwise covariances off-diagonal, capturing linear relationships among all variable pairs.

text: Why is the softmax function used for multi-class classification outputs?
options: It computes the absolute value of each output
It converts raw logits into a valid probability distribution that sums to one
It reduces the number of output classes automatically
It applies L1 regularization to the output layer
explanation: Softmax exponentiates each logit and normalizes by the sum, converting raw model outputs into probabilities that are non-negative and sum to one across all classes.

text: What is the geometric interpretation of matrix multiplication AB?
options: Adding the dimensions of A and B together
Applying transformation A after transformation B to input vectors
Computing the element-wise product of A and B
Finding the intersection of row spaces of A and B
explanation: Matrix multiplication AB represents the composition of two linear transformations: first applying B then applying A, combining their geometric effects sequentially.

text: What does a singular matrix indicate about a system of linear equations?
options: The system has exactly one unique solution
The system either has no solution or infinitely many solutions
The system requires iterative methods to solve
The system is overdetermined with no feasible solution
explanation: A singular matrix has a determinant of zero and is non-invertible, indicating that the corresponding system of equations is either inconsistent or has infinitely many solutions.

text: Why does the KL divergence fail to be a true distance metric?
options: It always equals zero regardless of the distributions
It is asymmetric and does not satisfy the triangle inequality
It can only compare discrete distributions
It produces imaginary numbers for continuous distributions
explanation: KL divergence is not a true metric because it is asymmetric and it does not satisfy the triangle inequality required of a distance metric.

text: What is the significance of the Hessian matrix in optimization?
options: It provides first-order gradient information for steepest descent
It captures second-order curvature information that determines the nature of critical points
It stores the training data in compressed form
It computes the learning rate automatically from the loss surface
explanation: The Hessian matrix of second partial derivatives provides curvature information at a point, allowing determination of whether a critical point is a minimum, maximum, or saddle point.

text: How does the reparameterization trick enable gradient-based training of variational autoencoders?
options: By removing the need for an encoder network entirely
By expressing the random sampling as a deterministic function of learnable parameters plus fixed noise
By replacing stochastic layers with deterministic dropout
By computing exact posteriors instead of approximate ones
explanation: The reparameterization trick moves randomness outside the computational graph by writing z = mu + sigma * epsilon where epsilon is fixed noise, making the sampling operation differentiable with respect to mu and sigma.

text: What property must a kernel function satisfy to be valid for use in support vector machines?
options: It must be differentiable everywhere in its domain
It must correspond to a positive semi-definite Gram matrix for any set of inputs
It must produce outputs strictly between zero and one
It must map inputs to a finite-dimensional feature space only
explanation: Mercer's theorem states that a valid kernel function must produce a positive semi-definite Gram matrix for any finite set of input points, ensuring it corresponds to an inner product in some feature space.

text: Why can saddle points be more problematic than local minima in high-dimensional optimization?
options: Saddle points only exist in two-dimensional spaces
Saddle points become exponentially more common relative to local minima as dimensionality increases
Saddle points always have larger loss values than local minima
Gradient descent cannot detect saddle points in any dimension
explanation: In high-dimensional spaces, saddle points proliferate exponentially compared to local minima because each critical point is a minimum along some dimensions and a maximum along others.

text: What is the significance of the Fisher information matrix in machine learning?
options: It computes the exact posterior distribution of parameters
It measures the amount of information that observable data carries about unknown model parameters
It determines the optimal batch size for training
It converts discrete distributions into continuous ones
explanation: The Fisher information matrix measures the expected curvature of the log-likelihood, quantifying how much information the observed data provides about the model parameters.

text: How does the Wasserstein distance differ from KL divergence for comparing distributions?
options: Wasserstein distance is only defined for Gaussian distributions
Wasserstein distance provides meaningful gradients even when distributions have non-overlapping supports
Wasserstein distance is always smaller than KL divergence
Wasserstein distance ignores the geometry of the sample space
explanation: Unlike KL divergence which can be infinite when distribution supports do not overlap, the Wasserstein distance accounts for the geometry of the underlying space and provides smooth gradients.

text: What does the spectral norm of a weight matrix control in neural network training?
options: The number of neurons in each hidden layer
The Lipschitz constant of the associated layer bounding its sensitivity to input perturbations
The learning rate required for convergence
The total number of trainable parameters in the model
explanation: The spectral norm bounds the Lipschitz constant of the layer, controlling how much small input perturbations can be amplified, which is critical for training stability.

text: Why is the natural gradient preferred over the standard gradient in certain optimization problems?
options: It requires fewer hyperparameters to tune manually
It accounts for the curvature of the parameter space using the Fisher information resulting in more efficient updates
It always converges in a single iteration
It eliminates the need for backpropagation entirely
explanation: The natural gradient pre-multiplies the standard gradient by the inverse Fisher information matrix, adapting the update direction to the local geometry of the parameter space.

text: What is the connection between the evidence lower bound and variational inference?
options: ELBO provides an exact computation of the marginal likelihood
ELBO is a tractable lower bound on the log evidence that is maximized to approximate the true posterior
ELBO measures the distance between two datasets
ELBO computes the optimal number of latent dimensions
explanation: The evidence lower bound provides a tractable objective for variational inference by lower-bounding the log marginal likelihood; maximizing ELBO simultaneously tightens this bound and makes the approximate posterior closer to the true posterior.

text: Which Python library is primarily used for numerical computing with arrays?
options: Flask
NumPy
Django
Requests
explanation: NumPy provides support for large multi-dimensional arrays and matrices along with mathematical operations on them.

text: What does the pandas function read_csv() do?
options: Writes data to a CSV file on disk
Reads a CSV file into a DataFrame
Converts a dictionary into a CSV string
Validates the format of a CSV file
explanation: The read_csv() function in pandas reads a comma-separated values file and loads it into a DataFrame for analysis and manipulation.

text: Which library is commonly used to create static visualizations in Python?
options: Scikit-learn
TensorFlow
Matplotlib
Beautiful Soup
explanation: Matplotlib is the foundational plotting library in Python used to create static charts, graphs, and visualizations.

text: What does import sklearn provide access to?
options: A web development framework
A machine learning library with common algorithms and tools
A database management system
An image editing toolkit
explanation: Scikit-learn is a Python library that provides simple and efficient tools for machine learning including classification, regression, and clustering algorithms.

text: What data structure does pandas primarily use to store tabular data?
options: List of tuples
Dictionary of sets
DataFrame
NumPy scalar
explanation: Pandas uses the DataFrame, a two-dimensional labeled data structure similar to a spreadsheet or SQL table, as its primary container for tabular data.

text: Which keyword is used to define a function in Python?
options: function
define
func
def
explanation: The def keyword is used to define a function in Python, followed by the function name and parameters in parentheses.

text: What is the output of len([1, 2, 3, 4]) in Python?
explanation: The len() function returns the number of items in a sequence; the list [1, 2, 3, 4] contains 4 elements.

text: Which method is used to check for missing values in a pandas DataFrame?
options: df.empty()
df.isnull()
df.missing()
df.check_na()
explanation: The isnull() method in pandas returns a boolean DataFrame indicating True where values are missing and False otherwise.

text: What does the fit method do in scikit-learn?
options: Evaluates the model on test data
Trains the model on the provided training data
Exports the model to a file
Visualizes the training data
explanation: The fit() method trains a scikit-learn model by learning parameters from the provided training data.

text: Which Python data type is immutable and ordered?
options: List
Dictionary
Tuple
Set
explanation: Tuples are immutable ordered sequences in Python, meaning their elements cannot be changed after creation.

text: What is the purpose of the train_test_split function in scikit-learn?
options: It trains two models simultaneously on the same data
It divides a dataset into separate training and testing subsets
It splits a neural network into two smaller networks
It separates features from labels in a single DataFrame
explanation: train_test_split randomly divides the dataset into training and testing portions, enabling model evaluation on unseen data.

text: How does NumPy broadcasting work when adding a (3,1) array to a (1,4) array?
options: It raises a shape mismatch error immediately
It produces a (3,4) result by expanding dimensions to match
It truncates the larger array to match the smaller
It concatenates both arrays into a (4,4) matrix
explanation: NumPy broadcasting stretches the (3,1) array across columns and the (1,4) array across rows, producing a (3,4) result.

text: What does df.groupby(column).agg() accomplish in pandas?
options: It sorts the DataFrame by the specified column only
It groups rows by unique values in the column and applies aggregation functions to each group
It filters out all duplicate values in the column
It renames the column and resets the index
explanation: groupby() partitions the DataFrame into groups based on unique column values, and agg() applies one or more aggregation functions to each group.

text: What is the advantage of using a pipeline in scikit-learn?
options: It increases the training speed by parallelizing all steps
It chains preprocessing and model steps ensuring consistent transformation on train and test data
It automatically selects the best algorithm for the data
It eliminates the need for cross-validation entirely
explanation: Pipelines chain preprocessing and modeling steps into a single object, ensuring that transformations fitted on training data are consistently applied to test data.

text: How does vectorized computation in NumPy differ from a Python for-loop?
options: Vectorized operations are always slower due to overhead
Vectorized operations execute in optimized C code making them significantly faster than Python loops
NumPy for-loops and vectorized operations have identical performance
Vectorized operations only work with integer data types
explanation: NumPy vectorized operations delegate computation to optimized C and Fortran code, avoiding Python interpreter overhead and achieving orders-of-magnitude speedups.

text: What is the purpose of the pickle module for machine learning models?
options: It converts models into human-readable JSON format
It serializes Python objects to byte streams for saving and loading trained models
It compresses training data to reduce storage requirements
It encrypts model weights for secure deployment
explanation: The pickle module serializes Python objects including trained ML models into byte streams that can be saved to disk and later deserialized.

text: What does the apply method do on a pandas DataFrame?
options: It permanently modifies the DataFrame schema in place
It applies a function along an axis of the DataFrame to each row or column
It merges two DataFrames based on matching keys
It converts all columns to numeric data types automatically
explanation: The apply() method applies a specified function along a given axis of a DataFrame, enabling custom transformations on each element, row, or column.

text: Why might you use np.random.seed() before training a model?
options: To encrypt the random number generator output
To ensure reproducibility of random operations across different runs
To increase the randomness of weight initialization
To reset the GPU memory before computation
explanation: Setting a random seed ensures that the sequence of pseudo-random numbers is identical across runs, making experiments reproducible.

text: What is the difference between loc and iloc in pandas?
options: loc uses integer positions while iloc uses label-based indexing
loc uses label-based indexing while iloc uses integer positions
Both loc and iloc use only integer-based indexing
Both loc and iloc use only label-based indexing
explanation: loc selects data by row and column labels, while iloc selects by integer position indices.

text: How does GridSearchCV in scikit-learn optimize hyperparameters?
options: It randomly samples hyperparameter combinations once
It exhaustively evaluates all specified hyperparameter combinations using cross-validation
It uses gradient descent to optimize hyperparameters
It trains a single model and adjusts parameters during inference
explanation: GridSearchCV performs an exhaustive search over a specified parameter grid, evaluating each combination using cross-validation.

text: What is the GIL and how does it affect parallel ML training in Python?
options: The GIL accelerates multi-threaded computation in NumPy
The Global Interpreter Lock prevents true parallel execution of Python bytecode limiting CPU-bound thread parallelism
The GIL only affects I/O operations not computation
The GIL is a security feature that sandboxes ML libraries
explanation: The Global Interpreter Lock ensures only one thread executes Python bytecode at a time; ML libraries circumvent this by calling optimized C extensions that release the GIL.

text: Why can pickling a scikit-learn model be problematic across different library versions?
options: Pickle files are always forward-compatible across versions
Deserialization may fail or produce incorrect results if the model class definition changed between versions
Pickle only works with models that have fewer than 100 parameters
Pickle files become corrupted after 30 days automatically
explanation: Pickle serializes the exact class structure; if scikit-learn internal class definitions change between versions, deserialization may raise errors or silently produce incorrect model behavior.

text: How does joblib.Parallel differ from multiprocessing.Pool for scikit-learn workloads?
options: joblib.Parallel cannot run tasks in parallel at all
joblib.Parallel provides memory-mapped array sharing and better integration with NumPy reducing serialization overhead
multiprocessing.Pool is always faster for all workloads
joblib.Parallel requires a GPU while Pool uses only CPUs
explanation: joblib.Parallel uses memory mapping for large NumPy arrays to avoid expensive serialization and provides configurable backends, making it more efficient for scikit-learn workloads.

text: What problem does StratifiedKFold solve that regular KFold does not?
options: It guarantees each fold has the same number of features
It preserves the class distribution in each fold preventing biased evaluation of imbalanced datasets
It reduces training time by skipping redundant folds
It ensures each data point appears in every fold exactly once
explanation: StratifiedKFold ensures that each fold maintains the same proportion of each class as the full dataset, which is critical for reliable evaluation on imbalanced tasks.

text: Why should you avoid fitting a scaler on the entire dataset before splitting into train and test?
options: It increases computation time without any benefit
It causes data leakage because test set statistics influence the scaler parameters
It makes the scaler incompatible with the model
It produces negative values which most models cannot handle
explanation: Fitting a scaler on the full dataset before splitting allows information from the test set to leak into the training pipeline, producing optimistically biased performance estimates.

text: What is the purpose of __getstate__ and __setstate__ methods in custom scikit-learn estimators?
options: They define the forward pass of a neural network layer
They control how the estimator is serialized and deserialized enabling custom pickle behavior
They specify which hyperparameters appear in get_params
They determine the order of pipeline step execution
explanation: __getstate__ and __setstate__ control the pickling behavior of custom estimators, allowing developers to exclude non-serializable attributes or transform state during serialization.

text: How does Cython integration improve performance-critical sections of scikit-learn?
options: Cython converts Python code to Java bytecode for JVM execution
Cython compiles Python-like code to C extensions enabling static typing and removing interpreter overhead
Cython replaces all Python data structures with SQL tables
Cython only affects I/O performance not computation
explanation: Cython allows annotating Python code with static types and compiling it to C extensions, removing the interpreter overhead and enabling performance comparable to hand-written C.

text: What is the risk of using inplace=True in pandas operations within a machine learning pipeline?
options: It always causes a memory leak in the Python interpreter
It can silently break method chaining and may create inconsistent DataFrame views leading to subtle bugs
It doubles the memory usage of every operation
It prevents the DataFrame from being passed to scikit-learn
explanation: inplace=True modifies the DataFrame directly, breaking method chains and sometimes operating on a view rather than a copy, which can lead to hard-to-debug inconsistencies.

text: How does dask.dataframe extend pandas for larger-than-memory datasets?
options: It compresses the entire dataset into memory using lossy encoding
It partitions the DataFrame into smaller pandas chunks and uses lazy evaluation with task scheduling
It converts all data to 8-bit integers to reduce memory usage
It streams data directly to the GPU without any RAM usage
explanation: Dask partitions a large dataset into many pandas DataFrames and constructs a task graph that is lazily evaluated, enabling parallel out-of-core computation on datasets that exceed available memory.

text: Why is np.float32 sometimes preferred over np.float64 in deep learning frameworks?
options: Float32 provides higher numerical precision than float64
Float32 uses half the memory and is faster on GPUs which are optimized for single-precision arithmetic
Float32 is the only type supported by Python natively
Float64 cannot represent negative numbers correctly
explanation: Float32 uses 4 bytes instead of 8, reducing memory bandwidth and storage requirements, and modern GPUs are heavily optimized for single-precision operations.

text: What is data preprocessing in machine learning?
options: Deploying a trained model to production servers
Transforming raw data into a clean and usable format before training
Visualizing the final predictions of a model
Selecting the best algorithm for a given task
explanation: Data preprocessing involves cleaning, transforming, and organizing raw data into a suitable format for machine learning model training.

text: What is a missing value in a dataset?
options: A value that exceeds the column maximum
An entry in the dataset that has no recorded information
A value that appears more than once in a column
A data point that falls below the mean
explanation: A missing value indicates that no data was recorded for a particular field in a data entry, often represented as NaN or null.

text: What does normalization do to numerical features?
options: Removes all outliers from the dataset permanently
Scales values to a fixed range typically between 0 and 1
Converts numerical features into categorical ones
Sorts the dataset in ascending order by value
explanation: Normalization rescales numerical features to a common range, typically 0 to 1, using the minimum and maximum values.

text: What is one-hot encoding used for?
options: Compressing numerical data into fewer bits
Converting categorical variables into binary vector representation
Encrypting sensitive data columns for privacy
Reducing the number of rows in a dataset
explanation: One-hot encoding creates binary columns for each category, with a 1 indicating the presence of that category and 0 otherwise.

text: Why is it important to handle duplicate rows in a dataset?
options: Duplicates improve model training by reinforcing patterns
Duplicates can bias the model and inflate performance metrics artificially
Duplicates are always caused by sensor malfunctions
Duplicates only affect unsupervised learning models
explanation: Duplicate rows can skew the distribution of the training data and lead to overly optimistic evaluation metrics, producing a biased model.

text: What is label encoding?
options: Assigning descriptive labels to visualization axes
Converting each unique category to a unique integer value
Creating labels for unsupervised clustering results
Encoding image pixels into binary format
explanation: Label encoding assigns each unique categorical value a unique integer, converting categories like red, blue, green to 0, 1, 2.

text: What is the purpose of splitting data into training and testing sets?
options: To reduce the overall size of the dataset
To evaluate the model on data it has not seen during training
To speed up the training process significantly
To ensure all categories are equally represented
explanation: Splitting data ensures the model is evaluated on unseen data, providing an honest estimate of how well it generalizes.

text: What is an outlier in a dataset?
options: A data point that is very close to the mean value
A data point that significantly deviates from other observations
A data point with a missing label column
A data point that has been correctly preprocessed
explanation: An outlier is an observation that lies far from the majority of the data, potentially indicating errors, noise, or genuine extreme values.

text: What does dropping a column mean in data preprocessing?
options: Renaming a column to a shorter name
Removing an entire feature column from the dataset
Sorting a column in descending order
Converting a column to a different data type
explanation: Dropping a column means completely removing that feature from the dataset, typically because it is irrelevant or has too many missing values.

text: What is standardization of features?
options: Converting all text data to uppercase letters
Transforming features to have zero mean and unit standard deviation
Removing all negative values from the dataset
Merging multiple features into a single column
explanation: Standardization transforms each feature to have a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1, making features comparable in scale.

text: What is the difference between mean imputation and median imputation for missing values?
options: Mean imputation always produces integer values while median does not
Mean uses the average and is sensitive to outliers while median uses the middle value and is robust to outliers
Median imputation requires the data to be normally distributed
Mean imputation can only be applied to categorical features
explanation: Mean imputation replaces missing values with the arithmetic average which can be skewed by outliers, while median imputation uses the middle value which is more robust.

text: Why might log transformation be applied to a skewed feature?
options: To reverse the order of data points in the column
To reduce right skewness and make the distribution more approximately normal
To convert continuous features into discrete categories
To eliminate all negative values from the dataset
explanation: Log transformation compresses the range of large values and stretches small values, reducing positive skewness and making the distribution closer to normal.

text: What is the curse of dimensionality in the context of feature preprocessing?
options: Having too few features for any model to learn from
As dimensions increase data becomes sparse and distance metrics lose meaningfulness
Models always perform better with more features regardless
High dimensionality only affects visualization not model performance
explanation: The curse of dimensionality refers to the phenomenon where data becomes increasingly sparse in high-dimensional spaces, making distance-based methods less effective.

text: When should you use robust scaling instead of standard scaling?
options: When all features follow a perfect normal distribution
When the dataset contains significant outliers that would distort mean and standard deviation
When the features are all binary categorical values
When the dataset has fewer than 10 observations
explanation: Robust scaling uses the median and interquartile range instead of mean and standard deviation, making it resistant to outliers.

text: What is target encoding for categorical variables and when is it useful?
options: Encoding targets as one-hot vectors for regression tasks
Replacing each category with the mean of the target variable for that category
Converting the target variable from numeric to categorical format
Dropping all categories with fewer than five observations
explanation: Target encoding replaces each category with the mean target value for that category, which is particularly useful for high-cardinality categorical features.

text: How does SMOTE address class imbalance during preprocessing?
options: It removes all majority class samples until classes are balanced
It generates synthetic minority class samples by interpolating between existing minority examples
It duplicates the entire dataset including both classes equally
It adjusts the loss function weights without modifying the data
explanation: SMOTE creates synthetic minority class examples by interpolating between existing minority instances and their nearest neighbors.

text: What is the difference between ordinal encoding and one-hot encoding?
options: Ordinal encoding creates binary columns while one-hot uses integers
Ordinal encoding preserves a meaningful order among categories while one-hot encoding treats all categories as equally unrelated
One-hot encoding can only handle two categories at a time
Ordinal encoding always introduces multicollinearity issues
explanation: Ordinal encoding assigns integers that reflect a natural ordering, while one-hot encoding creates separate binary columns treating categories as unordered.

text: Why should feature scaling be applied after the train-test split?
options: Scaling before splitting has no effect on the result whatsoever
Scaling before splitting causes data leakage by allowing test set statistics to influence training data transformations
Scaling must be done on the original raw data only
Scaling after splitting doubles the computation time unnecessarily
explanation: Fitting the scaler on the full dataset before splitting leaks information from the test set into the training process.

text: What is binning and when is it applied to numerical features?
options: Converting binary features into continuous values
Grouping continuous values into discrete intervals to reduce noise and capture non-linear patterns
Removing the first and last bins from a histogram
Sorting features into alphabetical order for indexing
explanation: Binning discretizes continuous features into intervals or bins, which can reduce the effect of minor observation errors and help capture non-linear relationships.

text: How does principal component analysis serve as a preprocessing step?
options: It removes all correlated features by deleting columns
It transforms features into a lower-dimensional space of uncorrelated components capturing maximum variance
It fills in missing values using matrix factorization
It standardizes all features to the same categorical format
explanation: PCA projects the original features onto orthogonal principal components ordered by explained variance, reducing dimensionality while retaining the most informative directions.

text: What is the problem of data leakage through temporal features and how can it be prevented?
options: Temporal leakage only occurs in image classification tasks
Using future information that would not be available at prediction time which is prevented by time-based splitting
Temporal features always improve model performance without risk
Data leakage is automatically handled by all scikit-learn transformers
explanation: Temporal data leakage occurs when features or data from the future leak into training; it is prevented by using time-based splits where training data always precedes test data chronologically.

text: Why can multicollinearity be problematic for linear regression but less so for tree-based models?
options: Multicollinearity crashes the training process for all model types
In linear regression it inflates coefficient variance making them unreliable while trees split on one feature at a time unaffected by correlations
Tree models cannot detect correlated features at all
Linear regression automatically removes correlated features during fitting
explanation: Multicollinearity inflates the variance of regression coefficients making them unstable, while tree-based models select the best single feature at each split and are largely unaffected.

text: What are the risks of applying SMOTE before cross-validation rather than within each fold?
options: There is no difference between the two approaches
Synthetic samples generated from test-fold neighbors leak information into training causing inflated performance estimates
SMOTE only works when applied to the entire dataset at once
Cross-validation automatically prevents any leakage from SMOTE
explanation: Applying SMOTE before cross-validation creates synthetic samples that may be based on points that end up in the test fold, leaking information.

text: How does target encoding with smoothing prevent overfitting for rare categories?
options: It drops all categories with fewer than a threshold number of observations
It blends the category-specific mean with the global mean weighted by category frequency to regularize estimates
It applies L2 regularization to the encoding values directly
It assigns the same value to all rare categories regardless of their target distribution
explanation: Smoothed target encoding computes a weighted average of the category mean and the global mean, where the weight shifts toward the global mean for categories with few observations.

text: What is the impact of covariate shift on a deployed preprocessing pipeline?
options: Covariate shift only affects unsupervised models not supervised ones
The input feature distribution changes between training and production causing the fixed preprocessing transformations to become suboptimal
Covariate shift improves model performance over time automatically
Preprocessing pipelines are immune to distribution changes by design
explanation: Covariate shift means the distribution of input features changes after deployment, so scaler parameters and other preprocessing steps fitted on training data may no longer be appropriate.

text: Why is Bayesian imputation considered superior to single-value imputation for missing data?
options: It is computationally cheaper than mean imputation
It models the uncertainty of missing values by generating multiple plausible imputations reflecting the posterior distribution
It always produces exactly the same result as mean imputation
It can only be applied to binary classification datasets
explanation: Bayesian imputation generates multiple plausible values for each missing entry drawn from the posterior distribution, properly accounting for imputation uncertainty.

text: What is the Yeo-Johnson transformation and when is it preferred over Box-Cox?
options: Yeo-Johnson is identical to Box-Cox but faster computationally
Yeo-Johnson handles both positive and negative values while Box-Cox requires strictly positive data
Box-Cox works on negative data while Yeo-Johnson requires positive values only
Yeo-Johnson can only be applied to integer features
explanation: The Yeo-Johnson transformation generalizes Box-Cox to handle zero and negative values, making it applicable to a wider range of features.

text: How does feature hashing handle high-cardinality categorical features and what is its main drawback?
options: It applies one-hot encoding with dimensionality reduction via PCA afterward
It maps categories to a fixed-size vector using hash functions but introduces collisions where different categories share the same bucket
It requires precomputing all possible category values before deployment
It converts categories to continuous values using target statistics only
explanation: Feature hashing uses a hash function to map categories to a fixed number of buckets, avoiding the need to maintain a vocabulary, but collisions can cause different categories to share the same feature index.

text: What is the difference between missing completely at random and missing not at random and why does it matter?
options: Both types can be safely handled with simple mean imputation
MCAR means missingness is unrelated to any data while MNAR means missingness depends on the unobserved value itself requiring more sophisticated handling
MNAR data can always be deleted without introducing bias
MCAR data requires more complex imputation than MNAR data
explanation: MCAR missingness is independent of all data so simple methods work well, but MNAR missingness depends on the missing value itself, making naive imputation methods biased.

text: Why can quantile transformation be more robust than standard scaling for feeding data into neural networks?
options: Quantile transformation preserves the original distribution shape exactly
It maps all features to a uniform or normal distribution regardless of outliers or skewness making training more stable
It eliminates the need for batch normalization in all cases
It only works with features that have fewer than 100 unique values
explanation: Quantile transformation maps each feature to a specified distribution by rank, making it inherently robust to outliers and skewness.

text: What does EDA stand for in data science?
options: Enhanced Data Algorithm
Exploratory Data Analysis
Estimated Data Accuracy
Extracted Data Archive
explanation: EDA stands for Exploratory Data Analysis, the process of examining and summarizing data characteristics before formal modeling.

text: What type of chart is best for showing the distribution of a single continuous variable?
options: Pie chart
Scatter plot
Histogram
Line chart
explanation: A histogram divides continuous data into bins and displays the frequency of observations in each bin, revealing the distribution shape.

text: What does the describe() method in pandas provide?
options: A visual plot of all numeric columns
Summary statistics including count, mean, std, min, and quartiles
The data types of each column in the DataFrame
A correlation matrix of all features
explanation: The describe() method computes summary statistics like count, mean, standard deviation, minimum, quartiles, and maximum for numeric columns.

text: What is a scatter plot used to visualize?
options: The frequency of categorical values
The relationship between two continuous variables
The proportion of each category in a dataset
The time trend of a single variable
explanation: A scatter plot displays individual data points on two axes, revealing the relationship and potential correlation between two continuous variables.

text: What is a box plot used to display?
options: The exact values of every data point in a dataset
The distribution summary showing median, quartiles, and potential outliers
The correlation between all pairs of features
The trend of data over time periods
explanation: A box plot shows the median, first and third quartiles, whiskers, and outliers, providing a compact summary of the distribution.

text: What does a correlation coefficient of 1.0 indicate?
options: No relationship between the two variables
A perfect negative linear relationship
A random non-linear association
A perfect positive linear relationship
explanation: A correlation coefficient of 1.0 indicates a perfect positive linear relationship where both variables increase together proportionally.

text: Which plot is most appropriate for comparing counts across categories?
options: Histogram
Scatter plot
Bar chart
Line chart
explanation: A bar chart displays rectangular bars with heights proportional to the count or value for each category.

text: What information does the shape attribute of a DataFrame provide?
options: The data types of all columns in the DataFrame
The number of rows and columns as a tuple
The memory usage of the entire DataFrame
The names of all columns in the DataFrame
explanation: The shape attribute returns a tuple (rows, columns) indicating the dimensions of the DataFrame.

text: What is a heatmap commonly used for in EDA?
options: Displaying geographic locations of data points
Visualizing the correlation matrix between numerical features using color intensity
Showing the distribution of a single variable
Plotting time series data trends
explanation: A heatmap uses color intensity to represent values in a matrix, commonly used to visualize correlations between features.

text: What does checking data types during EDA help identify?
options: The optimal machine learning algorithm to use
Columns that may need type conversion such as numbers stored as strings
The exact accuracy of the final trained model
The best color scheme for data visualization
explanation: Checking data types reveals columns that may be incorrectly typed, such as numerical values stored as strings, which need conversion before analysis.

text: How does a violin plot provide more information than a standard box plot?
options: It shows only the median and ignores the quartiles
It combines a box plot with a kernel density estimation showing the full distribution shape
It only works for categorical data unlike box plots
It displays exact data point values instead of summary statistics
explanation: A violin plot adds a rotated kernel density plot on each side of a box plot, revealing the full distribution shape including multimodality.

text: What does a QQ plot help determine about a dataset?
options: The optimal number of clusters for k-means
Whether the data follows a specified theoretical distribution such as normal
The correlation between two categorical variables
The best feature subset for a classification model
explanation: A QQ plot compares the quantiles of the data against the quantiles of a theoretical distribution; points along the diagonal indicate the data follows that distribution.

text: Why is it important to examine the skewness of features during EDA?
options: Skewed features always need to be removed from the dataset
Many algorithms assume or perform better with approximately symmetric distributions
Skewness only matters for categorical features not numerical ones
Skewed data always indicates measurement errors in data collection
explanation: Many machine learning algorithms work better with normally distributed features, so identifying skewness during EDA guides appropriate transformations.

text: What does a pair plot reveal about a multivariate dataset?
options: Only the correlation coefficients as numerical values
Pairwise relationships between all features along with individual distributions on the diagonal
The optimal hyperparameters for each feature combination
