The hierarchical clustering structure of all features
explanation: A pair plot creates a grid of scatter plots for each pair of features and distribution plots on the diagonal.

text: How can you detect multicollinearity during exploratory data analysis?
options: By checking if any feature has missing values above a threshold
By computing the correlation matrix and identifying feature pairs with very high absolute correlations
By plotting a single histogram for each feature independently
By checking if all features have the same data type
explanation: High absolute correlation values in the correlation matrix between feature pairs indicate multicollinearity.

text: What is the purpose of a cumulative distribution function plot in EDA?
options: It shows the exact probability density at each point
It displays the proportion of data less than or equal to each value revealing percentiles and distribution shape
It only works for time series data and shows trends
It compares the means of two different populations
explanation: A CDF plot shows the probability that a random variable takes a value less than or equal to x, making it easy to read percentiles.

text: When would you prefer a logarithmic scale on a plot axis during EDA?
options: When all data values are between 0 and 1 exclusively
When the data spans several orders of magnitude and a linear scale compresses most values
When the data contains only categorical features
When the data follows a perfect normal distribution
explanation: A logarithmic scale spreads out data that spans multiple orders of magnitude, making patterns visible that would be compressed on a linear scale.

text: What does the interquartile range tell you about data spread?
options: It measures the range between the minimum and maximum values
It measures the spread of the middle 50 percent of data between Q1 and Q3
It calculates the standard deviation of the entire dataset
It indicates the percentage of outliers in the data
explanation: The IQR is the difference between the 75th percentile and 25th percentile, representing the spread of the central half of the data.

text: How does a grouped bar chart differ from a stacked bar chart for comparing categories?
options: They display exactly the same information in the same way
Grouped bars place categories side by side for direct comparison while stacked bars show composition within each group
Grouped charts can only show two categories maximum
Stacked charts always distort the proportions of the data
explanation: Grouped bar charts place bars side by side enabling direct comparison, while stacked bar charts show how subcategories contribute to the total.

text: What can Andrews curves reveal about multivariate data during EDA?
options: Only the variance of individual features in isolation
Whether observations from different classes form distinct visual patterns suggesting separability
The exact decision boundary of a classifier
The optimal number of principal components to retain
explanation: Andrews curves map each multivariate observation to a Fourier series curve; if curves from different classes form distinct bundles, it suggests good class separability.

text: How does the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test complement visual EDA for distribution comparison?
options: It replaces all visual analysis with a single test score
It provides a formal statistical measure of the maximum distance between two cumulative distributions quantifying visual impressions
It can only compare a sample to a uniform distribution
It measures the correlation between two features not their distributions
explanation: The KS test computes the maximum absolute difference between two CDFs and provides a p-value, giving a rigorous statistical basis for distribution differences observed visually.

text: Why can Pearson correlation be misleading during EDA and what alternatives exist?
options: Pearson correlation captures all types of relationships perfectly
Pearson only measures linear association so non-linear relationships may be missed; Spearman rank or mutual information can capture non-linear dependencies
Pearson correlation is always superior to all other measures
Pearson correlation requires data to be uniformly distributed
explanation: Pearson correlation only detects linear relationships; Spearman rank correlation or mutual information can capture monotonic and general non-linear dependencies respectively.

text: What is Simpson's paradox and how can it mislead EDA conclusions?
options: It only occurs in datasets with more than one million rows
A trend that appears in aggregated data reverses when the data is split by a confounding variable
It is a statistical test for detecting outliers in time series
It describes the paradox of having too many features and too few samples
explanation: Simpson's paradox occurs when an association between variables reverses direction when a lurking confounding variable is accounted for.

text: How does the variance inflation factor help quantify multicollinearity beyond simple pairwise correlation?
options: VIF only detects correlations between exactly two features at a time
VIF measures how much a feature variance is inflated by its linear relationship with all other features simultaneously detecting multiway collinearity
VIF is identical to pairwise Pearson correlation
VIF can only be computed for binary categorical features
explanation: VIF regresses each feature against all others and measures the inflation of coefficient variance, capturing multicollinearity involving three or more features.

text: What is the difference between pointwise and pairwise mutual information in feature analysis?
options: They are mathematically identical with different names
Pointwise measures association between specific value pairs while pairwise measures the overall dependency between two entire variables
Pointwise can only be computed for continuous features
Pairwise mutual information is always larger than pointwise
explanation: Pointwise mutual information quantifies the association between specific value combinations, while pairwise mutual information averages over all value combinations to measure total statistical dependency.

text: How can bootstrapped confidence intervals improve the reliability of summary statistics during EDA?
options: They replace the original data with randomly generated values
They provide uncertainty estimates for statistics like the mean by resampling with replacement without assuming a parametric distribution
They require the data to follow a normal distribution exactly
They are only applicable to datasets with fewer than 30 observations
explanation: Bootstrapping generates many resampled datasets to estimate the sampling distribution of any statistic, providing non-parametric confidence intervals.

text: What are the limitations of using t-SNE plots for drawing conclusions about cluster structure?
options: t-SNE always preserves global distances perfectly between all points
t-SNE can create artificial clusters from uniform data and does not preserve global structure making visual cluster impressions potentially misleading
t-SNE is deterministic and always produces identical results
t-SNE works only for datasets with exactly two features
explanation: t-SNE can produce visually distinct clusters even from uniformly distributed data, and distances between clusters are not meaningful.

text: How does the Benford's Law test serve as an anomaly detection tool during EDA?
options: It tests whether data follows a Gaussian distribution
It checks if the leading digit distribution matches the expected logarithmic pattern indicating natural versus fabricated data
It only applies to binary classification datasets
It measures the skewness of the trailing digits in each number
explanation: Benford's Law predicts that the leading digit in naturally occurring numerical data follows a logarithmic distribution; deviations can indicate data fabrication or anomalies.

text: Why should EDA include an analysis of missing data patterns rather than just missing counts?
options: Missing counts provide all necessary information about the data quality
Missing data patterns reveal whether data is MCAR, MAR, or MNAR which determines the appropriate imputation strategy and potential bias
Pattern analysis is only relevant for time series data
Analyzing patterns always requires more data than is available
explanation: Missing data pattern analysis helps distinguish between MCAR, MAR, and MNAR mechanisms, which is critical for selecting appropriate imputation methods.

text: How does the concept of effective sample size affect EDA conclusions when data contains strong autocorrelation?
options: Autocorrelation has no effect on statistical summaries at all
Strong autocorrelation reduces the effective sample size below the actual count making confidence intervals and significance tests overly optimistic
Autocorrelation always increases the effective sample size
Effective sample size only matters for datasets with categorical features
explanation: When observations are autocorrelated, consecutive samples carry redundant information, reducing the effective independent sample size and leading to underestimated standard errors.

text: What is the main requirement for training a supervised learning model?
options: The data must be entirely unlabeled
The data must contain labeled input-output pairs
The data must have exactly two features only
The data must come from a real-time streaming source
explanation: Supervised learning requires labeled data where each input example is paired with the correct output.

text: What is linear regression used to predict?
options: Categorical class labels from discrete features
A continuous numerical output value
Clusters of similar data points
The probability of belonging to multiple groups
explanation: Linear regression models the relationship between features and a continuous target variable.

text: What is the purpose of the bias term in a linear model?
options: It penalizes large weight values to prevent overfitting
It allows the model to shift the prediction away from the origin
It randomly initializes the model parameters
It removes outliers from the training dataset
explanation: The bias term allows the linear model to fit data that does not pass through the origin by shifting the prediction function.

text: What does a decision tree use to split data at each node?
options: Random selection of data points without any criteria
A feature and threshold that best separates the target classes or reduces variance
The alphabetical order of feature names
The memory address of each data point in storage
explanation: A decision tree selects the feature and threshold at each node that maximally separates the classes, using criteria like Gini impurity or information gain.

text: What is the difference between classification and regression?
options: Classification outputs continuous values while regression outputs categories
Classification predicts discrete categories while regression predicts continuous values
Both always produce the same type of output
Classification requires more data than regression in all cases
explanation: Classification predicts discrete class labels, while regression predicts continuous numerical values.

text: What is overfitting in supervised learning?
options: The model performs equally well on training and test data
The model learns the training data too well including noise and performs poorly on new data
The model has too few parameters to learn anything
The model training completes too quickly
explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model memorizes training data including noise, achieving high training accuracy but poor generalization.

text: What is k-nearest neighbors classification based on?
options: Training a neural network with k hidden layers
Assigning the majority class of the k closest training examples to a new data point
Dividing the feature space into exactly k equal partitions
Building k separate decision trees and averaging their outputs
explanation: KNN classifies a new point by finding the k nearest training examples and assigning the most frequent class among those neighbors.

text: What is the logistic regression model primarily used for?
options: Predicting continuous house prices from square footage
Binary classification tasks that output probabilities between 0 and 1
Clustering customers into distinct segments
Reducing the number of features in a dataset
explanation: Logistic regression uses the sigmoid function to model the probability that an input belongs to a particular class.

text: What does the term learning rate refer to in gradient descent?
options: The total number of training epochs completed
The step size that controls how much model parameters are updated during each iteration
The ratio of training data to test data
The speed of the computer processor during training
explanation: The learning rate controls the magnitude of parameter updates during gradient descent.

text: What is a support vector machine designed to find?
options: The average of all training data points
The optimal hyperplane that maximally separates different classes
The cluster centers for unsupervised grouping
The principal components of the feature matrix
explanation: An SVM finds the hyperplane that maximizes the margin between the closest points of different classes.

text: How does regularization strength affect the bias-variance tradeoff?
options: Increasing regularization always decreases both bias and variance simultaneously
Increasing regularization increases bias but decreases variance trading model flexibility for stability
Regularization has no effect on either bias or variance
Decreasing regularization always improves test performance
explanation: Stronger regularization constrains model complexity, increasing bias but decreasing variance.

text: What is the kernel trick in support vector machines?
options: A method to speed up training by reducing the number of support vectors
A technique that computes inner products in a high-dimensional space without explicitly transforming the data
A trick to reduce the learning rate automatically during training
A method to select the optimal number of features for classification
explanation: The kernel trick allows SVMs to operate in a high-dimensional feature space by computing inner products via a kernel function without explicitly performing the transformation.

text: Why might a decision tree with no depth limit perform poorly on test data?
options: It would fail to learn any patterns from the training data
It would create overly complex rules that memorize training noise and fail to generalize
It would always underfit the training data significantly
Decision trees always perform better without depth limits
explanation: An unrestricted decision tree can grow deep enough to memorize noise, producing a model that does not generalize well.

text: What is the difference between L1 and L2 regularization in terms of feature selection?
options: L1 encourages sparse solutions driving some weights to exactly zero while L2 shrinks weights uniformly without zeroing them
L2 always produces sparser models than L1 in practice
Both L1 and L2 produce identical weight distributions
L1 is only applicable to classification while L2 is for regression
explanation: L1 regularization produces sparse solutions where some weights become exactly zero, effectively performing feature selection, while L2 shrinks all weights but rarely zeroes them.

text: How does gradient boosting build an ensemble of weak learners?
options: By training all trees in parallel on random subsets of data
By sequentially training each new tree to correct the residual errors of the previous ensemble
By averaging predictions from independently trained deep decision trees
By stacking multiple logistic regression models in layers
explanation: Gradient boosting sequentially adds weak learners where each new tree is trained to predict the residual errors of the current ensemble.

text: What is the purpose of the softmax function in multi-class classification?
options: It normalizes the input features before training begins
It converts raw output logits into probabilities that sum to one across all classes
It selects the top k most important features for prediction
It applies dropout to prevent overfitting during inference
explanation: Softmax converts a vector of raw scores into a probability distribution where all values are positive and sum to one.

text: Why is cross-validation preferred over a single train-test split for model evaluation?
options: It requires less total computation than a single split evaluation
It provides a more robust performance estimate by evaluating on multiple different test folds reducing variance
It always results in a higher accuracy score than a single split
It eliminates the need for any hyperparameter tuning
explanation: Cross-validation evaluates the model on multiple distinct test folds, providing a more reliable performance estimate.

text: How does the cost-sensitive learning approach handle class imbalance?
options: By oversampling the minority class with synthetic data points
By assigning higher misclassification penalties to the minority class adjusting the decision boundary
By removing all majority class samples until classes are equal
By using unsupervised clustering to rebalance the dataset
explanation: Cost-sensitive learning assigns different misclassification costs to different classes, shifting the decision boundary to better account for class imbalance.

text: What is the difference between hard and soft voting in ensemble classifiers?
options: Hard voting averages probabilities while soft voting uses majority class votes
Hard voting uses majority class votes while soft voting averages predicted probabilities for more nuanced decisions
Both methods always produce identical predictions
Hard voting is for regression while soft voting is for classification
explanation: Hard voting selects the class predicted by the majority, while soft voting averages the predicted probabilities from all classifiers.

text: Why might a Naive Bayes classifier still perform well despite its conditional independence assumption being violated?
options: It compensates by using a very large number of hidden layers
The decision boundary may still be approximately correct even when probability estimates are poorly calibrated
Naive Bayes can only work when features are truly independent
Violated assumptions always make Naive Bayes perform worse than random guessing
explanation: Even though probability estimates are poorly calibrated, the decision boundary can still be approximately correct, leading to good classification performance.

text: Why does the VC dimension matter for understanding generalization in supervised learning?
options: It measures the training speed of the algorithm in seconds
It quantifies the capacity of a hypothesis class and provides theoretical bounds on the gap between training and generalization error
It counts the total number of parameters in a neural network exactly
It determines the optimal learning rate for gradient descent
explanation: The VC dimension measures the expressive capacity of a model class and provides generalization bounds relating training error to test error.

text: How does the representer theorem simplify optimization in kernel methods?
options: It proves that kernel methods always outperform neural networks
It guarantees the optimal solution can be expressed as a linear combination of kernel evaluations at training points reducing infinite-dimensional optimization to a finite problem
It eliminates the need for regularization in kernel machines
It converts non-convex kernel optimization into a closed-form solution
explanation: The representer theorem states that the optimal solution in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space can be written as a finite linear combination of kernel functions centered at training points.

text: What causes the double descent phenomenon in modern supervised learning?
options: Test error strictly decreases as model complexity increases
Test error first decreases then increases with classical overfitting then decreases again as models become heavily overparameterized
The phenomenon has been disproven by recent theoretical work
Double descent only occurs with linear models and not neural networks
explanation: Double descent occurs when test error follows a U-shape, peaks near the interpolation threshold, then decreases again in the overparameterized regime as implicit regularization takes effect.

text: Why can XGBoost's approximate split-finding algorithm be more practical than exact greedy splitting?
options: The approximate method always finds better splits than exact greedy
It reduces time complexity by using quantile sketches on weighted data enabling distributed and large-scale training
The exact method cannot handle categorical features at all
Approximate splitting eliminates the need for regularization terms
explanation: XGBoost's approximate algorithm proposes candidate splits based on weighted quantile sketches, reducing computational cost for large datasets and enabling distributed training.

text: What is the Rademacher complexity and how does it relate to generalization bounds?
options: It measures the computational complexity of the training algorithm
It measures a model class ability to fit random noise and provides tighter data-dependent generalization bounds than VC dimension
It calculates the exact test error before evaluation
It is only relevant to unsupervised learning problems
explanation: Rademacher complexity measures how well a hypothesis class can correlate with random binary labels, providing data-dependent generalization bounds.

text: How does label smoothing in classification affect model calibration and generalization?
options: It randomly shuffles labels to prevent overfitting completely
It replaces hard 0/1 targets with soft targets reducing overconfidence and acting as a regularizer that improves calibration
It increases the learning rate proportionally to the label noise
It only applies to binary classification and not multi-class problems
explanation: Label smoothing replaces one-hot target vectors with a mixture of the hard target and a uniform distribution, preventing overconfidence and improving calibration.

text: What is the difference between transductive and inductive supervised learning?
options: Inductive learning memorizes training data while transductive generalizes
Inductive learning aims to learn a general function for all future inputs while transductive learning focuses specifically on predicting the given unlabeled test points
Transductive learning requires more training data than inductive
Both approaches are mathematically identical in all settings
explanation: Inductive learning learns a general mapping applicable to any future input, while transductive learning focuses specifically on predicting the given unlabeled test points.

text: Why is Platt scaling used after training an SVM and how does it work?
options: It scales the input features to improve SVM training convergence
It fits a sigmoid function to the SVM decision values to convert them into calibrated probability estimates
It adjusts the margin of the SVM to improve classification accuracy
It reduces the number of support vectors by pruning less important ones
explanation: Platt scaling trains a logistic regression model on the SVM decision function outputs to produce calibrated posterior probabilities.

text: How does the structured risk minimization principle guide model selection?
options: It always selects the most complex model available regardless of data size
It balances empirical risk and model complexity by selecting from a nested sequence of hypothesis classes the one with the tightest generalization bound
It minimizes only the training loss without considering generalization
It requires cross-validation for implementation and cannot be applied theoretically
explanation: Structured risk minimization considers a hierarchy of model classes and selects the class that achieves the best bound on generalization error.

text: What is the difference between epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty estimation in supervised models?
options: Both types of uncertainty are always equal for any given prediction
Epistemic uncertainty arises from limited data and can be reduced by collecting more data while aleatoric uncertainty is inherent noise in the labels and is irreducible
Aleatoric uncertainty only exists in unsupervised learning settings
Epistemic uncertainty cannot be estimated with current machine learning methods
explanation: Epistemic uncertainty reflects model ignorance and can be reduced with more data, while aleatoric uncertainty represents inherent noise and cannot be reduced.

text: What is an ensemble method in machine learning?
options: A technique that uses a single very complex model
A technique that combines multiple models to improve overall prediction performance
A method for reducing the size of training data
A tool for visualizing model predictions on a dashboard
explanation: Ensemble methods combine the predictions of multiple base models to produce a single prediction that is typically more accurate and robust.

text: What is bagging in ensemble learning?
options: Boosting models by adjusting their learning rates
Training multiple models on random subsets of data sampled with replacement and averaging their predictions
Removing the weakest model from an ensemble after training
Selecting the single best-performing model from a group
explanation: Bagging trains multiple models independently on bootstrap samples of the data and combines their predictions.

text: What type of ensemble method is Random Forest?
options: A boosting-based ensemble of logistic regression models
A bagging-based ensemble of decision trees with random feature selection
A single deep decision tree with many branches
A stacking ensemble of neural networks
explanation: Random Forest is a bagging ensemble that trains multiple decision trees on bootstrap samples with random feature subsets at each split.

text: What does majority voting mean in a classification ensemble?
options: Every model must agree on the same prediction unanimously
The class predicted by the most individual models is selected as the final prediction
The model with the highest confidence overrides all other predictions
Only the first model prediction is used as the final output
explanation: In majority voting, each model votes for a class and the class with the most votes becomes the final prediction.

text: Why do ensemble methods often outperform single models?
options: They always use more training data per model than single models
They reduce variance by averaging out individual model errors and biases
They require less computational resources than single models
They always eliminate all types of prediction errors completely
explanation: Ensembles reduce variance by combining diverse models whose individual errors tend to cancel out.

text: What is boosting in ensemble learning?
options: Training models in parallel on identical copies of the dataset
Sequentially training models where each new model focuses on correcting errors made by previous models
Randomly selecting a subset of features for each model independently
Combining models by only using the single most accurate one
explanation: Boosting sequentially trains weak learners, with each subsequent model giving more attention to misclassified examples.

text: What is the base learner in an ensemble?
options: The ensemble final combined prediction output
The individual model that forms a building block of the ensemble
The dataset used to train the ensemble method
The evaluation metric used to assess ensemble performance
explanation: The base learner is the individual model that is trained multiple times to form the components of an ensemble.

text: What is the primary advantage of Random Forest over a single decision tree?
options: It trains faster than a single decision tree always
It reduces overfitting by averaging predictions from multiple diverse trees
It uses less memory than a single deep decision tree
It always produces a perfectly interpretable model
explanation: Random Forest reduces overfitting by averaging predictions from many trees trained on different data subsets with random feature selection.

text: What does model diversity mean in ensemble learning?
options: All models use the same algorithm and hyperparameters identically
The individual models make different types of errors increasing the benefit of combining them
Each model predicts a different target variable entirely
The ensemble uses models written in different programming languages
explanation: Model diversity means individual models produce different error patterns, so combining them effectively reduces overall prediction error.

text: What is AdaBoost short for?
options: Advanced Boosting
Adaptive Boosting
Additional Boosting
Automated Boosting
explanation: AdaBoost stands for Adaptive Boosting, which adaptively adjusts the weights of training examples based on previous errors.

text: How does Random Forest handle the correlation between trees to improve performance?
options: It trains all trees on the exact same features and data
It randomly selects a subset of features at each split reducing the correlation between individual trees
It prunes all trees to have exactly the same depth
It uses only one tree but runs it multiple times on different hardware
explanation: By randomly selecting a subset of features at each split, Random Forest ensures that trees are less correlated, yielding more effective variance reduction.

text: What is the difference between gradient boosting and AdaBoost in terms of error correction?
options: Both methods correct errors in exactly the same manner
AdaBoost reweights misclassified samples while gradient boosting fits new models to the negative gradient of the loss function
Gradient boosting cannot handle classification problems
AdaBoost uses gradient descent while gradient boosting uses random search
explanation: AdaBoost increases the weights of misclassified examples, while gradient boosting trains each new learner to predict the residuals or negative gradients of the loss.

text: How does out-of-bag evaluation work in Random Forest?
options: It uses a completely separate holdout dataset created before training
Each tree is evaluated on the samples not included in its bootstrap sample providing a built-in validation estimate
It evaluates the ensemble after removing the worst-performing trees
It uses the training data directly without any form of validation
explanation: Since each bootstrap sample leaves out roughly one-third of the data, each tree can be evaluated on its out-of-bag samples, providing an unbiased estimate without a separate validation set.

text: What is stacking and how does it combine base model predictions?
options: It trains all models sequentially with boosting then selects the best one
It trains a meta-model to learn the optimal combination of base model predictions using their outputs as input features
It simply averages all base model predictions with equal weights
It stacks decision tree nodes to form a deeper single tree
explanation: Stacking trains a meta-learner that takes the predictions of multiple base models as input features and learns how to best combine them.

text: Why does increasing the number of trees in Random Forest eventually show diminishing returns?
options: Additional trees always continue to improve accuracy indefinitely
After enough trees the variance reduction saturates as adding more trees provides redundant information and the irreducible bias remains
More trees always increase overfitting and hurt performance
Random Forest has a strict maximum tree limit imposed by the algorithm
explanation: As more trees are added, the variance reduction diminishes because additional trees provide less new information; performance is eventually limited by the remaining bias.

text: What role does the learning rate play in gradient boosting?
options: It controls the number of features used at each split
It shrinks the contribution of each new tree controlling the tradeoff between number of trees and their individual influence
It determines the maximum depth of each individual tree
It sets the minimum number of samples required per leaf node
explanation: The learning rate scales down each tree's contribution, requiring more trees but improving generalization by preventing any single tree from having too much influence.

text: How does feature importance from a tree-based ensemble help with feature selection?
options: It assigns importance based on the alphabetical order of feature names
It measures how much each feature reduces impurity across all trees providing a ranking for feature relevance
It only considers the root node split of each tree for importance
It requires computing correlations between all feature pairs separately
explanation: Feature importance sums the impurity decrease across all splits on each feature across all trees, indicating which features contribute most to predictions.

text: What is the effect of subsampling rows in gradient boosting without replacement?
options: It makes gradient boosting identical to Random Forest in all ways
It introduces stochasticity that reduces overfitting and can improve generalization similar to how dropout works in neural networks
It always reduces performance by discarding useful training data
It only affects the first tree in the sequence and not subsequent ones
explanation: Row subsampling adds randomness to each iteration, acting as a regularizer that reduces overfitting while also speeding up training.

text: What is a blending ensemble and how does it differ from stacking?
options: Blending and stacking are completely identical techniques
Blending uses a fixed holdout set for generating meta-features while stacking uses cross-validation making stacking more data-efficient
Blending requires neural networks while stacking uses decision trees only
Blending trains models sequentially while stacking trains them in parallel
explanation: Blending reserves a fixed holdout portion for generating meta-learner features, while stacking uses cross-validated predictions, making stacking more data-efficient.

text: Why might an ensemble of diverse weak learners outperform a single strong learner?
options: Weak learners are always individually more accurate than strong learners
Diverse weak learners with uncorrelated errors provide complementary information that when combined reduces overall error more effectively
Weak learners train faster on GPUs than strong learners
Ensembles always use less memory than a single strong model
explanation: When diverse weak learners make different types of errors, combining them allows correct predictions from some to compensate for errors in others.

text: How does the bias-variance decomposition explain why bagging reduces variance but not bias?
options: Bagging reduces bias by selecting only the most accurate models
Averaging multiple models trained on bootstrap samples reduces variance by smoothing out fluctuations while the expected prediction remains similar preserving the base model bias
Bagging always reduces both bias and variance equally
The bias-variance tradeoff does not apply to ensemble methods
explanation: Bagging averages predictions from models trained on different bootstrap samples; since the expected value of an average equals the average of expectations, bias is preserved, but variance of the average is lower.

text: What is the theoretical justification for why boosting can reduce bias?
options: Boosting only reduces variance not bias
Each new learner is trained on the residual errors of the ensemble effectively fitting a more complex function in the space of additive models progressively reducing bias
Boosting applies regularization that automatically reduces bias
Bias reduction in boosting is a random side effect with no theoretical basis
explanation: Boosting operates in function space by greedily adding weak learners to minimize the loss, progressively expanding the effective hypothesis space and reducing bias.

text: What is negative correlation learning in ensemble neural networks?
options: Training networks to produce perfectly identical predictions
A regularization approach that encourages individual networks to make diverse errors by penalizing correlated predictions during joint training
Removing networks that have negative test performance
Training only on negatively labeled examples in the dataset
explanation: Negative correlation learning adds a penalty term that discourages ensemble members from making the same errors, explicitly encouraging diversity.

text: How does the random subspace method differ from bagging in promoting diversity?
options: The random subspace method samples random subsets of features while bagging samples random subsets of training instances
Both methods are mathematically identical and interchangeable
The random subspace method only works with neural network base learners
Bagging creates feature diversity while the random subspace method creates data diversity
explanation: The random subspace method trains each base model on a random subset of features, while bagging trains each model on a bootstrap sample of the data.

text: Why can boosting overfit and what mechanisms help prevent it?
options: Boosting never overfits regardless of the number of iterations
Boosting can overfit by adding too many complex learners that eventually fit noise which is mitigated by early stopping, shrinkage, and subsampling
Overfitting in boosting is only possible with linear base learners
Regularization techniques have no effect on boosting overfitting behavior
explanation: While boosting's sequential error correction can eventually fit noise, early stopping, shrinkage, subsampling, and limiting tree depth effectively control overfitting.

text: What is the PAC-learning connection to boosting algorithms?
options: PAC learning proves that boosting always converges in polynomial time
Boosting was theoretically motivated by the question of whether weak PAC learners can be combined to form a strong learner which Schapire proved possible
PAC learning only applies to unsupervised clustering methods
Boosting algorithms violate PAC learning assumptions in all cases
explanation: Schapire proved that any weak PAC learner can be boosted to arbitrarily high accuracy, providing the theoretical foundation for AdaBoost.

text: How does Bayesian model averaging differ from standard ensemble methods?
options: It always assigns equal weight to all models in the ensemble
It weights each model by its posterior probability given the data providing a principled probabilistic combination that accounts for model uncertainty
It uses boosting to combine Bayesian models sequentially
It replaces all base models with a single Bayesian neural network
explanation: Bayesian model averaging computes predictions as a weighted sum where weights are posterior probabilities reflecting how well each model explains the data.

text: What is the decomposition of ensemble error into average individual error and diversity?
options: Ensemble error equals the product of individual errors and diversity
Ensemble error equals the average individual error minus the diversity term showing that greater diversity directly reduces ensemble error
Diversity has no mathematical relationship to ensemble error
Ensemble error is always greater than the average individual error
explanation: The ambiguity decomposition shows that ensemble error equals average individual error minus diversity, proving that diversity directly reduces ensemble error.

text: Why might CatBoost ordered boosting approach reduce prediction shift compared to traditional gradient boosting?
options: CatBoost uses a random ordering that provides no real benefit
Ordered boosting trains each example using models fitted only on preceding examples preventing target leakage from the current example residuals into its own gradient estimation
CatBoost eliminates the need for any form of regularization
Ordered boosting only applies to categorical features and ignores numerical ones
explanation: Traditional gradient boosting uses the same data for computing gradients and fitting trees; CatBoost computes residuals for each example using only models trained on preceding examples, eliminating this bias.

text: How does snapshot ensembling leverage the training trajectory of a single neural network?
options: It trains one network and evaluates it at different random initializations
It saves model snapshots at local minima during cyclic learning rate training and ensembles them at no extra training cost
It prunes the network at different sparsity levels and combines them
It replicates the network weights across multiple identical copies
explanation: Snapshot ensembling uses cyclic learning rate schedules to drive optimization into multiple local minima, saving the model at each minimum and combining these snapshots into an ensemble.

text: What is unsupervised learning?
options: Learning with labeled data and known correct outputs
Learning from data without predefined labels or correct answers
Learning only from text data and not numerical data
Learning that requires human supervision at every step
explanation: Unsupervised learning finds patterns in data without labeled outputs, relying on the inherent structure of the data itself.

text: What is clustering in unsupervised learning?
options: Predicting a continuous target variable from features
Grouping similar data points together based on their characteristics
Selecting the best features for a supervised model
Sorting data in ascending numerical order
explanation: Clustering groups similar data points into clusters based on distance or similarity metrics.

text: What does the K-means algorithm require as input?
options: The correct labels for each data point
The number of clusters K to form
The exact coordinates of each cluster center in advance
The correlation matrix of all features
explanation: K-means requires the user to specify K, the desired number of clusters, before running the algorithm.

text: What is dimensionality reduction?
options: Adding more features to increase the size of the dataset
Reducing the number of features while preserving important information
Removing all numerical columns from a dataset
Increasing the number of data points in the dataset
explanation: Dimensionality reduction transforms data from a high-dimensional space to a lower-dimensional space while retaining meaningful information.

text: What is PCA primarily used for?
options: Training a classifier on labeled data samples
Reducing the number of features by projecting data onto principal components
Generating synthetic data for data augmentation
Detecting outliers using supervised anomaly labels
explanation: PCA projects data onto orthogonal axes that capture the maximum variance, reducing dimensionality while preserving the most information.

text: What is an example of anomaly detection using unsupervised learning?
options: Classifying emails as spam using labeled examples
Identifying unusual transactions in credit card data without fraud labels
Predicting house prices from historical sales data
Translating text from one language to another
explanation: Unsupervised anomaly detection identifies data points that deviate significantly from normal patterns without requiring labeled examples.

text: What does the term centroid mean in K-means clustering?
options: The data point that is farthest from all cluster members
The center point of a cluster calculated as the mean of all its members
The boundary line between two adjacent clusters
The initial random label assigned to each data point
explanation: A centroid is the mean position of all data points in a cluster, and K-means iteratively updates centroids to minimize within-cluster distance.

text: What is association rule learning?
options: Training a regression model to predict sales figures
Discovering interesting relationships or patterns between variables in large datasets
Clustering customers based on demographic features
Reducing features using principal component analysis
explanation: Association rule learning discovers frequent co-occurrence patterns in data, commonly used for market basket analysis.

text: What is the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning?
options: Supervised requires more computational resources than unsupervised
Supervised uses labeled data while unsupervised works with unlabeled data
Unsupervised always achieves higher accuracy than supervised
Supervised only works with numerical data while unsupervised handles categorical
explanation: The key difference is that supervised learning requires labeled input-output pairs, while unsupervised learning discovers patterns from unlabeled data.

text: Which algorithm is density-based and can find arbitrarily shaped clusters?
options: K-means clustering
Linear regression
DBSCAN
Logistic regression
explanation: DBSCAN groups points in dense regions and can discover clusters of arbitrary shapes unlike K-means which assumes spherical clusters.

text: How does the elbow method help determine the optimal number of clusters for K-means?
options: It plots training accuracy against the number of features used
It plots the within-cluster sum of squares against K and looks for a bend where adding clusters yields diminishing returns
It measures the execution time for different values of K only
It compares K-means results with hierarchical clustering output
explanation: The elbow method plots inertia for different values of K; the optimal K is at the elbow point where the rate of decrease sharply changes.

text: What is the silhouette score and what does it measure?
options: The total number of clusters formed by the algorithm
A measure of how similar each point is to its own cluster compared to the nearest neighboring cluster ranging from -1 to 1
The execution time of the clustering algorithm in seconds
The number of outliers detected by the algorithm
explanation: The silhouette score measures how well each data point fits within its assigned cluster versus the nearest alternative cluster.

text: How does hierarchical agglomerative clustering build a dendrogram?
options: It starts with K pre-specified clusters and splits them iteratively
It starts with each point as its own cluster and iteratively merges the closest pairs until a single cluster remains
It randomly assigns points to clusters and refines them
It uses gradient descent to optimize cluster assignments
explanation: Agglomerative clustering begins with each data point as a separate cluster and progressively merges the most similar pairs.

text: What distinguishes DBSCAN from K-means in terms of cluster assumptions?
options: DBSCAN assumes all clusters are spherical while K-means does not
DBSCAN does not assume a fixed number of clusters or spherical shapes and can identify noise points while K-means requires K and assumes convex clusters
Both algorithms make identical assumptions about cluster shapes
DBSCAN requires more hyperparameters than K-means in all cases
explanation: DBSCAN discovers clusters based on density without requiring a predetermined number and can find arbitrarily shaped clusters while labeling sparse points as noise.

text: What is the difference between hard and soft clustering?
options: Hard clustering uses supervised labels while soft clustering uses unsupervised labels
Hard clustering assigns each point to exactly one cluster while soft clustering assigns probabilities of belonging to multiple clusters
Soft clustering always produces better results than hard clustering
Hard clustering requires more memory than soft clustering
explanation: Hard clustering assigns each point to a single cluster, while soft clustering assigns probability memberships to multiple clusters.

text: How does the t-SNE algorithm preserve local structure during dimensionality reduction?
options: It maintains exact Euclidean distances between all point pairs
It converts pairwise distances to probability distributions and minimizes the KL divergence between high and low dimensional distributions focusing on nearby points
It uses linear projection identical to PCA but with different scaling
It only preserves the global structure and ignores local neighborhoods
explanation: t-SNE constructs probability distributions over pairs based on similarity, then minimizes KL divergence to produce a low-dimensional embedding preserving local neighborhoods.

text: What is the main idea behind autoencoders for unsupervised feature learning?
options: Training a supervised classifier on encrypted features
Training a neural network to reconstruct its input through a bottleneck layer that learns a compressed representation
Using a pre-trained model directly without any further training
Clustering the output of a random neural network
explanation: Autoencoders learn to compress input data into a lower-dimensional bottleneck and then reconstruct the original input, with the bottleneck encoding serving as a learned representation.

text: Why is K-means sensitive to the initial placement of centroids?
options: K-means always converges to the same solution regardless of initialization
Different initial positions can lead the algorithm to converge to different local optima potentially finding suboptimal cluster assignments
Initial centroids are always placed optimally by the algorithm
Centroid initialization only affects the first iteration and not the final result
explanation: K-means converges to a local minimum of the within-cluster sum of squares, so different random initializations can lead to different final configurations.

text: How does Gaussian Mixture Model clustering differ from K-means in its modeling approach?
options: GMM uses hard assignments identical to K-means in every way
GMM models each cluster as a Gaussian distribution with its own mean and covariance allowing soft probabilistic assignments and elliptical cluster shapes
GMM cannot handle more than two clusters at a time
GMM requires labeled data to estimate the Gaussian parameters
explanation: GMM models each cluster as a multivariate Gaussian, allowing elliptical shapes and probabilistic soft assignments.

text: What is the purpose of the epsilon parameter in DBSCAN?
options: It sets the maximum number of clusters the algorithm can find
It defines the radius of the neighborhood around each point used to determine density connectivity
It controls the learning rate of the clustering optimization
It specifies the minimum variance required within each cluster
explanation: Epsilon defines the maximum distance between two points for them to be considered neighbors, controlling the density threshold for cluster boundaries.

text: How does spectral clustering use graph theory to find non-convex clusters?
options: It directly applies K-means to the original feature space
It constructs a similarity graph, computes eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian, and clusters points in the spectral embedding space
It uses depth-first search to find connected components only
It applies PCA followed by hierarchical clustering sequentially
explanation: Spectral clustering builds a similarity graph, uses eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian to embed points into a lower-dimensional space where K-means can effectively separate non-convex clusters.

text: What is the identifiability problem in Gaussian Mixture Models?
options: GMMs cannot model more than three Gaussian components
The likelihood function has multiple equivalent maxima due to label permutations and degenerate solutions making the global optimum non-unique
GMMs require each component to have identical covariance matrices
The identifiability problem only occurs with diagonal covariance matrices
explanation: GMM identifiability is problematic because permuting component labels gives equivalent solutions, and the likelihood can become unbounded when a component collapses onto a single point.

text: Why can HDBSCAN be considered an improvement over standard DBSCAN?
options: HDBSCAN requires more hyperparameter tuning than DBSCAN
HDBSCAN extracts a cluster hierarchy for varying densities eliminating the need to specify a single epsilon value and handling datasets with clusters of different densities
HDBSCAN only works with two-dimensional data unlike DBSCAN
HDBSCAN is faster but less accurate than DBSCAN in all cases
explanation: HDBSCAN extends DBSCAN by building a hierarchy of clusters across all density levels and using cluster stability to extract flat clusters, automatically handling variable density.

text: How does contrastive learning enable self-supervised representation learning?
options: It trains a classifier on manually labeled contrastive examples
It learns representations by pulling augmented views of the same instance together and pushing different instances apart in embedding space
It contrasts supervised and unsupervised loss functions in a single objective
It requires ground truth cluster labels to define positive and negative pairs
explanation: Contrastive learning creates positive pairs from augmented views of the same data point and negative pairs from different data points, training an encoder to produce similar embeddings for positives and dissimilar for negatives.

text: What is the connection between K-means and the EM algorithm for Gaussian Mixture Models?
options: They are completely unrelated algorithms with no theoretical connection
K-means can be viewed as a special case of EM for GMMs where each Gaussian has identical fixed isotropic covariance and assignments are hard rather than soft
EM always converges faster than K-means regardless of the dataset
K-means provides soft assignments while EM provides hard assignments
explanation: K-means is equivalent to EM for a GMM where all components have equal mixing weights and identical spherical covariance matrices, with hard assignments.

text: How does variational inference approximate intractable posteriors in unsupervised latent variable models?
options: It samples from the exact posterior using Markov Chain Monte Carlo
It introduces a tractable approximate distribution and minimizes its KL divergence from the true posterior by maximizing the evidence lower bound
It computes the posterior analytically using matrix inversion
It uses grid search to enumerate all possible latent variable values
explanation: Variational inference posits a parametric family of approximate distributions, then optimizes their parameters to minimize KL divergence from the true posterior, equivalent to maximizing the ELBO.

text: What theoretical guarantee does Lloyd's algorithm for K-means lack?
options: It lacks a guarantee of terminating in finite iterations
It lacks a guarantee of converging to the global optimum of the objective function and may get stuck in local minima
It lacks a guarantee of processing all data points at least once
It lacks a guarantee of producing non-empty clusters
explanation: Lloyd's algorithm only guarantees convergence to a local minimum of the within-cluster sum of squares, not the global optimum.

text: Why does the curse of dimensionality particularly affect distance-based clustering methods?
options: High dimensions always make clustering faster and more accurate
In high-dimensional spaces distances between points converge making it difficult to distinguish nearest from farthest neighbors and undermining distance-based cluster separation
High dimensions only affect supervised learning and not clustering
Distance metrics become computationally cheaper in higher dimensions
explanation: As dimensionality increases, the ratio of the farthest to nearest neighbor distance approaches 1, meaning distance-based methods struggle to distinguish meaningful proximity from noise.

text: How does the information bottleneck method relate to unsupervised learning and clustering?
options: It is a lossless compression technique unrelated to clustering
It formalizes clustering as finding a compressed representation that preserves maximum relevant information about a target variable while minimizing mutual information with the input
It computes the bottleneck features of an autoencoder only
It measures the bandwidth limitation of data transfer during training
explanation: The information bottleneck method frames clustering as an information-theoretic optimization: finding a compressed representation that retains maximum mutual information with a relevant signal.

text: What is the difference between reconstruction-based and density-based anomaly detection approaches?
options: Both approaches are mathematically equivalent with identical results
Reconstruction methods flag anomalies when reconstruction error is high while density methods flag points in low-density regions each capturing different types of anomalies
Reconstruction methods require labeled anomalies while density methods do not
Density methods can only detect anomalies in two-dimensional data
explanation: Reconstruction-based methods detect anomalies that are hard to reconstruct, while density-based methods flag points in sparse regions; they capture different anomaly types and can be complementary.

text: What is accuracy in model evaluation?
options: The number of features used by the model during prediction
The proportion of correct predictions out of all predictions made
The speed at which the model generates predictions
The amount of memory the model uses during inference
explanation: Accuracy measures the fraction of total predictions that the model got correct.

text: What is a confusion matrix?
options: A matrix showing the weights of a neural network
A table showing true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for a classifier
A graph plotting training loss over epochs
A heatmap of feature correlations in the dataset
explanation: A confusion matrix summarizes classifier performance by showing how predictions compare to actual labels across all classes.

text: What is precision in classification?
options: The proportion of actual positives correctly identified by the model
The proportion of predicted positives that are actually correct
The total number of positive predictions made by the model
The ratio of true negatives to all negative predictions
explanation: Precision is the fraction of positive predictions that are truly positive.

text: What is recall also known as?
options: Specificity
Sensitivity or true positive rate
F1 score
False positive rate
explanation: Recall, also called sensitivity or true positive rate, measures the proportion of actual positives correctly identified.

text: What does the F1 score combine?
options: Accuracy and specificity into a single metric
Precision and recall into their harmonic mean
Training loss and validation loss into an average
True positives and true negatives into a sum
explanation: The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, balancing both metrics.

text: What is overfitting indicated by during model evaluation?
options: High training accuracy and high test accuracy together
High training accuracy but significantly lower test accuracy
Low training accuracy and high test accuracy
Equal training and test accuracy both being low
explanation: Overfitting is indicated when the model performs very well on training data but significantly worse on test data.

text: What is a training-validation-test split?
options: Dividing data into three parts for training, tuning hyperparameters, and final evaluation respectively
Using all data for training and evaluating on the same set
Splitting data by feature columns into three groups
Training three separate models on the same data
explanation: A three-way split uses training for fitting, validation for tuning hyperparameters, and test for final unbiased evaluation.

text: What does mean squared error measure in regression?
options: The number of predictions that exactly match the true values
The average of the squared differences between predicted and actual values
The correlation between predicted and actual values
The maximum error among all individual predictions
explanation: MSE computes the average of squared prediction errors, penalizing larger errors more heavily.

text: What is cross-validation used for?
options: Training the model on the entire dataset without any splits
Evaluating model performance more reliably by using multiple train-test splits
Selecting features based on their correlation with the target
Increasing the size of the training dataset artificially
explanation: Cross-validation repeatedly splits data into training and validation folds, providing a more robust estimate of generalization performance.

text: What is a baseline model in evaluation?
options: The most complex model available for a given task
A simple reference model used to establish minimum acceptable performance
The model with the most training data available
A model trained on test data and evaluated on training data
explanation: A baseline model is a simple model that establishes the minimum performance threshold that more complex models should exceed.

text: When is the ROC-AUC score more appropriate than accuracy for model evaluation?
options: When all classes are perfectly balanced in the dataset
When dealing with imbalanced classes because ROC-AUC evaluates discrimination ability across all thresholds
When the model only outputs hard class labels without probabilities
When the dataset has only two features
explanation: ROC-AUC measures the model ability to discriminate between classes across all thresholds, making it more informative for imbalanced data.

text: What is the difference between micro and macro averaging for multi-class metrics?
options: Micro averaging computes separate metrics per class while macro averages globally
Micro averaging aggregates all true positives and false positives globally while macro averaging computes the metric per class and averages them giving equal weight to each class
Both micro and macro averaging always produce identical results
Micro averaging is only used for binary classification tasks
explanation: Micro averaging pools all predictions across classes before computing the metric, while macro averaging computes the metric per class and takes the unweighted mean.

text: How does stratified cross-validation improve upon standard K-fold cross-validation?
options: It randomly shuffles data without considering class proportions
It ensures each fold preserves the class distribution of the original dataset preventing biased evaluation on imbalanced data
It always uses more folds than standard K-fold
It only works with regression problems not classification
explanation: Stratified cross-validation maintains the same class proportions in each fold as in the full dataset.

text: What does a calibration curve reveal about a probabilistic classifier?
options: Whether the model training loss is decreasing over epochs
How well the model predicted probabilities match the actual observed frequencies of positive outcomes
The feature importance ranking for the top 10 features
The optimal threshold for converting probabilities to class labels
explanation: A calibration curve plots predicted probability bins against actual positive rates; a well-calibrated model curve follows the diagonal.

text: Why is log loss preferred over accuracy for evaluating probabilistic classifiers?
options: Log loss is easier to compute than accuracy in all cases
Log loss penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily and evaluates the quality of probability estimates not just the final class label
Log loss always produces values between 0 and 1
Log loss ignores the predicted probabilities and only considers class labels
explanation: Log loss measures the quality of predicted probabilities by heavily penalizing confident incorrect predictions.

text: What is the purpose of a learning curve in model evaluation?
options: To plot the architecture of the neural network visually
To show how training and validation performance change with increasing training set size revealing underfitting or overfitting
To display the learning rate schedule across training epochs
To compare multiple models on the same bar chart
explanation: Learning curves plot model performance against training set size; converging with high error indicates underfitting while a large gap indicates overfitting.

text: How does the Matthews Correlation Coefficient handle class imbalance better than accuracy?
options: It gives extra weight to the majority class predictions only
It considers all four confusion matrix categories producing a balanced measure even when class sizes differ greatly
It ignores true negatives to focus only on positive predictions
It requires the dataset to be perfectly balanced before computation
explanation: MCC uses all four confusion matrix values and produces a score from -1 to +1 that remains informative even with severe class imbalance.

text: What is the difference between R-squared and adjusted R-squared in regression evaluation?
options: They always produce the same value regardless of the model
Adjusted R-squared penalizes the addition of irrelevant features while R-squared always increases or stays the same with more features
R-squared is only for linear regression while adjusted R-squared works for all models
Adjusted R-squared is always larger than R-squared
explanation: R-squared always increases when features are added, while adjusted R-squared penalizes irrelevant features, providing a more honest assessment.

text: Why should you report confidence intervals alongside point estimates of evaluation metrics?
options: Confidence intervals are only needed for datasets with missing values
They quantify the uncertainty in the metric estimate due to finite test set size indicating how reliable the reported performance is
They always make the model appear to perform better than it actually does
They are only meaningful for neural network evaluations
explanation: Confidence intervals communicate the range within which the true performance likely falls, enabling more informed model comparison.

text: What is nested cross-validation and why is it necessary for unbiased model selection?
options: It uses a single loop of cross-validation for both model selection and evaluation
It uses an outer loop for performance estimation and an inner loop for hyperparameter tuning preventing the selection process from biasing the evaluation
It nests multiple models inside each other like ensemble stacking
It is only required for datasets with more than one million samples
explanation: Nested cross-validation uses an inner CV loop for hyperparameter tuning and an outer CV loop for performance estimation, preventing optimistic bias.

text: How does the DeLong test compare two ROC curves statistically?
options: It visually inspects the two curves for obvious differences
It computes the variance of the AUC difference using the theory of U-statistics and tests whether the AUCs are significantly different
It only works when both models are logistic regression classifiers
It compares the training times of the two models
explanation: The DeLong test uses the statistical properties of the AUC as a Mann-Whitney U-statistic to test for statistically significant differences between two models.

text: What is the Brier score decomposition and what does each component reveal?
options: The Brier score cannot be decomposed into meaningful components
It decomposes into reliability measuring calibration, resolution measuring discriminative ability, and uncertainty measuring inherent class variability
It decomposes into precision and recall components only
It decomposes into bias and variance like the MSE decomposition
explanation: The Brier score decomposes into reliability (calibration), resolution (discriminative ability), and uncertainty (inherent unpredictability of the outcome).

text: Why can the ROC curve be misleading for highly imbalanced datasets and what alternative is preferred?
options: ROC curves are always the best choice regardless of class balance
ROC can appear optimistic because the false positive rate denominator is the large negative class; the precision-recall curve is preferred as it focuses on the minority positive class
Precision-recall curves are always worse than ROC curves for imbalanced data
ROC curves cannot be computed when class imbalance exceeds two to one
explanation: With severe class imbalance, even many false positives produce a small FPR due to the large negative class; precision-recall curves focus on positive class performance and reveal poor precision that ROC may hide.

text: How does Bayesian evaluation of classifiers differ from frequentist hypothesis testing?
options: Bayesian evaluation uses p-values while frequentist uses posteriors
Bayesian evaluation computes posterior probabilities of one model being better than another and can quantify the probability of practical equivalence unlike frequentist tests
Bayesian evaluation requires strictly normal distributions of metrics
Frequentist testing always provides more information than Bayesian approaches
explanation: Bayesian evaluation models the distribution of performance differences and computes the posterior probability that one model is better, equivalent, or worse.

text: What is the difference between expected calibration error and maximum calibration error?
options: Both metrics always produce the same value for any model
ECE measures the average calibration gap weighted by bin frequency while MCE measures the worst-case calibration gap across all bins
ECE is always larger than MCE by definition
MCE averages over all bins while ECE takes the maximum
explanation: ECE computes the weighted average absolute calibration difference across bins, while MCE reports the maximum such difference, useful for safety-critical applications.

text: Why is statistical power analysis important when designing evaluation experiments for ML models?
options: It determines the optimal model architecture automatically
It ensures the evaluation uses a large enough sample to reliably detect meaningful performance differences reducing the risk of false negatives
Power analysis is only relevant for medical trials not ML experiments
It replaces the need for cross-validation in all evaluation scenarios
explanation: Power analysis determines the minimum sample size needed to detect a specified effect size with a given confidence level.

text: How does the concept of Pareto optimality apply to multi-objective model evaluation?
options: It selects the model with the highest single metric regardless of other metrics
It identifies the set of models where no metric can be improved without worsening another representing the tradeoff frontier between competing objectives
It always reduces multiple metrics to a single scalar score
It only applies when exactly two evaluation metrics are considered
explanation: Pareto optimality identifies models on the non-dominated frontier where improving one metric necessarily worsens another.

text: What is the permutation test for model comparison and when is it preferred over the paired t-test?
options: Permutation tests require stronger distributional assumptions than t-tests
Permutation tests estimate the null distribution by randomly shuffling labels requiring no normality assumption making them more appropriate for non-normal or small samples
Permutation tests always have higher statistical power than t-tests
Permutation tests can only compare two-class classifiers
explanation: Permutation tests construct the null distribution empirically by shuffling labels, making no assumptions about the underlying distribution.

text: Why is the harmonic mean used for the F1 score rather than the arithmetic mean?
options: The harmonic mean is computationally cheaper to calculate
The harmonic mean penalizes extreme imbalances between precision and recall more heavily than the arithmetic mean encouraging both to be reasonably high
The harmonic mean always produces a higher value than the arithmetic mean
The arithmetic mean cannot be computed for precision and recall values
explanation: The harmonic mean produces a value closer to the smaller of the two quantities, ensuring that a good F1 requires both precision and recall to be reasonably high.

text: How does the concept of distribution shift affect offline evaluation reliability?
options: Distribution shift has no impact on the validity of offline evaluation metrics
When the test distribution differs from the deployment distribution offline metrics may not reflect real-world performance requiring domain adaptation or online evaluation strategies
Distribution shift only affects unsupervised learning models
Offline evaluation is always more reliable than online evaluation regardless of shift
explanation: If the test data distribution does not match the deployment distribution, offline metrics can be misleading; distribution shift requires domain adaptation or online monitoring.

text: What is feature engineering?
options: The process of deploying a model to a production server
The process of creating, transforming, or selecting features to improve model performance
The process of collecting raw data from external sources
The process of training a model on labeled data pairs
explanation: Feature engineering involves creating new features, transforming existing ones, or selecting the most relevant features to improve model performance.

text: What is a derived feature?
options: A feature that comes directly from the raw dataset without any changes
A new feature created by combining or transforming existing features
A feature that is automatically removed during training
A feature that contains only missing values
explanation: A derived feature is created by applying transformations or combinations to existing features, such as computing age from a birthdate.

text: Why might you create polynomial features?
options: To reduce the number of features in the dataset
To capture non-linear relationships between features and the target variable
To remove all outliers from the training data
To convert categorical features to numerical format
explanation: Polynomial features create new features by raising existing features to higher powers, allowing linear models to capture non-linear patterns.

text: What is feature selection?
options: Adding as many features as possible to the model regardless of relevance
Choosing the most relevant subset of features to use for model training
Generating synthetic data points for each feature column
Converting all features to a single data type
explanation: Feature selection identifies and retains only the most informative features, removing irrelevant or redundant ones.

text: What is an interaction feature?
options: A feature created by user feedback during model deployment
A new feature created by multiplying two or more existing features together
A feature that only exists in the test dataset
A feature with constant values across all data points
explanation: An interaction feature captures the combined effect of two or more features by multiplying them together.

text: What is the purpose of extracting the day of the week from a date feature?
options: To reduce the storage space of date columns
To capture weekly patterns or cycles that may influence the target variable
To convert the date into a random numerical value
To remove the date column from the dataset entirely
explanation: Extracting the day of the week allows the model to learn weekly patterns, such as higher sales on weekends.

text: What is a binary indicator feature?
options: A feature that takes any continuous value between 0 and 1
A feature that takes only two values typically 0 or 1 indicating the presence or absence of a condition
A feature encoded using the binary number system base 2
A feature that is always the target variable in classification
explanation: A binary indicator has values of 0 or 1, representing whether a specific condition is present or absent.

text: Why is domain knowledge important for feature engineering?
options: It eliminates the need for any automated feature selection methods
It helps create meaningful features that capture relevant patterns specific to the problem domain
It automatically tunes the model hyperparameters correctly
It ensures the model always achieves perfect accuracy on test data
explanation: Domain knowledge enables creating features that capture meaningful patterns and relationships specific to the problem area.

text: What does encoding the length of a text field as a feature capture?
options: The semantic meaning of the text content precisely
The verbosity or amount of text which may correlate with the target variable
The language the text was written in originally
The number of spelling errors in the text
explanation: Text length can serve as a useful feature that captures verbosity, which may correlate with outcomes like sentiment or importance.

text: What is a count feature?
options: A feature that counts the number of models in an ensemble
A feature representing the frequency or count of occurrences of an event or category
A feature that stores the total number of dataset rows
A feature measuring computation time during training
explanation: A count feature represents how many times something occurs, such as the number of transactions per customer.

text: How does target encoding differ from frequency encoding for categorical features?
options: They are identical techniques that produce the same encoded values
Target encoding uses the mean of the target variable per category while frequency encoding uses the count or proportion of each category
Target encoding requires no training data while frequency encoding does
Frequency encoding produces probabilities while target encoding produces counts
explanation: Target encoding replaces each category with the average target value, capturing the predictive relationship, while frequency encoding uses how often the category appears.

text: What is the purpose of creating lag features in time series data?
options: To remove trends from the time series completely
To make previous time steps available as input features enabling the model to learn temporal dependencies
To fill in missing values using future observations
To normalize the time series to zero mean and unit variance
explanation: Lag features shift the time series by one or more time steps, providing historical context as input features.

text: How does feature crossing help linear models?
options: It reduces the total number of features by merging similar columns
It creates new features from combinations of existing features allowing linear models to learn non-linear decision boundaries
It converts continuous features into missing values for imputation
It applies regularization to specific feature pairs automatically
explanation: Feature crossing creates new features by combining two or more features, enabling linear models to capture interaction effects and non-linear patterns.

text: What is the wrapper method for feature selection and how does it differ from filter methods?
options: Wrapper methods use statistical tests independent of any model while filter methods train models
