Wrapper methods evaluate feature subsets by training a model on each subset while filter methods rank features using intrinsic data properties without model training
Both wrapper and filter methods always produce the same selected features
Wrapper methods are always faster than filter methods for any dataset size
explanation: Wrapper methods evaluate feature subsets by training a model, directly measuring predictive impact, while filter methods use statistical properties independent of any model.

text: Why might ratio features be more informative than their component features individually?
options: Ratios always reduce the total number of features needed
Ratios capture relative relationships and proportions that are scale-invariant and often more meaningful than raw individual values
Ratios eliminate the need for feature normalization entirely
Individual features always contain more information than any derived ratio
explanation: Ratio features capture proportional relationships that are invariant to scale changes, often providing more predictive power than raw individual values.

text: How does recursive feature elimination work?
options: It adds features one at a time from most to least important
It repeatedly trains a model, removes the least important feature, and repeats until the desired number of features remains
It randomly removes features and evaluates model performance
It eliminates features based solely on their variance without model training
explanation: RFE iteratively fits a model, ranks features by importance, removes the least important, and repeats until reaching the target feature count.

text: What is the benefit of applying a log transform to a highly skewed count feature?
options: It converts the feature from numerical to categorical format
It compresses the range of large values and spreads small values making the distribution more symmetric and improving model performance
It guarantees the feature will follow a perfect normal distribution
It removes all zero values from the feature entirely
explanation: Log transformation reduces right skewness by compressing large counts and expanding small counts, producing a more symmetric distribution.

text: How does the variance threshold method work for feature selection?
options: It selects features with the highest variance in the target variable
It removes features whose variance falls below a specified threshold as low-variance features carry little discriminative information
It computes the variance of model predictions to select features
It sets a threshold on the maximum allowed variance per feature
explanation: The variance threshold method removes features with variance below a cutoff, since near-constant features provide minimal discriminative power.

text: What is the advantage of cyclical encoding for features like hour of day or month?
options: It reduces the number of features needed to represent temporal data
It preserves the circular nature of periodic features so that the last value is close to the first value which linear encoding fails to capture
It converts cyclical features into binary indicators only
It eliminates all seasonality from the time series data
explanation: Cyclical encoding using sine and cosine preserves the circular relationship where hour 23 is close to hour 0, which integer encoding cannot represent.

text: How does mutual information guide feature selection for classification tasks?
options: It measures the linear correlation between each feature and the target only
It quantifies the amount of information a feature provides about the target class capturing both linear and non-linear dependencies
It only works with binary features and binary classification tasks
It requires the features to be normally distributed for accurate results
explanation: Mutual information measures the reduction in uncertainty about the target given knowledge of a feature, capturing any type of statistical dependency.

text: How does automatic feature engineering using deep feature synthesis work?
options: It uses genetic algorithms to evolve optimal feature expressions
It recursively applies mathematical transformation and aggregation primitives across related tables to generate features from relational data
It trains a neural network to select the best features from a fixed set
It randomly generates feature combinations and evaluates them with cross-validation
explanation: Deep feature synthesis automatically generates features by stacking transformation primitives and aggregation operations across related entities in a relational dataset.

text: What is the benefit of target-aware feature engineering and what is its primary risk?
options: It always produces unbiased features with no risk of overfitting
It creates highly predictive features using target information but risks severe overfitting and data leakage if not properly validated with out-of-fold encoding
It cannot create more predictive features than target-unaware methods
It only works with regression targets and not classification targets
explanation: Target-aware features can be highly predictive but create a direct dependency on the target; without proper out-of-fold computation, they leak target information causing overfitting.

text: How do entity embeddings for categorical features differ from one-hot encoding in deep learning?
options: Entity embeddings and one-hot encoding produce identical representations
Entity embeddings learn dense low-dimensional vector representations that capture semantic similarity between categories while one-hot creates sparse orthogonal vectors
Entity embeddings only work for binary categories with two values
One-hot encoding always produces better neural network performance
explanation: Entity embeddings map each category to a dense learnable vector where similar categories can have similar representations, unlike one-hot sparse orthogonal vectors.

text: Why might feature selection based on single-feature importance miss important feature interactions?
options: Single-feature methods always capture all types of interactions correctly
A feature may appear uninformative individually but become highly predictive when combined with another feature and single-feature ranking cannot detect such synergistic effects
Feature interactions do not affect model performance in any measurable way
Single-feature selection methods always outperform interaction-aware methods
explanation: Univariate feature selection evaluates each feature independently, so features that are individually weak but jointly powerful will be incorrectly ranked as unimportant.

text: How does permutation importance differ from impurity-based importance for tree models and when might they disagree?
options: They always produce identical feature rankings for any dataset
Permutation importance measures the actual performance drop when a feature is shuffled while impurity importance sums split gains which can be biased toward high-cardinality features
Impurity-based importance is always more accurate than permutation importance
Permutation importance cannot be computed for tree-based models
explanation: Impurity-based importance can be biased toward high-cardinality features, while permutation importance directly measures performance degradation when a feature is shuffled.

text: What is the concept of feature leakage and how can it be systematically detected?
options: Feature leakage only occurs when features are manually created by domain experts
Feature leakage occurs when information from the target or future data contaminates training features and can be detected by checking for unrealistically high single-feature predictive power
Leakage always manifests as lower model performance not higher
Feature leakage is automatically prevented by all standard ML libraries
explanation: Feature leakage introduces information not available at prediction time, often detectable by examining features with suspiciously high individual predictive power.

text: How does the Boruta algorithm improve upon standard Random Forest feature importance for selection?
options: Boruta uses gradient boosting instead of Random Forest for importance
Boruta creates shadow features by permuting all original features and selects features that consistently outperform their shadow counterparts across multiple runs
Boruta only works with linear models and cannot use tree ensembles
Boruta selects exactly the top K features specified by the user
explanation: Boruta adds randomized shadow copies of all features and uses a statistical test to identify features consistently more important than the best shadow feature.

text: What is the theoretical connection between information gain and mutual information in feature selection?
options: They are completely unrelated concepts from different mathematical fields
Information gain from a decision tree split on a feature is equivalent to the mutual information between that feature and the target making them mathematically identical
Information gain is always larger than mutual information by a constant factor
Mutual information can only be computed for continuous features while information gain applies to categorical ones
explanation: Information gain computed at a decision tree split is mathematically equivalent to the mutual information between the feature and the target variable.

text: Why can feature engineering introduce multicollinearity and how should it be managed?
options: Derived features are always independent of their source features
Transformations and interactions of existing features often create correlated features which can destabilize linear models and should be managed through regularization or VIF analysis
Multicollinearity never affects model predictions only interpretability
Feature engineering always reduces multicollinearity in the dataset
explanation: Derived features are inherently correlated with their sources, which can inflate coefficient variance in linear models; this is managed through regularization, VIF pruning, or PCA.

text: How does the concept of representation learning subsume traditional feature engineering?
options: Representation learning and feature engineering are completely unrelated approaches
Representation learning using deep networks automatically discovers hierarchical feature representations from raw data potentially replacing manual feature engineering
Representation learning always produces worse features than manual engineering
Traditional feature engineering is a subset of representation learning by definition
explanation: Representation learning through deep neural networks automatically learns hierarchical feature representations from raw data, effectively automating the feature engineering process.

text: What is a neural network inspired by?
options: The structure of relational databases
The structure and function of the human brain
The design of modern operating systems
The architecture of the internet protocol
explanation: Neural networks are inspired by biological neural networks in the human brain, using interconnected nodes that process information.

text: What is a hidden layer in a neural network?
options: The output layer that produces final predictions
A layer between the input and output layers that processes intermediate computations
A layer that stores the training data during model fitting
A layer that is invisible to the optimizer during training
explanation: Hidden layers are intermediate processing layers that learn to extract increasingly abstract features from the data.

text: What does an activation function do in a neural network?
options: It initializes the weights of the network randomly
It introduces non-linearity allowing the network to learn complex patterns
It determines the learning rate for gradient descent
It controls the number of epochs during training
explanation: Activation functions introduce non-linearity, enabling the network to learn complex non-linear relationships.

text: What is the purpose of backpropagation?
options: To forward propagate input data through the network layers
To compute gradients of the loss with respect to each weight for parameter updates
To randomly initialize all network weights before training
To evaluate the model on the test set after training
explanation: Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight by applying the chain rule backwards through the network.

text: What is an epoch in deep learning training?
options: A single update to one weight in the network
One complete pass through the entire training dataset
The time it takes to train the model in minutes
The number of layers in the neural network
explanation: An epoch represents one full iteration through the entire training dataset.

text: What is the ReLU activation function?
options: A function that outputs the negative of its input always
A function that outputs the input directly if positive and zero otherwise
A function that always outputs a value between -1 and 1
A function that computes the logarithm of its input
explanation: ReLU outputs max(0, x), passing positive values unchanged and converting negative values to zero.

text: What does the loss function measure in deep learning?
options: The total number of parameters in the neural network
How well the model predictions match the actual target values
The speed of forward propagation through the network
The amount of memory used by the model during training
explanation: The loss function quantifies the difference between predictions and actual targets, providing a signal for optimization.

text: What is a batch size in neural network training?
options: The total number of training epochs specified
The number of training examples processed together before updating model parameters
The number of hidden layers in the network architecture
The size of the model file on disk in megabytes
explanation: Batch size is the number of training examples used in one forward and backward pass before the model weights are updated.

text: What is dropout in deep learning?
options: Permanently removing neurons from the network architecture
Randomly deactivating a fraction of neurons during training to prevent overfitting
Reducing the learning rate at each epoch by a fixed amount
Dropping the last layer of the network after training
explanation: Dropout randomly sets a proportion of neuron activations to zero during training, forcing the network to learn redundant representations.

text: What is a convolutional neural network primarily used for?
options: Natural language translation tasks exclusively
Processing grid-like data such as images by detecting spatial patterns through learned filters
Clustering tabular data into predefined groups
Optimizing database query performance
explanation: CNNs use convolutional filters to automatically learn spatial hierarchies of features from grid-like data, making them effective for image tasks.

text: How does batch normalization improve neural network training?
options: It removes the need for activation functions in each layer
It normalizes layer inputs to have zero mean and unit variance reducing internal covariate shift and allowing higher learning rates
It eliminates the need for dropout regularization completely
It automatically selects the optimal batch size for training
explanation: Batch normalization normalizes inputs to each layer across the mini-batch, reducing sensitivity to initialization and allowing higher learning rates.

text: What is the vanishing gradient problem and which architectures address it?
options: Gradients becoming too large during backpropagation causing weight explosion
Gradients becoming exponentially small in deep networks preventing early layers from learning which is addressed by residual connections and LSTMs
Gradients becoming random noise during the first epoch only
Gradients being computed too slowly due to hardware limitations
explanation: The vanishing gradient problem occurs when gradients shrink exponentially through many layers; residual connections and gated units provide shortcut paths that maintain gradient flow.

text: How do residual connections in ResNet help train very deep networks?
options: They skip every other layer and train only half the network
They provide identity shortcut paths that allow gradients to flow directly through skip connections preventing degradation in very deep networks
They replace all activation functions with linear transformations
They reduce the number of parameters by sharing weights across layers
explanation: Residual connections add the input directly to the output, creating shortcut paths that enable unimpeded gradient flow and allow learning residual functions.

text: What is the difference between SGD and Adam optimizer?
options: SGD uses adaptive learning rates while Adam uses a fixed learning rate
Adam maintains per-parameter adaptive learning rates using first and second moment estimates while SGD uses a single fixed learning rate for all parameters
Both optimizers always produce identical training trajectories
Adam requires more training data than SGD to converge
explanation: Adam combines momentum with adaptive per-parameter learning rates using moment estimates, often converging faster than vanilla SGD.

text: How does transfer learning work with pre-trained deep learning models?
options: The pre-trained model weights are randomly reinitialized for the new task
The pre-trained model learned feature representations are reused and fine-tuned on a new task with limited data
Transfer learning requires the new task to be identical to the pre-training task
Pre-trained models cannot be modified after their initial training
explanation: Transfer learning reuses learned feature representations from a pre-trained model and fine-tunes them on a new task with limited data.

text: What is the purpose of learning rate scheduling during training?
options: To keep the learning rate constant throughout the entire training process
To adjust the learning rate during training typically decreasing it to achieve finer convergence in later stages
To randomly change the learning rate at each batch to add noise
To set a different learning rate for each layer permanently
explanation: Learning rate scheduling reduces the learning rate during training, allowing larger steps initially and smaller steps later for precise convergence.

text: Why is weight initialization important for deep networks?
options: Initialization has no effect on the training outcome of deep networks
Poor initialization can cause gradients to vanish or explode from the first forward pass making training fail while proper initialization keeps activations in a healthy range
Weights must always be initialized to zero for optimal training
Initialization only matters for networks with a single hidden layer
explanation: Proper weight initialization ensures that activations and gradients maintain appropriate magnitudes through many layers.

text: What is the role of pooling layers in convolutional neural networks?
options: They increase the spatial resolution of feature maps
They reduce spatial dimensions of feature maps providing translation invariance and reducing computation
They add new features to the existing feature maps
They apply the activation function to each pixel individually
explanation: Pooling layers downsample feature maps, reducing spatial dimensions, providing some translation invariance, and decreasing computational cost.

text: How does data augmentation help prevent overfitting in deep learning?
options: It reduces the size of the training dataset to prevent memorization
It artificially increases training data diversity by applying random transformations like rotation, flipping, and cropping
It adds noise directly to the network weights during training
It removes the most difficult training examples from the dataset
explanation: Data augmentation creates transformed versions of training images, effectively increasing the training set diversity and making the model more robust.

text: What is the difference between a generative and a discriminative model in deep learning?
options: Generative models can only produce images while discriminative models produce text
Generative models learn the joint probability distribution to generate new data while discriminative models learn the decision boundary to classify existing data
Discriminative models are always more accurate than generative models
Generative models require labeled data while discriminative models do not
explanation: Generative models learn the underlying data distribution and can generate new samples, while discriminative models learn the conditional probability to classify inputs.

text: How does the lottery ticket hypothesis challenge conventional wisdom about neural network training?
options: It proves that overparameterized networks always perform worse than small networks
It proposes that dense networks contain sparse subnetworks that when trained in isolation from initialization can match the full network performance
It shows that random initialization is unnecessary for training
It demonstrates that pruning always improves model accuracy
explanation: The lottery ticket hypothesis posits that dense networks contain sparse subnetworks that can be trained independently to match the full network accuracy.

text: What is the spectral bias of neural networks and how does it affect learning dynamics?
options: Neural networks learn all frequency components of the target function simultaneously and uniformly
Neural networks tend to learn low-frequency components first and struggle with high-frequency details creating a systematic bias in learning order
Spectral bias only affects recurrent neural networks not feedforward ones
Spectral bias causes networks to learn only high-frequency noise patterns
explanation: Neural networks preferentially learn low-frequency components before high-frequency ones, which explains why they generalize well as smooth functions but may struggle with rapidly varying targets.

text: How does knowledge distillation compress a large teacher model into a smaller student?
options: The student model copies the exact weights from the teacher model directly
The student learns to match the teacher soft probability outputs which contain richer information about class relationships than hard labels alone
Knowledge distillation only works when teacher and student have identical architectures
The teacher model is pruned to create the student without any additional training
explanation: Knowledge distillation trains a smaller student to mimic the teacher's soft probability distributions, which encode inter-class similarity relationships that hard labels do not convey.

text: Why do neural networks in the lazy training regime behave similarly to kernel methods?
options: Neural networks always behave identically to kernel methods regardless of width
In the infinite-width limit with small learning rates the Neural Tangent Kernel remains approximately constant during training making the network behave like kernel regression
Lazy training only applies to networks with a single hidden layer
Kernel methods are always more powerful than neural networks in practice
explanation: In the lazy or NTK regime, sufficiently wide networks have weights that barely change from initialization, making the Neural Tangent Kernel approximately constant and training dynamics equivalent to kernel regression.

text: What is the role of the Hessian spectrum in understanding loss landscape geometry of deep networks?
options: The Hessian spectrum only contains information about the learning rate
The distribution of Hessian eigenvalues reveals whether the loss landscape has sharp or flat minima and many near-zero eigenvalues indicating directions of approximate invariance
The Hessian spectrum is constant throughout training and never changes
It determines the optimal number of layers for the network architecture
explanation: The Hessian eigenvalue spectrum reveals loss surface curvature: sharp minima have large eigenvalues, flat minima have small ones, and near-zero eigenvalues reflect the manifold of equivalent solutions.

text: How does mixed-precision training achieve speedup without significant accuracy loss?
options: It trains the entire model in 8-bit integer precision exclusively
It uses 16-bit floats for forward and backward passes while maintaining a 32-bit master copy of weights for accumulation preventing underflow during updates
It reduces the number of training epochs by half to compensate for precision loss
Mixed precision only works on CPU hardware not on GPUs
explanation: Mixed-precision training performs computations in FP16 for speed while keeping an FP32 master copy for gradient accumulation, using loss scaling to prevent underflow.

text: What is the information bottleneck theory of deep learning?
options: It describes the physical bandwidth limitations of GPU communication
It posits that deep networks learn by first fitting the training data then compressing representations to retain only information relevant to the output
It proves that shallower networks always learn more information than deeper ones
It only applies to autoencoders and not to classification networks
explanation: The information bottleneck theory suggests training proceeds in two phases: fitting then compression, where irrelevant input information is discarded while task-relevant information is preserved.

text: Why do sharp minima tend to generalize worse than flat minima according to PAC-Bayesian theory?
options: Sharp and flat minima always generalize identically regardless of loss surface geometry
PAC-Bayesian bounds depend on the volume of the parameter region with low loss which is larger for flat minima allowing more perturbation without increasing loss
Sharp minima contain more parameters than flat minima
PAC-Bayesian theory only applies to linear models not deep networks
explanation: Flat minima allow larger perturbations without significantly increasing loss, corresponding to a broader posterior that achieves tighter generalization bounds.

text: How does neural architecture search automate the design of network architectures?
options: It manually tests every possible architecture through human expert evaluation
It uses optimization techniques like reinforcement learning or evolutionary algorithms to search a space of possible architectures for the one that maximizes validation performance
It always produces the same optimal architecture regardless of the task
It only searches over the number of layers and ignores all other architectural choices
explanation: NAS uses automated optimization methods to explore a defined search space of architectures, evaluating candidates on validation performance to discover task-specific optimal designs.

text: What is the grokking phenomenon in neural network training?
options: Networks achieving perfect training accuracy instantly at initialization
Networks generalizing long after achieving perfect training accuracy with a delayed and sudden transition from memorization to generalization
Networks that permanently overfit and never generalize on any task
A technique for speeding up convergence in the first few epochs
explanation: Grokking describes the phenomenon where a network first memorizes training data, then suddenly transitions to generalization far later than expected.

text: What is a recurrent neural network designed to process?
options: Only fixed-size images of uniform dimensions
Sequential data where the order of elements matters
Tabular data with independent rows and columns
Static graph structures without temporal ordering
explanation: RNNs are designed for sequential data like text and time series, maintaining a hidden state that captures information from previous time steps.

text: What does LSTM stand for?
options: Linear Short-Term Mapping
Long Short-Term Memory
Layered Spatial Transformation Model
Latent Sequence Training Method
explanation: LSTM stands for Long Short-Term Memory, a type of RNN designed to learn long-range dependencies in sequential data.

text: What is a generative adversarial network composed of?
options: A single encoder and a single decoder network
A generator network and a discriminator network that compete against each other
Three separate classification networks trained on different data
A clustering algorithm paired with a regression model
explanation: A GAN consists of a generator that creates synthetic data and a discriminator that tries to distinguish real from fake data.

text: What is an autoencoder used for?
options: Supervised classification of labeled images only
Learning compressed representations of data by encoding and reconstructing inputs
Real-time object tracking in video streams exclusively
Encrypting data for secure network transmission
explanation: Autoencoders learn to encode input data into a compressed latent representation and decode it back to reconstruct the original input.

text: What is the Transformer architecture primarily known for?
options: Processing audio signals using convolutional filters
Using self-attention mechanisms to process sequences in parallel without recurrence
Clustering data points in unsupervised learning tasks
Generating random synthetic tabular data for augmentation
explanation: The Transformer uses self-attention mechanisms to weigh the importance of different parts of the input sequence, enabling parallel processing.

text: What does the term attention mechanism refer to in deep learning?
options: A technique for monitoring GPU memory usage during training
A mechanism that allows the model to focus on the most relevant parts of the input when making predictions
A method for distributing training data across multiple machines
A regularization technique that forces the model to attend to all inputs equally
explanation: Attention mechanisms enable the model to dynamically weight different parts of the input, focusing on the most relevant elements.

text: What is fine-tuning in the context of pre-trained models?
options: Training a model from scratch on a new large dataset
Further training a pre-trained model on a new task-specific dataset to adapt it
Reducing the number of parameters in a trained model
Evaluating a model performance on a validation set only
explanation: Fine-tuning continues training a pre-trained model on a smaller task-specific dataset, adapting the learned representations to the new task.

text: What is a variational autoencoder designed to do?
options: Classify images into predefined categories with high accuracy
Learn a probabilistic latent space that allows generating new data samples similar to the training data
Segment images into distinct regions for object detection
Translate text from one language to another automatically
explanation: A VAE learns a probabilistic mapping to a latent space, enabling generation of new data samples by sampling from the learned distribution.

text: What is the purpose of the encoder in a sequence-to-sequence model?
options: To generate the final output sequence one token at a time
To process the input sequence and produce a context representation that summarizes it
To compute the loss function for the training process
To randomly shuffle the input sequence before processing
explanation: The encoder processes the entire input sequence and compresses it into a context representation for the decoder to generate the output.

text: What does GPT stand for in the context of language models?
options: General Processing Technique
Generative Pre-trained Transformer
Gradient Propagation Tool
Graph Pattern Tokenizer
explanation: GPT stands for Generative Pre-trained Transformer, a family of language models that generate text by predicting the next token.

text: How does multi-head attention differ from single-head attention in Transformers?
options: Multi-head attention uses a single large attention computation for efficiency
Multi-head attention runs multiple attention computations in parallel with different learned projections capturing diverse relationship patterns
Multi-head attention requires separate training for each head sequentially
Single-head attention always captures more information than multi-head
explanation: Multi-head attention projects queries, keys, and values into multiple subspaces and performs attention independently in each, capturing different types of relationships.

text: What is the purpose of positional encoding in the Transformer architecture?
options: To encode the vocabulary size of the input tokens
To inject information about the sequential position of tokens since self-attention has no inherent notion of order
To normalize the attention weights to sum to one
To reduce the dimensionality of the input embeddings
explanation: Since self-attention treats all positions equally, positional encodings add position information to the input embeddings.

text: How does teacher forcing work during sequence-to-sequence model training?
options: The model generates tokens autoregressively using its own previous predictions during training
The ground truth previous token is fed as input at each decoder step instead of the model own prediction to stabilize training
The teacher model weights are copied to the student model directly
The loss function is only computed for the first and last tokens in the sequence
explanation: Teacher forcing provides the actual target sequence as decoder input at each step, rather than using the model's own potentially incorrect predictions.

text: What is the mode collapse problem in GAN training?
options: The generator produces overly diverse outputs with no recognizable patterns
The generator learns to produce only a limited variety of outputs ignoring the full diversity of the training distribution
The discriminator becomes too weak to provide useful training signal
The GAN training converges too quickly to a perfect solution
explanation: Mode collapse occurs when the generator finds a few outputs that reliably fool the discriminator and stops exploring the full data distribution.

text: How does BERT differ from GPT in its pre-training approach?
options: BERT uses left-to-right autoregressive training while GPT uses masked language modeling
BERT uses bidirectional masked language modeling seeing context from both directions while GPT uses left-to-right autoregressive language modeling
BERT and GPT use identical pre-training objectives
BERT is pre-trained on images while GPT is pre-trained on text
explanation: BERT pre-trains with masked language modeling using bidirectional context, while GPT uses causal left-to-right next-token prediction.

text: What is the key innovation of the U-Net architecture for image segmentation?
options: It uses only fully connected layers without any convolutions
It uses skip connections between the encoder and decoder at corresponding resolution levels to preserve fine-grained spatial details
It processes images in a single forward pass without any downsampling
It uses only attention mechanisms without convolutions for segmentation
explanation: U-Net's skip connections concatenate encoder feature maps with corresponding decoder layers at each resolution, preserving fine spatial details for precise segmentation.

text: How does curriculum learning improve deep network training?
options: It presents all training examples simultaneously in random order at every epoch
It orders training examples from easy to hard gradually increasing difficulty which can lead to faster convergence and better generalization
It requires the model to learn a fixed curriculum defined by the user manually
It only applies to reinforcement learning and not supervised deep learning
explanation: Curriculum learning presents training examples in a meaningful order from easy to hard, often leading to faster convergence and better final performance.

text: What is the reparameterization trick role in training variational autoencoders?
options: It eliminates the need for a decoder network in the VAE architecture
It allows backpropagation through the stochastic sampling step by expressing it as a deterministic function plus independent noise
It replaces the KL divergence loss with a simpler mean squared error loss
It converts discrete latent variables into continuous ones automatically
explanation: The reparameterization trick rewrites sampling as z = mu + sigma * epsilon where epsilon is fixed noise, making the operation differentiable.

text: What is the purpose of the cross-attention mechanism in encoder-decoder Transformers?
options: To compute self-attention within the encoder layers independently
To allow the decoder to attend to relevant parts of the encoder output when generating each output token
To merge multiple encoder outputs into a single representation
To normalize the encoder hidden states before passing them to the decoder
explanation: Cross-attention uses decoder representations as queries and encoder output as keys and values, allowing the decoder to focus on relevant parts of the input.

text: How does gradient clipping help stabilize training of recurrent neural networks?
options: It eliminates the vanishing gradient problem entirely in all RNN architectures
It caps the gradient magnitude to a maximum threshold preventing exploding gradients from causing unstable weight updates
It clips the weights directly instead of the gradients during training
It only applies during the first few epochs and has no effect afterward
explanation: Gradient clipping limits the gradient norm to a maximum threshold, preventing extremely large gradients from causing destructively large weight updates.

text: How does the straight-through estimator enable training of networks with discrete operations?
options: It provides exact gradients for discrete operations using symbolic differentiation
It uses the identity function as a surrogate gradient during backpropagation while keeping the discrete forward pass allowing approximate gradient flow through non-differentiable operations
It replaces all discrete operations with continuous relaxations permanently
It is only applicable to binary neural networks and not other discrete architectures
explanation: The straight-through estimator passes gradients through discrete operations unchanged during backpropagation while maintaining the discrete forward computation.

text: What is the key insight behind the attention mechanism in Neural Turing Machines?
options: Attention in NTMs is identical to standard Transformer self-attention
NTMs use differentiable soft attention over an external memory matrix allowing the network to learn to read write and address memory locations through gradient descent
NTMs require discrete hard attention that cannot be trained with backpropagation
NTMs replace all attention with convolutional operations on memory
explanation: Neural Turing Machines use differentiable attention mechanisms to address an external memory bank, enabling soft reading and writing through gradient-based training.

text: How does the Perceiver architecture handle arbitrary input modalities?
options: It requires separate architectures for each modality with no shared components
It uses cross-attention to project high-dimensional inputs of any modality into a fixed-size latent array then processes this array with self-attention decoupling computation from input size
It only handles text and image inputs through specialized encoders
It converts all modalities to text before processing them
explanation: The Perceiver uses cross-attention to compress arbitrary-dimensional inputs into a compact latent array, then applies self-attention, making computation independent of input dimensionality.

text: What is the fundamental tradeoff in the ELBO objective for variational autoencoders?
options: There is no tradeoff as both terms always decrease together during training

The reconstruction loss encourages accurate data reproduction while the KL divergence term encourages the latent distribution to match the prior creating a tension between reconstruction quality and latent space regularity
The tradeoff is between training speed and model accuracy only
The ELBO only contains a single term with no competing objectives
explanation: The ELBO balances reconstruction fidelity against latent space regularization; optimizing one at the expense of the other leads to either blurry generations or poor latent structure.

text: How does the Mixture of Experts architecture achieve conditional computation?
options: All experts process every input token and their outputs are averaged equally
A gating network selectively routes each input to a sparse subset of expert networks enabling large total capacity while only activating a fraction of parameters per input
MoE requires all experts to be identical copies of the same network
The mixture is computed after training during inference only
explanation: In MoE, a learned gating network routes each input to a small number of specialized experts, enabling massive parameter scaling while keeping per-input computation constant.

text: What is the theoretical motivation behind normalizing flows for generative modeling?
options: They use adversarial training like GANs to generate realistic samples
They use invertible transformations with tractable Jacobian determinants to map a simple distribution to a complex one enabling exact likelihood computation
They approximate the data distribution using a fixed number of Gaussian components
They require the data distribution to be unimodal for correct operation
explanation: Normalizing flows construct invertible transformations from a simple base distribution to the target, with exact log-likelihood computation via the change of variables formula.

text: Why does the Transformer self-attention have quadratic memory and time complexity and how is it addressed?
options: Self-attention has linear complexity and no efficiency concerns
Computing attention over all pairs of positions requires O(n-squared) operations which is addressed by sparse attention, linear attention, and flash attention
The quadratic cost only applies to the feedforward layers not the attention
Reducing attention complexity always significantly degrades model quality
explanation: Full self-attention computes pairwise scores between all n positions giving O(n-squared) complexity; efficient alternatives include sparse attention, linear attention, and flash attention.

text: How does contrastive predictive coding learn representations from sequential data?
options: It trains a classifier on manually labeled sequential segments
It encodes sequential context and uses a contrastive loss that maximizes mutual information between the context and future observations while minimizing it with negative samples
It requires the sequence to be processed in reverse order only
It computes pairwise distances between all time steps and clusters them
explanation: CPC uses an autoregressive model to encode past context and trains with a contrastive loss that distinguishes true future observations from randomly sampled negatives.

text: What is the score matching objective and how does it relate to diffusion models?
options: Score matching trains a model to predict class labels from noisy inputs
Score matching trains a network to estimate the gradient of the log probability density which diffusion models use to iteratively denoise samples by following the learned score function
Score matching is only applicable to discrete data distributions
Diffusion models do not use score matching and rely on adversarial training instead
explanation: Score matching trains a neural network to estimate the score function of the data distribution; diffusion models learn to denoise by estimating the score at each noise level.

text: How does the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis at Scale differ from its original formulation?
options: There is no difference between the original and scaled versions
At scale the winning tickets found at initialization are replaced by tickets found early in training because the original random initialization property does not hold for very large networks
Large-scale networks always contain better lottery tickets than small networks
The hypothesis has been disproven for all networks larger than ResNet-50
explanation: For larger networks, winning tickets are found after a brief period of initial training rather than at random initialization, as the original approach fails at scale.

text: What is tokenization in NLP?
options: Converting audio speech into text transcriptions
Breaking text into smaller units such as words or subwords
Translating text from one language to another
Summarizing a long document into key points
explanation: Tokenization splits raw text into individual tokens that serve as the basic units for further NLP processing.

text: What is a stop word in text processing?
options: A word that signals the end of a document
A commonly occurring word like the or is that is often removed because it carries little meaning
A word that contains a spelling error
A word that appears only once in the entire corpus
explanation: Stop words are high-frequency words that carry little semantic meaning and are often removed during preprocessing.

text: What is sentiment analysis?
options: Analyzing the grammatical structure of sentences
Determining the emotional tone or opinion expressed in text such as positive or negative
Counting the total number of words in a document
Converting spoken language into written text
explanation: Sentiment analysis classifies the emotional tone of text, typically as positive, negative, or neutral.

text: What does a bag-of-words model represent?
options: The physical arrangement of words on a printed page
Text as a collection of word frequencies ignoring grammar and word order
A neural network trained on vocabulary lists
A visualization of the most important words in a document
explanation: The bag-of-words model represents text as a vector of word counts or frequencies, disregarding grammar and word order.

text: What is named entity recognition?
options: Identifying the language a document is written in
Identifying and classifying proper names, locations, organizations, and other entities in text
Correcting spelling errors in a document
Generating new text based on a given prompt
explanation: NER identifies and categorizes named entities in text into predefined categories such as person names, organizations, and locations.

text: What is stemming in NLP?
options: Adding prefixes to words to create new meanings
Reducing words to their root form by removing suffixes such as running to run
Translating words into their synonyms automatically
Capitalizing the first letter of each word in a sentence
explanation: Stemming reduces words to their base form by stripping suffixes, helping to group related word forms together.

text: What does TF-IDF measure?
options: The total frequency of all words in a single document only
How important a word is to a document relative to a collection of documents
The grammatical correctness of sentences in a text
The reading difficulty level of a document
explanation: TF-IDF measures word importance by combining how often a word appears in a document with how rare it is across the entire corpus.

text: What is text classification?
options: Sorting documents by their file size on disk
Assigning predefined categories or labels to text documents based on their content
Converting structured data into natural language text
Extracting all numerical values from a text document
explanation: Text classification assigns one or more predefined categories to text documents based on their content.

text: What is lemmatization and how does it differ from stemming?
options: Lemmatization and stemming always produce the same results
Lemmatization reduces words to their dictionary base form using vocabulary and morphological analysis while stemming applies simple rule-based suffix removal
Stemming is more accurate than lemmatization in all cases
Lemmatization only works for English language text
explanation: Lemmatization uses morphological analysis and vocabulary lookup to reduce words to their proper dictionary form, while stemming simply chops off suffixes.

text: What is a corpus in NLP?
options: A single sentence used for grammar checking
A large collection of text documents used for training and evaluating NLP models
A type of neural network designed for language tasks
A specific algorithm for text summarization
explanation: A corpus is a large, structured collection of text documents that serves as training or evaluation data for NLP models.

text: How do word embeddings like Word2Vec capture semantic relationships?
options: They store dictionary definitions as text strings for each word
They map words to dense vectors in a continuous space where semantically similar words have similar vector representations
They encode words using one-hot binary vectors with no semantic information
They group words alphabetically without considering meaning
explanation: Word2Vec learns dense vector representations from word co-occurrence patterns, positioning semantically related words close together in the embedding space.

text: What is the key advantage of subword tokenization methods like BPE?
options: They always produce fewer tokens than word-level tokenization
They handle out-of-vocabulary words by breaking them into known subword units balancing vocabulary size and coverage
They require a larger vocabulary than character-level tokenization
They can only be applied to English language text
explanation: BPE decomposes rare or unknown words into common subword units, eliminating the out-of-vocabulary problem while maintaining a manageable vocabulary size.

text: How does the attention mechanism in Transformers improve machine translation?
options: It forces the model to translate word by word in strict sequential order
It allows the decoder to dynamically focus on relevant source words for each target word rather than relying on a fixed-length context vector
It reduces the vocabulary size needed for translation
It eliminates the need for any training data in the target language
explanation: Attention allows the decoder to directly access any part of the encoder output, dynamically weighting source positions based on relevance.

text: What is the difference between extractive and abstractive text summarization?
options: Extractive methods are always more accurate than abstractive methods
Extractive methods select and combine existing sentences from the source while abstractive methods generate new sentences that may not appear in the original text
Abstractive methods can only produce single-sentence summaries
Extractive methods require pre-trained language models while abstractive does not
explanation: Extractive summarization selects important existing sentences, while abstractive summarization generates novel sentences that paraphrase and condense the content.

text: How does the CRF layer improve named entity recognition compared to independent token classification?
options: CRFs make independent predictions for each token without context
CRFs model the dependencies between consecutive label predictions ensuring valid label sequences like preventing I-PER from following B-LOC
CRFs can only be used with character-level features
CRFs always achieve perfect accuracy on NER benchmarks
explanation: A CRF layer models transition probabilities between adjacent labels, enforcing valid sequences rather than making independent predictions.

text: What is perplexity and how is it used to evaluate language models?
options: Perplexity measures the vocabulary size of the language model
Perplexity measures how well a language model predicts a text sample with lower values indicating better prediction ability
Perplexity counts the number of grammatical errors in generated text
Perplexity is only meaningful for character-level language models
explanation: Perplexity represents how surprised the model is by the test text, with lower perplexity indicating better language modeling performance.

text: How does beam search differ from greedy decoding in text generation?
options: Beam search always selects the single highest-probability token at each step
Beam search maintains multiple candidate sequences at each step exploring a wider search space to find more globally optimal output sequences
Greedy decoding explores more possibilities than beam search
Beam search is only applicable to machine translation tasks
explanation: Beam search keeps the top-k most probable partial sequences at each step, often finding higher-probability complete sequences than greedy single-best decoding.

text: What is the purpose of masking in BERT pre-training objective?
options: To hide the model attention weights from users for privacy
To randomly replace tokens with a mask symbol forcing the model to predict them from bidirectional context creating a self-supervised training signal
To reduce the sequence length during training for efficiency
To prevent the model from attending to padding tokens only
explanation: BERT masks 15% of input tokens and trains the model to predict them from surrounding bidirectional context, creating a self-supervised pre-training objective.

text: How does constituency parsing differ from dependency parsing?
options: They always produce identical tree structures for any sentence
Constituency parsing breaks sentences into hierarchical phrase structures while dependency parsing identifies directed relationships between individual words
Constituency parsing only works for English sentences
Dependency parsing requires labeled training data while constituency parsing does not
explanation: Constituency parsing builds a hierarchy of nested phrases, while dependency parsing creates directed grammatical relationships between individual words.

text: What is the role of the special CLS token in BERT?
options: It marks the end of a sentence for tokenization purposes
Its final hidden state serves as an aggregate sequence representation used for classification tasks
It replaces masked tokens during the pre-training phase
It separates different documents in the input batch
explanation: The CLS token is prepended to every input and its final hidden state serves as the aggregate sequence representation for classification tasks.

text: How does the sparse attention pattern in Longformer address the quadratic complexity of standard Transformers for long documents?
options: It simply truncates long documents to a maximum fixed length
It combines local sliding window attention with global attention on select tokens reducing complexity from quadratic to linear in sequence length
It uses standard full attention but on smaller batches
Longformer cannot handle documents longer than standard Transformers
explanation: Longformer uses local windowed attention plus task-specific global attention, achieving O(n) complexity while maintaining the ability to capture long-range dependencies.

text: What is the key innovation of RLHF in aligning language models with human preferences?
options: It uses reinforcement learning to maximize perplexity on human-written text
It trains a reward model on human preference comparisons then uses reinforcement learning to optimize the language model outputs to score highly according to that reward model
It applies RL only during the tokenization phase of text processing
It replaces the Transformer architecture with a recurrent policy network
explanation: RLHF first collects human preference comparisons to train a reward model, then uses policy optimization to fine-tune the language model to generate outputs that score highly.

text: How does the retrieval-augmented generation approach improve factual accuracy of language models?
options: It increases the model parameter count to store more factual knowledge
It augments the language model generation by retrieving relevant documents from an external knowledge base and conditioning the output on both the query and retrieved evidence
It only retrieves cached previous model outputs and reuses them
RAG requires retraining the language model from scratch for each new query
explanation: RAG combines a retrieval component that fetches relevant passages from a knowledge base with a generative model that conditions output on both the query and retrieved evidence.

text: What is the exposure bias problem in autoregressive language models and how does scheduled sampling address it?
options: Exposure bias causes the model to train too slowly on large datasets

The model is trained on ground truth inputs but at inference uses its own possibly erroneous predictions; scheduled sampling gradually transitions from teacher forcing to model predictions during training
Exposure bias only affects non-autoregressive models
Scheduled sampling completely eliminates exposure bias in all cases
explanation: Exposure bias arises because models see ground truth during training but their own predictions at inference; scheduled sampling gradually transitions to model predictions during training.

text: How do prefix tuning and prompt tuning achieve parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models?
options: They retrain all parameters of the language model on the new task
They prepend trainable continuous vectors to the input or hidden states while keeping the pre-trained model frozen learning task adaptation with far fewer parameters
They reduce the vocabulary size to make fine-tuning faster
They only fine-tune the final classification layer and freeze everything else
explanation: Prefix and prompt tuning prepend learnable vectors while keeping the pre-trained model frozen, learning task-specific behavior through a tiny number of additional parameters.

text: What is the key challenge of evaluating open-ended text generation and why are automatic metrics insufficient?
options: Automatic metrics like BLEU perfectly capture all aspects of text quality
Open-ended generation has many valid outputs making reference-based metrics unreliable since they cannot account for the diversity of acceptable responses requiring human evaluation
Automatic metrics are always sufficient for evaluating any NLP task
Only word-level accuracy metrics are needed for generation evaluation
explanation: Open-ended generation admits many valid outputs, but reference-based metrics compare against a small set of references and cannot capture the full space of acceptable responses.

text: How does the mixture of softmaxes approach address the softmax bottleneck in language modeling?
options: It replaces softmax with a simpler argmax operation for faster inference
It uses a weighted combination of multiple softmax distributions to increase the rank of the output probability matrix overcoming the limitation of a single low-rank softmax
It applies softmax independently to each token position without any mixing
The softmax bottleneck has been proven to not exist in practice
explanation: Mixture of softmaxes combines multiple softmax distributions with learned weights, increasing the effective rank and expressiveness of the output distribution.

text: What is the catastrophic forgetting problem when sequentially fine-tuning language models on multiple tasks?
options: Models become faster at learning each subsequent task through accumulated knowledge
The model performance on previously learned tasks degrades significantly as it adapts to new tasks because new weight updates overwrite important earlier representations
Catastrophic forgetting only occurs in convolutional models not Transformers
Sequential fine-tuning always improves performance on all tasks simultaneously
explanation: When fine-tuned sequentially on multiple tasks, neural networks tend to overwrite representations learned for earlier tasks, causing performance degradation on previous tasks.

text: How does the copy mechanism in pointer networks improve handling of rare words in NLP?
options: It copies the entire input sequence to the output without modification
It allows the decoder to directly copy tokens from the input sequence by pointing to them rather than generating from the fixed vocabulary enabling handling of rare words
It creates copies of the model for parallel processing
It only copies punctuation marks from the input to the output
explanation: Pointer networks augment the decoder with a pointing mechanism that can copy tokens directly from the source, enabling reproduction of rare words not well-represented in the output vocabulary.

text: What is the fundamental limitation of position embeddings in Transformers for generalizing to longer sequences than seen during training?
options: Position embeddings work perfectly for any sequence length without limitation

Absolute position embeddings are only learned for positions seen during training so the model cannot properly represent positions beyond the training length without techniques like rotary position embeddings or ALiBi
Position embeddings automatically extrapolate to any length
The limitation only applies to encoder-only models not decoder-only
explanation: Standard absolute position embeddings are learned for a fixed maximum length; relative position methods like RoPE and ALiBi encode position as a function of distance, enabling better length generalization.

text: What is computer vision?
options: A technique for improving monitor display resolution
A field of AI that enables machines to interpret and understand visual information from images and videos
A method for compressing video files for storage
A technology for adjusting screen brightness automatically
explanation: Computer vision is an AI field that enables machines to extract meaningful information from visual inputs like images and videos.

text: What is image classification?
options: Sorting images by file size on a hard drive
Assigning a predefined label or category to an entire image based on its content
Splitting an image into smaller tiles for storage
Converting a color image to grayscale format
explanation: Image classification assigns a category label to an entire image, such as identifying whether a photo contains a cat, dog, or car.

text: What is a pixel in a digital image?
options: A type of image file format like JPEG or PNG
The smallest unit of a digital image representing a single point of color
A filter used to blur images during preprocessing
A measurement of image file size in bytes
explanation: A pixel is the smallest addressable unit of a digital image, representing a single point with specific color and intensity values.

text: What is object detection?
options: Identifying the resolution of an image in megapixels
Locating and classifying multiple objects within an image by drawing bounding boxes around them
Compressing an image to reduce its file size
Adjusting the contrast and brightness of photographs
explanation: Object detection identifies and localizes multiple objects in an image, providing both class labels and bounding box coordinates.

text: What type of neural network is most commonly used for image tasks?
options: Recurrent neural network
Convolutional neural network
Generative adversarial network
Long short-term memory network
explanation: CNNs use learnable filters to automatically extract spatial features from images, making them the standard architecture for image tasks.

text: What does image segmentation do?
options: Increases the resolution of a low-quality image
Divides an image into regions or assigns a class label to each pixel
Removes the background color from an image
Converts a still image into a short video clip
explanation: Image segmentation classifies each pixel in an image into a category, partitioning the image into meaningful regions.

text: What is a convolutional filter in a CNN?
options: A tool for removing noise from images after prediction
A small matrix that slides over the image to detect patterns like edges and textures
A method for resizing images to a standard dimension
A technique for converting images from RGB to binary format
explanation: A convolutional filter is a small weight matrix convolved across the image to detect specific local patterns.

text: What is data augmentation in computer vision?
options: Collecting additional images from the internet automatically
Applying random transformations like rotation and flipping to existing images to increase training data diversity
Removing duplicate images from the training dataset
Converting all images to a single standard resolution
explanation: Data augmentation creates variations of existing training images through transformations, increasing training set diversity.

text: What does the term bounding box refer to in object detection?
options: The border around the entire image frame
A rectangle drawn around a detected object indicating its location and size
The maximum resolution supported by the detection model
A box plot showing the distribution of pixel intensities
explanation: A bounding box is a rectangular region that encloses a detected object, specifying its location and approximate extent.

text: What is facial recognition?
options: Detecting any shapes in an image that resemble geometric patterns
Identifying or verifying a person identity from their facial features in an image or video
Converting a face photograph into a cartoon illustration
Measuring the resolution quality of portrait photographs
explanation: Facial recognition uses AI to identify or verify individuals by analyzing and matching facial features.

text: How does transfer learning with ImageNet pre-trained models benefit medical imaging?
options: It provides medical-specific features that require no further adaptation
It provides general visual features like edges and textures that transfer well to medical images reducing the need for large medical training datasets
It eliminates the need for any medical training data completely
It only works for natural photographs and never for medical images
explanation: ImageNet pre-trained models learn general visual features that transfer effectively, significantly reducing the amount of labeled medical data needed.

text: What is the difference between one-stage and two-stage object detectors?
options: One-stage detectors always achieve higher accuracy than two-stage detectors
Two-stage detectors first propose regions then classify them while one-stage detectors predict locations and classes in a single pass trading some accuracy for speed
One-stage detectors only detect a single object per image
Two-stage detectors require two separate GPUs for processing
explanation: Two-stage detectors generate region proposals then classify each region, while one-stage detectors predict locations and classes directly in a single pass.

text: How does non-maximum suppression work in object detection post-processing?
options: It removes all detected objects below a confidence threshold only
It suppresses overlapping detections by keeping the highest confidence box and removing boxes with high IoU overlap to eliminate duplicate detections
It increases the resolution of detected bounding boxes for precision
It normalizes all confidence scores to sum to one across detections
explanation: NMS iteratively selects the highest confidence detection and removes all others that overlap above an IoU threshold, eliminating redundant boxes.

text: What is the intersection over union metric used for in computer vision?
options: Measuring the pixel accuracy of image classification models
Measuring the overlap between a predicted bounding box and a ground truth box to evaluate detection quality
Counting the number of unique objects in an image
Computing the color histogram intersection between two images
explanation: IoU calculates the overlap area divided by the union area between predicted and ground truth boxes, measuring localization accuracy.

text: How do feature pyramid networks improve multi-scale object detection?
options: They only detect objects at a single fixed scale for consistency
They create a pyramid of feature maps at different resolutions with top-down connections enabling strong features at all scales for detecting objects of varying sizes
They increase the input image resolution to detect all sizes uniformly
They replace the backbone network entirely with a simple pyramid filter
explanation: FPNs build a top-down pyramid with lateral connections that merge high-resolution low-level features with semantically rich high-level features.

text: What is the purpose of anchor boxes in region-based object detectors?
options: They fix the detected objects in place to prevent movement between frames
They provide predefined reference boxes of various sizes and aspect ratios at each spatial location that the model refines to predict actual object locations
They anchor the model weights to prevent gradient updates
They determine the color space used for image preprocessing
explanation: Anchor boxes are predefined templates at each spatial position that the detector learns to adjust to match actual object locations and sizes.

text: How does batch normalization affect training of deep CNNs?
options: It removes all batch-to-batch variation by using the entire dataset at once
It normalizes activations within each mini-batch stabilizing training and enabling higher learning rates while also providing mild regularization
It only normalizes the input images and not internal layer activations
It increases the batch size automatically during training
explanation: Batch normalization normalizes activations across the mini-batch at each layer, stabilizing training and allowing higher learning rates.

text: What is the difference between semantic segmentation and panoptic segmentation?
options: They produce identical output masks for any input image
Semantic segmentation labels each pixel by class without distinguishing instances while panoptic segmentation combines both semantic labeling and instance separation
Panoptic segmentation only labels background regions and ignores foreground objects
Semantic segmentation requires more computation than panoptic segmentation
explanation: Semantic segmentation assigns class labels without distinguishing instances, while panoptic combines semantic labeling for stuff classes with instance segmentation for thing classes.

text: How does depthwise separable convolution reduce computational cost compared to standard convolution?
options: It applies the same filter across all channels simultaneously without separation
It factorizes standard convolution into depthwise convolution per channel and pointwise convolution for channel mixing significantly reducing multiply-add operations
It reduces cost by using smaller input images rather than changing the convolution
Depthwise separable convolution always produces lower accuracy than standard convolution
explanation: Depthwise separable convolution splits into a depthwise per-channel convolution and a pointwise 1x1 mixing convolution, significantly reducing computation.

text: What role does the stride parameter play in convolutional layers?
options: It determines the number of output channels in the feature map
It controls how many pixels the filter moves between applications affecting the spatial resolution of the output feature map
It sets the padding size around the input image
It specifies the activation function used after convolution
explanation: Stride determines the step size of the convolutional filter; a stride of 2 halves the spatial dimensions and reduces computation.

text: How does the deformable convolution operation generalize standard convolution?
options: It uses fixed rectangular grids identical to standard convolution
It learns offset displacements for each sampling position allowing the receptive field to adapt its shape to the geometry of objects in the image
It only applies to one-dimensional signals not two-dimensional images
It replaces convolution with fully connected layers at each spatial position
explanation: Deformable convolution adds learnable 2D offsets to each sampling location, enabling the receptive field to adapt its shape to match object geometry.

text: What is the key challenge of video understanding compared to single image analysis?
options: Video frames are always lower resolution than still images
Videos introduce temporal dynamics requiring models to capture both spatial appearance within frames and temporal relationships across frames while managing vastly increased data volume
Video analysis only requires processing the first and last frames
Standard CNNs applied independently to each frame fully solve video understanding
explanation: Video understanding must capture spatial features within frames and temporal dynamics across frames while handling orders-of-magnitude more data.

text: How does the Segment Anything Model achieve zero-shot segmentation across diverse domains?
options: It uses a fixed set of category-specific segmentation heads trained on a closed vocabulary
It combines a prompt-based architecture with massive diverse training data enabling generalization to novel objects and domains through geometric prompts like points and boxes
It only segments objects from the 1000 ImageNet categories
It requires fine-tuning on each new domain before any segmentation
explanation: SAM uses a promptable architecture trained on a massive diverse dataset, learning to segment any object in response to geometric prompts and generalizing to novel domains.

text: What is the difference between monocular and stereo depth estimation and their respective challenges?
options: Monocular estimation is always more accurate because it avoids calibration issues
Monocular depth estimation from a single image is inherently ill-posed relying on learned priors while stereo estimation uses geometric triangulation but requires precise calibration
Stereo estimation cannot handle occluded regions while monocular handles them perfectly
Both approaches produce identical depth maps for all scenes
explanation: Monocular depth is fundamentally ill-posed and requires learned priors, while stereo uses geometric triangulation but struggles with textureless regions and calibration.

text: How does the Vision Transformer challenge the necessity of convolutions for image understanding?
options: It proves that convolutions are always superior to attention for images
It applies self-attention to sequences of image patches achieving competitive or superior performance demonstrating that convolution is not strictly necessary
ViT only works on images smaller than 32x32 pixels
ViT requires convolutional preprocessing before applying attention
explanation: ViT splits images into patches and processes them with Transformer self-attention, achieving state-of-the-art results and challenging the assumption that convolutions are essential for vision.

text: What is the neural radiance field approach to 3D scene representation?
options: It stores 3D scenes as traditional polygon meshes with texture mapping
It represents a 3D scene as a continuous function mapping 3D coordinates and viewing direction to color and density enabling photorealistic novel view synthesis
It only reconstructs flat 2D panoramic images from multiple views
NeRF requires depth sensors and cannot work with regular cameras alone
explanation: NeRF represents a scene as an implicit continuous function mapping 5D input to volume density and color, enabling photorealistic rendering of novel viewpoints.

text: How does contrastive learning in CLIP enable zero-shot image classification?
options: CLIP trains separate models for each possible image category independently
CLIP learns a shared embedding space for images and text through contrastive pre-training enabling classification by comparing image embeddings with text descriptions of categories
CLIP only works for categories explicitly seen during pre-training
CLIP uses supervised classification on ImageNet labels for its zero-shot ability
explanation: CLIP contrastively aligns image and text encoders; at inference, zero-shot classification compares image embeddings with text embeddings of class descriptions.

text: What is the fundamental trade-off in anchor-free versus anchor-based object detection?
options: Anchor-free methods are always strictly inferior to anchor-based approaches
Anchor-free methods avoid the hyperparameter-heavy anchor design but may struggle with precise localization for extreme aspect ratios compared to well-tuned anchor-based methods
Anchor-based methods require no hyperparameter tuning whatsoever
Both approaches have been proven mathematically equivalent
explanation: Anchor-based detectors rely on carefully designed anchor priors adding complexity but providing strong priors, while anchor-free methods simplify the pipeline but may need additional mechanisms for challenging aspect ratios.

text: How does knowledge distillation in object detection differ from classification distillation?
options: Detection distillation is identical to classification distillation in every aspect
Detection distillation must handle localization knowledge and foreground-background imbalance in addition to classification logits requiring specialized distillation losses
Detection distillation only transfers the classification head knowledge
Knowledge distillation cannot be applied to object detection tasks
explanation: Detection distillation faces unique challenges: it must transfer both classification and localization knowledge and handle severe foreground-background class imbalance.

text: What is the challenge of domain adaptation in computer vision when training data and deployment environments differ significantly?
options: Domain adaptation is unnecessary because CNN features always generalize perfectly across domains
Distribution shift between source and target domains causes degraded performance requiring techniques like domain-adversarial training to align feature distributions
Domain adaptation only applies to text data not visual data
Simply collecting more source domain data always solves domain shift problems
explanation: Distribution shift between training and deployment domains causes performance degradation; domain adaptation techniques help bridge this gap by learning domain-invariant representations.

text: What does the term big data generally refer to?
options: Any data stored on a personal laptop computer
Datasets that are too large or complex to process with traditional data management tools
A specific database software product name
Data that has been compressed to a very small size
explanation: Big data refers to datasets characterized by high volume, velocity, and variety that exceed the capacity of traditional tools.

text: What are the three Vs of big data?
options: Visualization, Validation, Verification
Volume, Velocity, Variety
Version, Value, Volatility
Vector, Variable, Variance
explanation: The three Vs describe big data: Volume (large amounts), Velocity (high speed), and Variety (diverse types).

text: What is Hadoop primarily used for?
options: Creating interactive web applications
Distributed storage and processing of large datasets across clusters of computers
Designing graphical user interfaces
Managing version control for software code
explanation: Hadoop is an open-source framework for distributed storage (HDFS) and processing (MapReduce) of large datasets.

text: What is Apache Spark?
options: A database management system for small datasets only
